Chicago Bears vs. Kansas City Chiefs

12:00 p.m., KCTV-5

Predictions (Wins In Parenthesis)

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Well, well. With New Year approaching, it signals the start of the “road to the Super Bowl”. Isn’t it strange that the Chiefs are actually in the mix? For so many yeaars, we, fans have waited and waited and now, we are in the playoffs. Arrogant sportswriters are saying we are the “weakest link”, reviewing our schedule and predicting an early demise in the playoffs. But, the Chiefs have demonstrated repeatedly, that they can win, with just an average performance from the defense. So, we asked the Monday Niters what should the Chiefs wish for in 2004? Lloyd Greenfield wanted the Chiefs to rise to the occasion. Ed West smiled a sneaky smile and wished that the Chiefs would not permit any 100-yard rushers (not even in the playoffs). Carl Clark just wants a secondary, without Eric Warfield in it. Cleaver, Booker, and Allen believe the Chiefs are ready for prime-time and can win, with what they currently have. But, ol’ Ike wants next year’s draft, devoted exclusively to drafting the best defensive players in the country. Ike may not have his way, for with this remarkable record, the Chiefs will be picking last, unless there are trade deals. Anyway, the Chiefs have done and wil continue to excel, especially in early 2004. How far they will go?. . . We will see!. . . Won’t we? Happy New Year.

Bubba Cleaver (13)

Chiefs by 18

Chiefs will BEAR it all

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

Play-Off Race Shaping Up

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

We, Monday Niters, are still recovering from the devastating loss to Oakland, two weeks ago out at Arrowhead stadium. We've replayed it all a thousand times and still have not resolved how a team can be up 17 points, at home, and still lose. We have also pondered who the next kicker will be...hopefully, someone who won't kick barefooted. More important, we hope that all of the positions will be evaluated, not just special teams. The offense, and particularly the quarterback position, needs further review. And the defense, especially the cornerbacks, needs some new faces.

And, we are all avoiding Ike Gardner. That's because he, Paul, and Ed all picked Oakland to win. What's more Ike has been teasing us throughout the year that this team would fold, especially as the season moved toward the play-offs. Sad to say...but Ike was right, as we all witnessed against Seattle and Oakland. So really, we don't have much to say right now about the Chiefs. Thus, we will slowly turn our attention to the play-offs. Dallas is out. Miami hangs on. Tampa Bay's defense makes them a contender. And Minnesota and St. Louis are ready to meet and see who's who in the National Football Conference. Tennessee got a big break. And it is all shaping up to be an interesting arrangement.

Bottom line, we are going to skip this week's predictions and wait until next week when the picture is a little clearer and there are fewer teams in the race to Atlanta (January 30 Super Bowl). It is interesting that two African American coaches could pair up for the National Football Conference championship game next week (Dennis Green of Minnesota and Tony Dungy of Tampa Bay). See you next week with our predictions.

Thanks for going along on our odyssey.

Monday Nite Footballers

By Jim "Granddad" Nunnelly

Carolina Panthers

At

Kansas City Chiefs

12 p.m., WDAF TV 4

"Quote Of The Week I played football in high school for the love of the game. After the pride I had in my school and my team. After that, I tried my hand at semi-professional ball. We played and traveled under the most difficult conditions i.e. no sponsors, no insurance. If someone got hurt, we would pass the hat. The most I ever made per game was $35. Those days are gone. Players today can and do make megabucks. While football is a business, it is still a game. When the love of the game is diminished, winners become losers. Wake up Chiefs and the NFL."

LLOYD GREENFIELD

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

A LETTER TO SANTA. . . Dear Santa, I know this is your busy season and I will try to make it short and to the point. As a long-time Chiefs' fan, I know you know that there are several things they need. And, since you seem to be the only one left to ask, I want to share their needs with you. Forget about my personal list. Just do something for this team of ours. First off, find them a quarterback, one that can run, pass, and lead. And while you are at it, get us a running back, one that can catch and run. And gain at least 1,000 yards in a long 16 game season. Oh yes, we have five running backs but we really just need one good one. Speaking of running back, get us someone who can return kicks for more than 15 yards. I see other teams running kicks for back for 50 and 60 yards and quite frankly, Santa, I am quite envious. Speaking of envy, I really get mad when I see how our once heralded defense has fallen. Once, we had Derrick and Neil and a whole lot of respect. Now, teams laugh at us and know that the defense really sucks. It's just a shame the way we screwed up in front of the national television audience last Monday. I will never understand why we threw a last second pass for five yards and we need to throw for a touchdown. Perhaps, the coaches saw something 40 million people didn't. Speaking of coaches, try to give them what they are asking for 100. For the Lord knows, they need some help. While I am not sure what Lamar Hunt and Carl Peterson are going to do about their future here in Kansas City, just whatever you think will be okay. Finally, Santa, you have been so good to us, but it's been a long time since we got any good gifts for our football team. People are calling them "the Cheaps" and I don't like it. If you took care of Oakland, I'm sure you can take care of us. Thanks Santa. I'll be home all during the holiday season, for again, the Chiefs won't be going anywhere.

Sincerely, One Championship Starved Fan

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

San Diego Chargers vs. Kansas City Chiefs

3:15 p.m. KCTV-5

Conversation, Commentary and Chatter

Reminiscing, that's what the Monday Niters did over to Ed West's house, this past Monday night. To help everyone "look back", there was an author present, who was writing a book on "Monday Night Football". Overall, the reminiscing was actually fun, for it had been a long time since the guys had just sit down and looked back. Paul Booker began to nod. Of all the Monday night televised games, the Kansas City-Buffalo game, here in 1981, was considered "the best". That October night, the Chiefs actually beat the Marv Levy-coached, Super Bowl-bound Buffalo Bills, 33-6. Then someone remembered the announcer trio, consisting of Howard Cosell, Dandy Don Meredith and Frank Gifford. Cosell was praised for his supportive relationship with Muhummad Ali. Booker is fully asleep by now. Conversation switched to how African Americans in the corporate workplace, must be extra cautious, especially, when they express their dissatisfaction with something. Their remarks are usually over-interpreted to mean that violence is likely to follow. Then conversation switched again. When Charles Allen and Ed West host Monday Nite Football, they actually do their own cooking. Braggarts (the rest of us need all the help we can get). Then, Greenfield, a realtor, was praised for guiding our group to become NAACP Life members. By now, Booker is snoring quite loudly, sprawled out in Ed West's gadget-filled message chair. And as usual, the argu-ment over how much CALL papers cost in 1940. Bubba Cleaver kindly recounts how and why he became a member of this garrulous group. Ollie Hubbard thinks he is not wicked, as he slips away. Then the conversation again goes awry, in several different directions. Reminiscing, while it is good for the soul, can get out of hand. Then, soft-spoken Carl Clark thanks the group for its participation especially in the Riwers/Harvester's partnership. Too much to keep up with. Especially when there are ten men, all talking at the same time, all on different subjects. That Paul Booker has slept through the entire football game. How could he with all this noise? And the author, who came to get information for his upcoming book, put up his note pad. And, he just gave up and joined in the reminiscing. So much for the book.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Ollie Hubbard is one of the more colorful Monday Niters. Having had a barber for a father and graduating from the old R.T. Coles school has allowed Ollie to rack up thousands of acquaintances and friends around Kansas City.

He reads the obituary religiously and attends as many funerals as he can. And his telephone rings night and day, every day. “Bg Hub” flat out knows everybody. That knowledge distinguishes him as a top Lincoln university, alumnus. Ollie used to attend Homecoming festivities every year, for about 30 consecutive years, never missing and always first to arrive in Jefferson City.

As for his involvement in the Monday Nite Footballers, Ollie is most noted for his humerous, colorful comments, when predicting the Chiefs’ football games. Freely borrowing from the “blues”, Ollie warns the Chiefs not to “make their move too soon.” He reminds the Chiefs of that day against the Green Bay Packers in the first Super Bowl.

Even though Otis Taylor caught an over-the-shoulder pass for a touchdown, the Chiefs still lost. Today, he cautiously says that the Chiefs are off to a good start. And that there are still 13 games remaining. Again, the Chiefs should not “make their move too soon.”

Sports Editor Note: Either the waters have parted over the Missouri river or “the Priest” has walked on water. Whatever the case, a miracle has taken place. One that you haven’t seen in centuries. Our cantankerous friend, “Ike”, has picked the Chiefs for the third consecutive week. Had he picked the Chiefs in the season opener, ol’ Ike would be tied with the rest of the Chiefs’ faithful Monday Niters. Of course, ol’ Ike either doesn’t believe in miracles or he simply has a problem with our boys at One Arrowhead Drive. Well, miracles do happen and you’re looking at one right now. Way to go Ike. Don’t lose your personna.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Baltimore Ravens 8:00 p.m., KMBC TV 9

Predictions (Wins In Parenthesis)

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Ike Gardner and Paul Booker are just plain haters. This anti-Chiefs duo has expanded and now includes the likes of Carl Clark, Ollie Hubbard, and the highly-respected Bubba Cleaver. I call these rebels, "Ike and the Haters". While it is true that the Chiefs are 0-3, these haters continue to believe that the Chiefs are just doomed. But the truth is they said the same thing last year, when the Chiefs went 9-0 and eventually onto the playoffs.

But, Ike is well-known for his rugged individualisms. In fact, Lura Anderson hosted a surprise birthday party for Ike. He turned about 85 last Sunday. Every-body here commented on Ike's widely-known cantankerousness. The 40-odd guests traded stories about Ike, ranging from putting "do not disturb" signs on his hospital door to driving from St. Louis in a hard-driving snow, scaring Charles and Carl to death. Some referred to Ike as a "gentle giant". While I respect the person who said this hughely, we have yet to see either gentle or giant in Ike.

Bottom line, Ike hates everything. And, this haterness is spreading to others. Pretty soon, everybody will be hating our Chiefs and there will be no one to be found, when they make it to the playoffs (only three teams out of 168 have ever started 0-3 and made it to the playoffs).

Monday Nite Footballers

Predicting Since 1970

Kansas City Chiefs At San Diego Chargers 3:15 p.m., KCMO TV 5

Quote Of The Week “In spite of the losses, the Chiefs really are a pretty good foot-ball team, especially the players themselves. However, the coaching sucks. Gunther is more like a cheerleader than an NFL coach. Face it y'all, he should be replaced.”

(Image of a man) CARL CLARK

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

SUPERFICIAL SUCCESS FOR CHIEFS . . . Okay. Okay. We're under the Gun(ther) and we are not going to make the playoffs. But, the Chiefs did beat the Super Bowl Champions, the St. Louis Rams. And if you stretch it just a little, the Chiefs did bury "Marty Ball" and assembled a surprising passing offense. But, all that is so superficial. . . doesn't mean a thing. Our sure-footed defense became lame and mistake-filled. Even though the running-back-by-committee system was supposed to be dead, it was still in force last Sunday when they lost to Buffalo. And, while Grbac has amassed large passing statistics, he still will throw an interception at the most inappropriate time. And to make matters worse, he never became the leader the national press tout-ed him to be. And, Jimmie Raye, former quarterback and now offensive coordinator . . . was at best mediocre with his play-calling. All of this means that the Chiefs will be lucky to finish the season at 8-8 and won't be playing anywhere in January. Of course, all of us have our reasons to explain the "up and down" vicissitudinary season for our Chiefs. But, like all hard times, we get use to it, or we stop talking about it, or we redi-rect and re-channel our energies and interests. Anybody for chess?

POTPOURRI AND OTHER STUFF . . .

Marylyn and Carl Clark, attended the renown Michigan-Ohio State football game in Columbus, Ohio, this past week-end. Shawn Edwards, host of the Hot 103's "Generation Rap" and Fox 4's film critic, was the hon-oree at a birthday party, given by G-Rap crew members. According to a Kauffman Foundation study, drug use among youth in Jackson County is generally lower than the metropolitan area as a whole. COMBAT sponsors 38 drug prevention programs.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Detroit Lions vs. Kansas City Chiefs

12 p.m., WDAF-TV 4

Conversation, Commentary and Chatter

Okay, Chiefs' fans everywhere seem to be recovering nicely from last week's reality check (the embarrassing beating from the Denver Broncos). Now, come on, everybody in the country knew that the Chiefs would not go 16-0 (except maybe Charles Allen). And we are not that far removed from the days when the Chiefs would just blow everything. And, of course, we knew the offense would fail one day, and openly demonstrate just how weak our run defense really is. And it all happened, out in Denver, last Sunday. The Broncos beat the Chiefs so badly in the second half, when the Chiefs' highly-touted offense just plain quit. The Chiefs performed just miserably, as they have been known to do. The mistakes they made were unexplainably dumb. Then, too, Monday Niters expected that Denver, Minnesota, and Cincinnati would be formidable opponents because of an otherwise easy schedule. So once again, the early season. They have to crank up the offense, so as to conceal the often-exposed, vulnerably sub-par defense. That means Priest will have to get more touches; and the sometimey receivers will have to catch more and run further after the catch. It's funky but it's fair. It's like that and that's the way it is. Just like Bubba, Ike, and Ed said it was. By the way, the Chiefs will clinch the AFC West by beating up on the roar-less Detroit Lions. What a difference a week can make.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Buffalo Bills 7:30 p.m. ESPN

Game Prediction (Wins In Parenthesis)

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Each year, we change something about our organization...not just to attract new readers but to broaden our horizons. Last year, we added the Reverend Emanuel Cleaver to our "brainiac-maniac" football group. Listed under the moniker, Bubba, our readers immediately began to question where did this "Bubba" come from. Now, affectionately known as "Rev", and "Mayor", Cleaver's first nickname was given to him, by his oldest of three sisters, trying to say "Brother". It stuck and anybody from his home state, Texas, would certainly know who Bubba is. Besides, having the most recognized name in all of Kansas City, Cleaver is a genuine Chiefs' football fan. Like his fellow Monday Niters, Cleaver actually was an All-Star linebacker and defensive back, in the '60s, way back, in high school. He was destined for a promising college career before injuring his knee. In fact, Cleaver could write a medical journal because he has been operated on 11 times mostly for continuing knee problems. When Cleaver joined up with the Monday Niters, there was quite a bit of talk, about his presence...being a former mayor and a minister and socially interacting with three of his parishioners. When asked, Cleaver crisply states that he became a part of Monday Nite Football, just to have fun and be around a bunch of guys who like football and each other. Further, it gives him a chance to be just "one of the guys". Now, even though he acts down-to-earth and all, none of us would ever dare to refer to him as "just one of the guys". We, Monday Niters, regard Cleaver with the highest of respect. We really respect his ability to prognosticate, for he is the current leader in correct predictions. So, we just rate his football savvy, right up there with his ability to preach, his political prowess, and his God-given talent to lead wherever he goes. We all envy his unique ability to remember any conversation, even though it might have taken place months or years ago. And we are all proud to have him, as a friend to football and to us, as well.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Charles Allen is, first and foremost, a well-respected dentist and is held in high esteem by both colleagues and clients, alike. Along with a loyal and capable staff, Dr. Allen has built one of the largest and best dental practices in the metropolitan area.

Even though successful, he still works hard at reducing the time it takes to get an appointment with him. And he is quick to point out that he likes to see every patient, whether it is preventative teeth-cleaning or a major denture repair.

But, that’s Charles. Loyal, trusting, professional, unrelenting. That’s exactly the way Charles is about his Chiefs. Unlike Ike, he always supports the Chiefs. In his 34 years as a Monday Niter, he has never once predicted a Chiefs loss, not even in the lean days of the recent past. One of his special qualities is his fondness for Chiefs trivia. He smiles when he remembers the Sunday afternoon when Derrick Thomas, the late Chiefs’ All-Pro linebacker, sacked Seattle’s David Krieg seven times, out at Arrowhead. In fact, he tells his 8-year-old grandson, Robert that Von Holliday and the newly-oiled Chiefs’ defense will lead Kansas City to the Super Bowl. . . If not this year, then certainly in 2004. While some of his fellow Monday Niters will disagree with Charles’ steadfastness, that doesn’t stop Charles from being loyal, trusting, professional, and unrelenting. Just like he is with everything else.

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Tennessee Titans

12:00 p.m.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

An African Proverb on Hope

These days, it is quite popular to refer to our African heritage via quotes and quotations. And after the Chiefs sixth loss in a row and a now-lost season, I can try and find an appropriate African proverb to elucidate another Chiefs' disastrous season. Get ready for the players to start saying "we just gotta correct these mistakes" or they will say "we all are family and that includes everybody". And the coaches will start admitting that their experiments didn't work.

Yes, if it was Lamar Hunt's dream to win a Super Bowl again, he must be rotating in his grave. For the current Chiefs are nowhere near Super Bowl calibre. All we can do is hope for a better tomorrow. Which reminds me of a simple old African proverb... "What one hopes for is always better than what one has". Now, ain't that the truth?

Otis Taylor Chiefs by 6 Chiefs will play top-notch football inside the 20

Sports Editor’s Note: Well, we knew it wouldn’t last and it didn’t. Ol’ Ike broke his record three straight weeks of picking the Chiefs. Like I said last week, Ike doesn’t believe in miracles. But that’s okay. We all knew that he was a faithless fan. Some day Ike you’ll learn. Just like the faithful Monday Niters already have.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

This week, we will dedicate our words to the recently departed fellow Monday Niter, Lewis Walter Livingston. Paul Booker, Ike Gardner, and Ollie Hubbard, all knew “Uncle Walt” from his days as an educator and a co-worker at the Niles Home For Children. It was that association that led Walt to become an active part in the Monday Night Footballers.

Though a quiet man, he held his own, among those louder Monday Niters. Walt had a way of correcting misinformation, especially about all levels of football. It was if he waited for someone to make a mistake. And whammo, he would pounce on them, lightly but forcefully. In his later years, he fell victim to a series of illnesses. However, he kept in contact with Monday Niters, by even entertaining them at his assisted-living quarters, just south of Kansas City. While he wasn’t an extrovert, those of us who got to know him, knew that we had a true friend and someone who could set us straight . . and still remain friends.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

San Diego Chargers At Kansas City Chiefs KCMO TV 5, 101.1 FM 3:05 p.m.

Quote Of The Week “Chiefs are a playoff team except for four flaws: Cunningham, Grbac, Schottenheimer, and Jimmy Raye.” Carl Clark Retired Journeyman

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary Chiefs Sunday date with the Chargers – Ollie Hubbard, known for his quips of wisdom, says the Chargers will create an "electric slide" over the Chiefs, meaning that the Chiefs will experience their third defeat. Ol' Ike Gardner, usually a maverick against the mien, says just the opposite. Surprisingly, he believes the Chiefs will prevail with God's mercy. However so, it is still premature to talk about resignations, playoffs, and losing seasons – just yet. There hasn't been enough time to see who will step up and help the Chiefs become a winning team. But it is clear the Chiefs definitely miss Derrick Thomas. In the meantime, we wish Cunningham would stop saying Grbac is a great team asset – that alone could cost him his job.

Kudos For Northeast Middle Scholars – As a result of homework bootcamp and some tough summer school hours, 15 Northeast Middle seventh-graders are expected to maintain their good grades through their second year of middle school. These students were a part of an intensive effort by COMBAT, ARC, the Kansas City School district, SMART PARTNERS and the Northeast community. Called NIA, the program emphasizes staying in school, getting good grades, and becoming good school citizens. Starting in October, the program will admit sixth graders. Threescore and Seven For Earl Abel – The Rev. Earl Abel was honored by his congregation for his 70th birthday anniversary. As he was presented with a portrait, he displayed a humility-filled smiled of appreciation. Rev. Abel, Palestine's only pastor for 41 years, praised his congregation for their loyalty and faith. Rev. Abel has been steadfast in his commitment to serve the Lord, developing services for senior citizens and youth.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Kansas City Chiefs

At

Oakland Raiders

3:15 p.m., KCMO TV 5

Quote Of The Week "The Chiefs demonstrated they have championship caliber talent when they defeated St. Louis. However, they lack four key ingredients necessary to achieve a champion-ship: consistent, intelligent coaching; consistent, special teams with a quality running game; and a championship-caliber quarterback. Grbac's excellent statistics are directly attributable to three factors: Alex-ander, Gonzales and Morris. Plus, he has completed almost as many passes to the defense as theh offense. His ineptness late in the game continues to plague the Chiefs."

[Image of Ed West] Ed West

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

NOTHING MATTERS SUNDAY . . . Sunday's game will mark the NFL, halfway point, especially for our Kansas City Chiefs and their arch rivals, the Oakland Raiders. The fact that Rich Gannon is on the front cover of Sports Illustrated does not matter. Neither does the fact that the Raiders are 7-1, their best start in years. Conversely, the fact that Grbac has risen from a goat to the third-rated AFC passer is of no value. And it certainly won't matter that big-play, receiver Alexander is second in touchdowns and fourth in receiving yards. Absolutely nothing matters really – except blind fate. No one can really say who is going to be "on" on Sunday. Conversely, no one can safely say which team will be "off." As usual, Ike, Paul and Ollie say the Raiders will win it by a touchdown or better. On the contrary, the rest of us clearly believe in the Chiefs again. Thus, the outcome is or will be totally unpredictable. Of course, there will be resounding cheers and deafening boos. As can be expected, the team that scores first won't win. And the game won't be decided until the last moments. Penalties will likely plague both sides. And most of all, some instant replay will probably determine the outcome/direction of the game. Thus, lay aside your loyalties and knowledge about professional football, because it won't matter either. Just join us in watching every-body's Game of the Week. And it all will be happening Sunday afternoon between the well-balanced Oakland Raiders and our, uprising Kansas City Chiefs. (I said all that to say, the Chiefs will be 6-3 on Sunday night.)

POTPOURRI AND OTHER STUFF ... Ask Rev. Emanuel Cleaver what practical joke does he have planned for Ike Gardner. Ike predicted the Chiefs would lose against St. Louis (wrong); There is word that Cicely Tyson may be coming to town this weekend; Alvin Ailey is not only a well-known, dance troupe, it also offers drug prevention services through COMBAT.

Predictions (Wins In Parenthesis)

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Kansas City Chiefs vs. San Francisco 49ers, 12:00 p.m. KCTV 5

Commentary

Chuck's Corner

Does a 14-5 week spell disaster? Naw, just warming up for the perfect week! Of course it won't be this week. Tough picks for week four.

Green Bay at Philadelphia. No re-run for the Pack here. Eagles will soar. Prediction: Philadelphia

Detroit at St Louis. Rams airways will be busy. Lions remain winless. Prediction: St. Louis

Indianapolis at N.Y. Jets. Sorry Jets, this isn't Buffalo. Too many weapons to defend. Prediction Indianapolis

Miami at Houston. Texans could spring a surprise. COULD is the operative word! Pre- diction: Miami

Dallas at Tennessee. Stop me if you've heard this before. Tennessee is playing for a high draft pick. Prediction: Miami

Cleveland at Oakland. The one game I figured Oakland would win. NOT!! Prediction: Cleveland

New England at Cincinnati. Bengals refuse to take any prisoners. Prediction: Cincinnati

Seattle at Chicago. No Alexander, no win, no way!! Defenses will dictate outcome. Prediction: Chicago

New Orleans at Carolina. The party’s over. Carolina has regrouped. Saints won't come marching in! Prediction: Carolina

Minnesota at Buffalo. Tough one to figure. Is Minnesota for real? I think so. Prediction: Minnesota

San Diego at Baltimore. Ravens finally face a winning team. They fall from the unbeaten ranks. Prediction: San Diego

San Francisco at Kansas City. Finally a KC win! Perhaps! Only if their defense carries them. Prediction: Kansas City

Bye week: Pittsburgh, Denver, Giants, Tampa

Last week record: 9-5

Season record: 28-18

Chuck James

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Cincinnati Bengals vs. Kansas City Chiefs

12 p.m., KCTV-5

Predictions (Wins in Parenthesis)

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

We’ve spent the last nine weeks, highlighting each of the Monday Niters. Now, it is time to really talk about our Kansas City Chiefs. I can now see why Hank Stram was inducted into the Hall of Fame. That was the last time, up until now, that our Chiefs were held in high esteem. You would see the late Buck Buchanan or Willie Lanier or Otis Taylor on the streets and look up to them, talk about the upcoming game and actually feel that they represented our community and the team well. That was over thirty years ago. Many of the current Chiefs’ players were not even born, yet. Over those three decades, we have really forgotten what it is like to have a team...that just might win. In fact, long time fans, like Barbara Hill, don’t know how to react, when their team is actually leading the NFL in scoring, hosting the top touchdown produc-er, and is undefeated after ten games into the season. We all are tempted to pinch our-selves and say ‘it isn’t so’. And to top all that, Trent Green actually is doing his part, by not giving the ball to the opposing team. That, alone, makes it scary to even say the “SB” words. We can talk about the Super Bowl of 1970. You know the Chiefs did play Minnesota and the score was 24-7. Otis Taylor was the star and the American Football Conference prevailed over the National Football League. Hey! Stop that! That was a long, long time, ago. Brady To See Much Different Colts Defense

INDIANAPOLIS (AP) – The Indianapolis Colts' defense was bashed by opponents and critics for most of the regular season. It suddenly figured out how to counterpunch in the playoffs. By keeping opposing offenses out of the end zone and off the field, the once-maligned defense has taken Indianapolis right into Sunday's AFC championship game against New England. "We're playing better," Coach Tony Dungy said. "We're tackling a little bit better. We've always had a pretty good rush in the long-yardage situations, but in the third-and-5, third-and-6, we've tackled the underneath throws a little bit better." The dramatic turnaround has surprised every- one, except perhaps the Colts. "I think we're playing consistently well for 60 minutes, and we're playing with an intensity that's been unmatched," former Pro Bowl linebacker Cato June said. "We're having fun doing it, and showing that all the stuff in the regular season doesn't matter." Whatever the explanation, the improved defense has been able to get off the field quickly and played well enough to overcome two subpar games from two-time MVP Peyton Manning. "It's not the 3-4, it's not the people, it's not the scheme," Dungy said. "It's doing your job on every play, and I thought that we did. The one thing that we've done well in this postseason is that we've played well on third down."

Split Personality: Grossman Shows Flashes Of Bart Starr

CHICAGO (AP) – After almost 500 throws dur- ing the regular season, half that many in practice each and every week, and three dozen more against the Seattle Seahawks. Bears quarterback Rex Grossman finally let one fly that kept a city from tossing him into the lake. This week, anyway. His teammates, taking their cue from stubborn Bears coach Lovie Smith, have bailed Grossman out a few times and supported him after every one. Grossman must do it again when the Saints come to town -- with a much stronger offense and a healthy secondary to boot. Grossman knows the pickings won't be that easy against New Orleans. "We're in the NFC championship, and we win one game, we're in the Super Bowl," Grossman said. "Two wins away," he said, "from having a ring on my finger for the rest of my life."

TET-A-TETE – Strange word. And we’ll show how it fits around the current events for our Kansas City Chiefs. Now, that most of you have turned you attention to Christmas Eve and some even toward Kwanzaa, you must not forget that the Chiefs have one more game to play. Some gave up on the Chiefs after they lost unexplainably to San Diego. Some don’t even know we’re playing in the Georgia Dome, down in Atlanta on Christmas Eve Sunday. And that is precisely where TETE-A-TETE comes in. This word/phrase means a private, head-to-head conversation between two people. Nobody else and no interference. And that conversation has to happen between Carl Peterson, president and general manager, and Gunther Cunningham, head coach of the Chiefs. In this private conversation, the two will decide next year’s goals or next year’s coach. If Gunther wins, the talk will be how to improve on an 8-8 season. If the Chiefs lose, the conversation will take on a more chilled atmosphere. Showing Gunther the exits from Arrowhead Stadium. So, the game at Atlanta is more than a Christmas Eve football game. It will decide who wants to be the 2001 head coach. We believe Gunther will be back and the Chiefs will have stayed off another losing season. Merry Christmas, Gunther and to all of you.

Ed West left the little country town of Osawatomie, Kas., in 1961, to teach here at Lincoln High school. Although somewhat quiet and shy, he had been an All-Star running back at Pittsburg State and had even been invited to try out for an NFL team. After teaching for a few years, Ed accepted a management position at the now-defunct Western Electric Plant in 1966. Trained in Navy electronics, he was a perfect fit for all the intricacies of this rapidly budding industry. Even today, those unique skills have made him very popular, especially among the youngsters. Now in semi-retirement and having returned to teaching as a substitute, he is frequently called on to either build a computer or help someone unravel the complexities of owning or using a personal computer. His actual "hands-on" football experiences, coupled with his remarkable computer wizardry, gives Ed an exalted, special place among his fellow Monday Niters. When Ed West speaks, everybody listens. He is a walking embodiment of what he does and feels and his peers both respect and admire him. In addition, his posh, suburban home is immersed in and full of DVD's, High Definition tv's, and a host of other computer-generated gadgets. When asked how many games the Chiefs would win, Ed quickly shouts out "at least 12." In fact, he thinks the Chiefs may even win more, even though it is too early to talk Super Bowl talk. This guy may be from the country, but he sure is smart when it comes to football and computers and the Chiefs.

SUPER BOWL POTPOURRI

As one can discern, the Monday Niters are divided about what team will win on Sunday. Enough said about that. Ike and Booker are the season’s best predictors. Enough said about that, too. But rather than write an organized, thoughtful column, let’s just look at some Super Bowl trivia and potpourri (just miscellaneous stuff). This will be particularly helpful to those of you who are casual football fans and look to our column for helpful hints.

Here goes:

•Janet Jackson has been replaced by Paul McCartney as the halftime keynote.

•It's been 24 years, since the Eagles landed at the Super Bowl. Dick Vermeil was the coach, then.

•Jacksonville is the smallest and newest city to host a Super Bowl. The site is Alltell Stadium.

•New England will be in the Super Bowl for the third time in four years. Tom Brady, Patriot quarterback, was named MVP in the two games won by the Patriots.

•Most of the chatter has been about whether Terrell Owens will play. Chances are he will suit up and star. The Pro Bowler caught 14 touchdown passes in 14 games.

•Tony Gonzalez, Chiefs' All-Pro tight end, says New England will win. Also, Renee Gardner, Ike's daughter, believes in the Patriots, as well.

•It was Super Bowl 22, in 1988 when Doug Williams, first black quarterback to start a Super Bowl, just showed out in Washington’s victory over John Elway and the Denver Broncos. The score was 42-10, with Doug throwing for 4 touchdowns.

•New England safety, Rodney Harrison is considered the “dirtiest” player in the NFL. He has been fined over $350,000 for his vicious hits on receivers.

•Lloyd and Geneva Greenfield will host the Monday Niters and their guests at their Birchwood subdivision home for the annual Super Bowl party. Lloyd is a real estate advisor and Geneva is a renown storyteller.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Ike Gardner Guest Columnist

In My Defense

All during the 1995 Chiefs football season I have been chided, verbally harassed, and accused of being a traitor by church members, family, friends and former co-workers because I had the vision to see the Chiefs as they really are.

At best, the Chiefs are a mediocre team who lucked up on a 13-3 record, which clearly belies their true performance.

When the facts are scrutinized objectively the following is disclosed: 1. Though 13-3, the Chiefs played only four teams that have a .500 or better record; 2. The Chiefs have struggled to get by some of the weakest teams in the League e.g., the New York Giants and the Houston Oilers; 3. In terms of offense, the Chiefs ranked 18th (out of 30) in the NFL; 4. Some of the Chiefs' victories were not only against scrubby teams, but against teams whose key players were out because of injury e.g., San Diego, Denver and Oakland; and 5. Bono does a good job only of being a pal to Joe Montana ( for whatever that's worth).

For all of you who were disappointed, last Sunday's defeat was really a blessing. At least the score was close.

If Pittsburgh would have come into town this Sunday, Nick Lowery and Jan Stenerud in their prime would not have made a difference. The Chiefs rode the shoulders of the Almighty all season long, but like Moses, they did not make it to the promised land (nor did they deserve to).

What goes wrong with the Chiefs? Each year they start out winning, start to slip in mid-season and go on to sink to new lows? Is it our garage sale quarterback? Is it coaching? Is the defense overloaded and expected to carry more than its share? Is the pass coverage both suspect and sub-optimal? Are the unpredictable Chiefs enter their 10th game, the answer to all of these questions are not that simple. Truthfully, the Kansas City Chiefs are still a work in progress...rebuilding the prundite might call it. At any rate, the Chiefs are not an average team that has many faults and they show up. Just like in the last two weeks against Tampa Bay and the Indianapolis Colts. Even Stevie Wonder can see the obvious, i.e., the kickoff returners fumble, the secondary (Dishman and Warfield) get beat a lot, and Elvis will throw the ball to the opponent as if they were Chiefs players. Put this together with a spectacular defense and you have what the Chiefs are a very average 5-4 team who are hell-bent on courtring catastrophe in the fourth quarter.

Nonetheless, there is a good side to all of this. Somehow the Chiefs manage to emerge and erase some of these blatant shortcomings before the hometown crowd. It is as if they take a magic pill and just do it in friendly Arrowhead Stadium. Why is this so? We don’t know and can’t even imagine a decent explanation. But we had better try because Sunday, the first place, well-coached Seattle Seahawks come to town Sunday. No doubt about it, if we survive and beat the Seahawks, it will be the equivalent of making the mid-season playoffs. To achieve this miracle, the locals have to do three things...One, Derrick Thomas must sack and tackle like never before. Two, our ever-changing running backs must tank for a long run and Grbac and Alexander or Horn must connect for two big plays. On top of that, McGlockton, Hicks, Edwards, Hasty and Woods must play at superlative levels, getting to the quarterback right from the start.

If the Chiefs do these things and the play-calling is adequate, then the 78,000 Arrowhead fans can help. In short, Kansas City can no longer live by even normal means. Usual Chiefs' supporters like Ed West and Carl Clark have changed their minds and believe Seattle will win handily. They join ol' Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, and Paul Booker who collectively think the Chiefs will lose their first home game Sunday and leave the Chiefs at 5-5. On the other hand, Dr. Charles Allen and Lloyd Greenfield know that the Chiefs will find a way to win at home, as unexplainable as it might seem. Look for the Chiefs to win their sixth game and resurrect thoughts of having a decent, play-off likely season. I’m with Charles and Lloyd.

TOO MANY CHIEFS AND NOT ENOUGH YARDS -- If William Shakespeare were alive today, he would say that old adage would summarize the Chiefs and their ineffective, five-man running back system. On first hearing, one may think that if we keep fresh backs in the game, it will wear down the defense and one of the quintet will break one or two for one or two touchdowns. The ridiculousness of this system was in full force last week against Oakland, when these fresh backs only gained a mere 60 yards on the ground. Richardson stumbled while Anders ambled while Moreau moped while Cloud continued to further cloudy the situation. The time has come for the Chiefs to decide that one running back is going to carry the ball and that's it. Coach Cunningham announced Wednesday that Kimble Anders will be the Chiefs starting running back on Sunday. That decision will surely result in more yardage, more scoring, and even better passing game. No other NFL team employs such a medieval, terribly outdated system, leaving opponents completely unmoved by this pigskin, group therapy. If the Chiefs decide to change, they can anticipate a win on Sunday. If they continue with this proven nonsense, they will slip into another year without playoff participation and their fourth loss. St. Louis is highly favored but a Chiefs' victory is not out of the question.

There's center Tim Grunhard and guard Will Shields who together open large, gaping holes and pass blocks with accuracy, skill, and without holding penalties.

Predicting Since 1970

Hank Stram, Enlarging Our Territory

Hank Stram was the first head coach of the Kansas City Chiefs.

Only those of us over 50 will remember the days when Kansas City was part of the old American Football League. This was the early sixties, when the predominant National Football League touted franchises. . . like the Chicago Bears, San Francisco Forty-Niners, and the Washington Redskins. And Hank Stram and others had the task of competing with and enlarging the territory of the fragile but willing League. And enlarge they did. And Hank Stram, under the guidance of Lamar Hunt, brought Kansas City up from nowhere into the big leagues.

Before merging, the AFL actually had to outbid and outwit the more formidable NFL. More important, they also had to outplay them, as well. And that’s where Hank Stram came in. With smaller, lesser-known players, Hank had to be an innovator. With a small, frail quarterback like Len Dawson, Stram implemented a “moving pocket”. That allowed the bigger, faster offensive linemen to outmaneuver the slower, meaner defensive linemen. And that would lead to a more fan-friendly game. While Hank was a master at coaching, he knew how to motivate the newly-found, small-college athletes. Especially the young athletes from Grambling, Southern, and Texas Southern. And it is here where he will be most remembered by folks of color. Never was there any kind of racial uprising or unfair play on any of Stram’s teams. He just didn’t tolerate it and he made everybody feel included. More important, he treated them fairly at the pocketbook level, as well. Many came poor and left well-paid, especially in their later years. In conclusion, Hank Stram was more than innovation to this new-f league. He was more than a coach, who coined phrases like “matriculating down the field”. Since Stram was in charge, we have not been near a Super Bowl. But he was right up there with Branch Rickey, who cleared the path for Jackie Robinson to play major league baseball. With the same level of commitment, Stram cleared the “economic path” for these young unheard-of athletes, to make a lot of money. Simple as that. He did enlarge our territory.

Thanks, Hank.

Ike is a maverick. . .an individualist. . .a tough character. But when he hosts, he makes sure each of us gets their favorite dish. And though he never articulates it, he really likes most people, especially those who strive to do their best. That's because he has always stood out, in everything he has done. And he still exhibits this "rugged individualism" today, even though he is 72. Thus, this "maverick thing" is deeply-rooted in his youth. Let's explore.

Even as an eight-year-old, he actually rode the train from Kansas City to Detroit, all alone and having to change trains in Chicago. And, most of us met Ike, when he dared to take the chairmanship of the infamous Desegregation Task Force, way back in the early seventies. . .when Kansas City received national attention for its faulty efforts at desegregating its schools. And he is still mad at the University of Missouri for not admitting him, even farther back in the fifties. And when you catch him reminiscing, he even drifts back to his Air Force days when segregation was at its worst in Texas.

Still looking back, Ike speaks positively about his undergraduate days at the University of Michigan and Howard universities. Proudly, he talked about how his Alpha "frat-brothers" saved him from a good ol' spanking, while on a weekender in East Lansing, Michigan. Now, all of this is Ike for he literally lived in times gone by. Times when things were better; parents were stricter; and everybody lived up to a standard. He flat-out revels in yesterday. And it is here that he has problems with the Chiefs. Even though the CALL readers get mad at Ike, he still is a loyal Chiefs' fan. Let me explain.

You see, Ike was literally one of the first season-ticket holders, way back when they played the games at Municipal Stadium at 22nd and Brooklyn. Never missing a game, ol' Ike has seen some real heroes in a Chiefs' uniform. He's seen Ernie Ladd, Mike Garrett, and Otis Taylor in their finest hours. And it angers and disappoints him when he witnesses the Chiefs lapsing slowly into a constant mediocrity. In fact, he thinks the Chiefs will win only three more games this year. To make matters worse, he thinks lowly Oakland will beat the Chiefs on Sunday. That's just the "rugged individualism" that lives inside of him. But, he's good at what he does. In fact, he is the only Monday Nite to pick the point spread exactly on two occasions in the same year. And arguably, he may well be the all-time best predictor. And I guess that's why he goes to extreme lengths, when hosting us... giving us everything we like, including that precious walnut-laced chocolate cake. Even in his kindest moments, he still is a "maverick".

Thus, so many people totally misunderstand him. But he knows that. And, quite frankly, he doesn't give a damn, either. End of story.

A Tribute To Buck

Oh yes, we talked about the Chiefs over at Ike Gardner's house. And we frequently made good references to the chocolate-walnut cake, prepared by Ike's Ann Lillian. And we got the good news that Carl Clark was okay, after a brief visit to the emergency room Sunday. And of course, we highly praised the Chiefs for their comeback victory over the hapless Arizona Cardinals. However, the always-loyal Lloyd Greenfield stated upfront that we ought to dedicate this column to Buck O'Neil. Thus, you have our joint thinking about Buck.

All of us knew Buck, personally. We can see him now, driving around in his late-model Cadillac, wearing that broke-down brim and the extra-thick glasses. Always smiling, he always managed to uplift whomever he met. And for the record, Buck would forget your name but never the circumstances around which he met you. Buck might have enjoyed being in touch with folks, more than anyone I've ever met. And to enhance the encounter Buck would go into oft-repeated act of singing, talking about important folks he'd met, or make some historical reference to his connection to baseball. Night or day. Old or young. Buck acted like he liked and knew everybody.

Regardless of your age, Of Buck could charm you. And if you tarried long enough, you could also see the sounds of serenity, just emanating from his personality. Every hard to reach and teach teenager warmed up when Buck came near. They'd never seen anybody remain exactly the same, regardless of the day of the week or time of day. He seemed forever glad to see you and all of Buck confirmed that.

And regardless of how your day was going, Buck could improve it, right on the spot.

Ain't it funny we don't see a lot of people like Buck anymore? Someone who just enjoyed life to the fullest, complaint-free. And just make sure that he would be remembered, he'd use the smallest things to make big impressions. I can see him now... singing the "Greatest Thing," or he'd be talking about kissing Hillary Clinton, and reminiscing about the many times he hit for the "cycle." We have a job opening in our community now. Is there anyone out there who can replace Of Buck O'Neil?

Probably not. They just don't make 'em like that any more. Case closed.

The answer may lie in the scriptures. The Chiefs are like the prodigal son for they have taken the Kansas City fans through so many changes, so many vicissitudes (ups and downs). In-fact, it is no longer safe to say that you are a Chiefs fan. Take the prodigal son, one day they'll come back...a little raggedy but willing to start all over and become the team they are capable of being. Like the father, who waits for news of the crew why. We must just wait from them, when they decide to turn from their wicked ways.

Dr. Allen, Lloyd Loyal, and I think they are just inches away from “showing up and showing out” again. Yes, we have had to go through some real drama every year, of watching them play good one Sunday only to look like clowns the next. And then we'll welcome the Chiefs to the playoffs, even home-field advantage...and Larry John-son will be crowned the MVP...and the Chiefs will beat not only Baltimore but Oakland, San Diego and Jacksonville. And the television announcers will be saying, “What about those Chiefs?” Wait! Am I dreaming or what? Let's go back to the Hubbards and start this conversation all over.

Chiefs: the best of the worst or the worst of the best?

Let’s give it to the Chiefs. They played half-way respectably against

the Colts. The defense has developed and for the most part, they “hung in there”. But the Chiefs’ problems are far beyond the struggling offensive line and the newly-appointed quarterback. It goes much deeper than that. Either the Chiefs are the "best of the worst" or they are “the worst of the best”.

Either way, they have failed to convince anyone that they are serious contenders. Just reflect on how the national sportscasters ignore us completely, preferring to show highlights of our opponents. That alone tells you that no one thinks the Chiefs are important enough to even mention. At 4-6, we will again struggle in December to make it to a .500 season. And you will remember that we really didn’t make the playoffs last year.

Through a series of luck, we fortuitously landed into the playoffs, only to be ousted quickly. And so let’s start thinking about 2008. Or if you are not ready for that, let’s start looking to who will represent the AFC in the Super Bowl. Will it be undefeated New England or badly-shaken Indianapolis? And start looking at Dallas and Green Bay. Now those are complete teams, combining defense and offense and special teams.

Perhaps the Chiefs ought to relinquish all their games and instead, allow these teams to mentor them in the art of winning. If they do that, maybe they will grow and mature into a respectable NFL franchise.

So, as the holiday season starts, relieve yourself of any fancy thoughts about the Chiefs. Instead, transfer your loyalty to one of the real teams. That way, you will have a great Thanksgiving. Something the Chiefs cannot give you, this year.

BLACK ON BLACK CRIME IN CHICAGO

I hate it when we have to talk about black coaches playing opposite black coaches. It is as if there is some kind of Cinderella brotherhood that will magically turn the game into a last-minute tie or even a win for both teams.

It’s like the NFL should change its rules . . . when black coaches play each other. Remember, all the hoopla, surrounding the last Super Bowl when Lovie Smith and the Chicago Bears played Tony Dungy and the Indianapolis Colts. Well, this week, Herm “The Firm” Edwards will play against Lovie Smith. Both black and both with stellar reputations.

But, Lovie has the better team. He’s got a quarterback. Herm don’t. He’s got a defense. Herm don’t. Lovie can go several deep. Herm can’t. Whatever the match, Lovie will kick the devil out of Herm and his Chiefs. It will be “black on black crime”. And at the end of the game, Herm will meet Lovie at the center of the field. They will whisper something in each other’s ears. And security will lead them in opposite directions.

But, opinion varies when it comes to Green Bay. The Haters, led by Ike and Ike Booker think that Brett Favre is too much for the Chiefs to handle, even at home.

And here's some news. . . Congressman Bubba Cleaver has joined up with The Haters, after witnessing how Favre wiped out Denver last Monday night. But, the Thinkers of the Group believe the Chiefs have a chance. Carl, Dr. Allen, Lloyd, and Ed are going with the Chiefs. Now, the "Thinkers" study the game and have concluded that the Chiefs can be real contenders, with a few breaks. They earnestly believe the Chiefs have developed a solid defense and a surprising passing offense, all leading to unexpected victories.

This emerging pass offense includes the new "Otis Taylor". . Dwayne Bowe. He is a star about to shine and we all have zeroed in on his big-time playmaking ability. But, I'm with the Thinkers. . . the Chiefs can and will beat Green Bay.

In the meantime, I repeat. . . "Don't take me too far, too fast, with too little".

HAPPY 2008 DRAFT

Each week for the last 40 years, Monday Nite Footballers have met and discussed football. Mainly about the Chiefs because it was in the late ‘60s, when the Chiefs were in their last and only heyday. In fact, the Chiefs have not made it to the Super Bowl since 1969.

That memorable victory over Minnesota put Kansas City on the map. Otis Taylor, Glouster Richardson, Tank Holmes, Buck Buchanan and Bobby Bell became local and world-wide celebrities. And each of us vicariously enjoyed the hype. Funny thing though. If you are under 50, you probably don't even remember those days and for you all, the Chiefs have stunk, as long as you have known about them.

Add to the frustration, the Chiefs were the backbone of the AFC West. Ironically, each of their division partners have been to the Super Bowl. . . since the Chiefs have. Oakland, San Diego and Denver, all have been there and won. And yet, the Chiefs, then the most innovative team in football, hardly can earn any kind of victory. . . anywhere. And it is sad for us old guys, who once went to Arrowhead, in the rain, in the snow, in the cold. Today, only Ike and Dr. Allen still go with any regularity. Moreover, our one-channel conversations have drifted to “Search For Tomor-

row”.

Yes, New England will go undefeated all the way. And yes, it will be between Dallas and Green Bay in the Super Bowl against the Patriots. And yes, there will be no black coaches vying for the coveted ring, this year. So, all we have to talk about is the draft, come April. And you know, Ike and Carl are demanding that the Chiefs draft an offensive linemen. With Shields and Roaf gone, the front line must receive priority. And after that, draft some cornerbacks. Both Surtain and Law are Medicare-eligible and need to move up to assistant coaches in some middle school. After that, we don't care who the Chiefs draft.

Bottom line, we hope the Chiefs get back to the Super Bowl, before we die. After all, we don't have that much longer to even remember. Go Chiefs in 2008.

'Monday Night Footballers' Hold Annual New Year's Party

With fun, festivity and food, the "Monday Nite Footballers" 1981 annual New Year's Eve party went off "with a big bang." Over 250 persons jammed the Barcelona and Mediter-ranean meeting rooms at the Glenwood Manor, 91st and Metcalf.

Once there, the guests were treated to the finest food, the best drink and the best music possible. There was enough of everything for everybody and once started, the party lasted till around 4 a.m.

There was no space on the dance floor.

The dinner, featuring turkey and brisket, salads and blackeyed peas, was the high point of the even-ing. Two large bars, operated by members of the group, served "spirits" to move the festivities all night right into New Year's Day.

With horns, whistles and traditional hats, the group wished everyone in Kansas City a prosperous New Year and hoped that those present will again join them in 1982.

Group members are Charles Allen, D.D.S.; C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Ollie Hubbard, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., James T. Nunnelly, Jerry Price, Ed West and Thomas Brown.

Editor's Note: San Francisco was eliminated, losing to the New York Giants on Sunday, Dec. 30.

Before each Superbowl playoff, the Monday Nite Footballers have a vote to predict something about the Super Bowl Game. Founding members Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., and Ollie Hubbard all differed on the “final two.”

Monday Nite Football Members Speak On The Chiefs And Mackovic

By Jim Nunnelly

Now that the In- terscholastic League foot- ball year is over, we have to turn our attention to other local matters. Name- ly, the plight of the Kansas City Chiefs’ head football coach, John Mackovic.

But before we consider that question, lets thank all the coaches and players for a good Interscholastic League football season. Special praise to Sam Cofield, his staff, Lincoln Academy, and Southeast for their strong playoff season.

Now back to the Chiefs and John Mackovic. Three critical issues need to be addressed when talking as to whether Mackovic should be retained as head coach, his record, his rela- tionship with his players and the owners’ position. Then after considering those points, we will again make our views known.

First his record. This season the Chiefs have won three and lost eight games, more importantly, they have lost seven straight games. This all comes after a hopeful 3-1 start. Everyone was looking to the Chiefs to better last year’s 8-8 record. Right now, they would have to win all five remaining games to equal last year’s record. That’s pretty unlikely.

Now let’s consider his relationship with his players. It is evident that Mackovic blames his players. Earlier, he took away the player’ lunches, reacting to their poor per-

formance. When something like that happens, no player will perform when there is squabbling going on.

And just what should the owners do? Well, Lamar Hunt owner of the 23 year old franchise has only said Mackovic will finish his third year here. After that, he will make a decision on his future. Mackovic, if fired, would follow every Chiefs coach’s plight; Hank Stram, Paul Wiggin, and Marv Levy, all were fired. And, only Stram has taken the Chiefs to the playoffs.

Well, what do the Mon- day Nite-ers think? Some of our members have watch- ed the Chiefs even since Municipal Stadium days. Ike Gardner and Carl Clark, like many fans, are frustrated that the Chiefs can’t win. They know there is no pride in being a fan and no joy in being a “Chief”.

For those reasons, we have to say that “it’s time for a change.” We cannot afford to make Kansas City a losing franchise like Buf- falo and Baltimore. We were once a Super Bowl championship and the news media spoke of Kansas City as if it was the Roman Em- pire of professional foot- ball.

The bottom line is that Mackovic must be reliev- ed. And we should remember too, that if fans thought Stram was so bad, they now wish for his in- novation and prestige. It is something to think about.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Sam Cofield:

Pushing, Without A Cry

Not the cry, but it’s the flight of the wild duck that leads the flock to fly and follow. Let’s see how that old Chinese proverb applies to Sam Cofield.

First, his flight. For three years now, he’s been the Interscholastic League Coordinator for the Kansas City, Mo., school district. And with declining school enrollments and tougher academic standards, he faces the most critical times of inner city high school athletics.

He reads daily where some district or some state has followed the trend and set tougher grade stand ards for the high school athlete. Proposition 48 al- ready says that no fresh- man can enter a NCAA Division I school unless he or she has made a 2.0 grade point average and scored 700 on a nationally recognized test. Wow, he says without a cry. He’s real careful not to push too hard when he talks about this. No excitement, just calm.

He’s also trying to pre- pare his league and school district to be a leader – not a follower – in accepting this well-entrenched trend. Everyone, everywhere is pushing for tougher aca- demic standards for ath- letes.

But, it’s not only the ath- letes he has to convince of this trend, there’s the nine schools, the fans, the community, the school board. And, in particular, there’s the coaches.

The coaches are already implementing programs to meet this 2.0 GPA standard before August, 1986. That’s good. And Sam is proud and cautious about this. He knows that it is only a step in the right direction. It’s also difficult to do where the coaches are the lowest paid of all the surrounding districts.

So, at this point, he’s not willing to push for a “no pass, no play” rule, where the athlete must pass all courses before being allowed to participate. In- stead, he wants to proceed gradually toward a tougher grade point - say 2.3 - and allow a student to fail maybe one course. Not quite like Texas, but Cofield understands that a thou- sand mile journey starts with just that one step.

Those who know Cofield say he’s quiet and calm but effective. He boasts that the coaches are al- ready implementing pro- grams to meet this trend.

He wants the community, particular groups like Mon- day Nite Footballers, to “watch” the progress. And, help, by supporting tutoring programs – to assure that the athletes do well in the classroom. He wants the Interscholastic League to be a good example.

Speaking of examples, let’s now take a look at Sam Cofield, himself. Be- sides being a preacher’s son and married to Frances Cofield, Sam is a “quiet go getter”—up and at work by 6:45 a.m. and doesn’t stop until 10 p.m. Always at a sports event, some- where near 121 nights a year.

Beginning as a practice teacher at Lincoln (Acade- my) book in 1966, he has steadily progressed up through the system. The next year he became a coach. Later, the Athletic Director and then, in 1983, he took on the issues-laden position he now holds – Interscholastic League Coordinator. So he knows “his word” from the bot- tom up – that word, ex- ample.

Now, the most important force that guides Cofield is that he was a star athlete who did well in the class- room. In 1961, he was an All-Metro Selection at Sumner high school in Kan- sas. Most important he was on the honor roll. And at Lincoln university, he was on the basketball team, until a back injury crippled his career. But, he was a honor student, there, too - talk about an example.

And Cofield knows what the athletes, the coaches, the teachers, are all faced with – when a nation de- cides it wants its athletes to be good students as well. He’s been each of them, at one time or another. Further, he knows it’s real- ly possible to get tougher. He know it’s just a short time when it’ll be imple- mented – everywhere, and just in the big schools. And, he’s telling everybody that – quietly, with graceful articulation, and without a cry.

Not the cry, but it’s the flight of the wild duck that leads the flock to fly and follow. Good luck, Sam.

Monday Nite Footballers Treat 'Sweethearts' To Candlelight Dinner

By Jim Nunnelly

Last Saturday evening, the Monday Nite Footballers entertained their sweethearts at the Alameda Rooftop restaurant on the Country Club Plaza. Seated at the two center-most tables, the group easily became the main attraction in the 280-seat facility.

Before the delightful dinner, the ladies were treated to cocktails in the Rooftop Lounge, overlooking the well-lighted Country Club Plaza. The lounge and restaurant have been a favorite spot for visitors and Kansas Ciians for the past fourteen years.

Michael Turner, manager of the restaurant, stated, "It was a delight serving such an illustrious group."

The group tries' to treat the sweethearts to a "night out" in return for their many support activities. The sweethearts have been instrumental in supporting the group with parties, community events, fund raising, and weekly hosting activities.

It goes without saying that the ladies and men were dressed for the occasion. The event usually is held around Valentine's Day.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Horace Peterson: Questions And Answers For A Black Sports History

There's a man in the old fire station and he's got a lot of old stuff up there. Let's go hear what he has to say. Who's the man, what's he got and what's he talking about?

Well, the man is Horace Peterson and the old fire station is the Black Archives of Mid America Building located at 2033 Vine. Some of the old stuff is information and exhibits about black sports; and he has a whole lot to say about it. Let's look at all four topics — up close — starting with the man.

Horace M. Peterson, 1964 Central high school grad-uate, accompanied Martin Luther King to Selma, Ala. in 1965 and marched in Washington in 1963. He's a jogger, television show producer, and 22-year member of Freedom, Inc. He was also a relay runner of Central's State Cham-pionship track team in 1964. Peterson is married to Barbara Peterson and the father of four children. That's Horace-in facts.

But that's not all there is to Peterson. It's what he says and what he does that puts the "excitedly serious" smile on his face. For 13 years, he's been the executive director of the Black Archives.

Now, about the old fire station building. Built in 1911 during the old Pen derghast period, the two-story structure was built by "WPA" hands. It's fitting that it would now house Peterson and his adminis trative assistant, Deborah Jones, and tons of black "memories." There is even more up close. Now, let's see what's there.

On those two floors and in the garage are lots of information, pictures and exhibits, particularly about black sports. There's O.J. Simpson's first pro jersey, number 32 for Buffalo, donated by Woody Smallwood, a former member of the Negro New York Yankees and now a repre sentative for Miller Brew ing Company. Now, how about that!

Then, there's information on the old Kansas City Buf faloes, a semi-pro football team of the late fifties. Baseball fans will like all the information about the Kansas City Monarchs and the Kansas City Clowns. These items were donated by Arrington "Bub Ble" Klice.

And now Horace turns the excitement up. He's talking seriously now — and proud. There's an exhibit featuring a black big game hunter in town. And, Horace has his collection right there in the old fire station.

And there's more, too much to mention here. Now, Horace quickens gracefully; and like a seasoned pro and begins to talk about the whole subject — fewer facts, more com mentary.

What does the man at the old fire station say about all these exhibits and pictures? He speaks slowly now — for emphasis.

It all starts to "come alive". He has a focus on black baseball — the Monarchs, the Clowns, Satchel Paige, Woody Smallwood, Jackie Robinson, the 1924 Black World Series, Frank White. It's here that his "militant approach to his tory" flourishes. "We've been jammed," he states. Baseball meant a lot to blacks here in Kansas City and Horace is a little mad about it all.

He wants to know why they didn't name the Royal's baseball stadium after Satchel Paige? Why doesn't "homegrown" Frank White have a life-time contract with the Royals? Why is there a "special" section in Base ball's Hall of Fame for the old Negro Leagues? Why don't people know that major league baseball for blacks was a natural out-growth of the old Negro Leagues?

Maybe the reasons aren't so obvious, Horace. And, maybe, that's why Black Archives is important — to tell us. And maybe that's why Monday Nite Foot ballers are writing about you and your work here in this column. And maybe one day the many questions will have answers.

And those answers will be housed over at the "old fire station at 2033 Vine." And those answers will be another step of progress in the racial struggle. And people will say — "Old Horace Peterson did his part."

It's something to thing about.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnclly

Debi Thomas: Falling or Rising?...

Editors Note: The comments in this column were rendered by the sports-minded wives of the Monday Night Footballers. While each wanted to remain anonymous, the reason for including them in this particular column is obvious.

Did Debi Thomas fall or did she rise? That is a big question and it always, history will judge as. Before we answer that ques- tion, let’s look back to that Saturday night, when a Su- per-Bowl like audience watched Debi in the Calgary Winter Olympics. The tension. The disappoint- ment. The dignity.

Everybody knew that Debi Thomas was tense, some even say that she just plain “froze up.” Especially since she was the leading competitor and was to perform last. Many believe this was the cause of her eventual defeat. The athletic anticipation turned into neurotic waiting and finally emotional exhaustion. After all, the eyes of the entire world awaited her arrival, particularly after the cautious perfor- mance of her ever-present rival, Katerina Witt.

That night at Calgary, history was made with an ebony “soul on ice.” Debi took to the paradise-like pavement, knowing that she had to beat not only Witt, but a Japanese young- ster and a homegrown Ca- nadian. All had performed above and beyond expec- tations.

Now, quickly to the disappointment. Within a minute from her beginning, an unsteady turn flawed her hopefully-perfect, long program. And after the second flaw, we all knew that her hopes for the top medal had fallen and faded. And as they showed the Thursday night before, the restrictively strict judges would reduce her score from gold to bronze. The disappointment was there. And we all felt it.

Yes, the much-publicized ice goddess had fallen. And the “Star Spangled Ban- ner” would not play for Debi. And at last, the re- alization of having lost be- gan to slowly set into her face. Debi, her gender, her country, third world na- tives, and a whole race of hopeful blacks shared the disappointing fact that the possibility of a black Olympic figure skating champion would have to be delayed to another point. Another time. Dignified and astonished, she searched for a way out. She searched for the right words to describe the long awaited, fought-for dream that had slowly slipped away. Not in Calgary. Not in 1988. Not in the Olympics.

Commentary

A way out. Oh, no. She didn’t really need a way out. Debi Thomas has won not only a bronze medal, but gained the hearts and respect of all the world ath- letes and spectators alike.

Regaining her stately, slightly-girlish smile, one neither explained nor com- plained. And unlike so many athletes, she was prepared for the day when her athletic prowess, phys- ical grace, and the hand clapping crowd would not be a part of her everyday world. In her head, she hung up the figure skating shoes and reached for the long, white, physician only coat. Though we didn’t ac- tually see this transition

DEBI THOMAS

could see her accepting the victorious defeat and mentally preparing her- self for the next challenge. Not an athletic challenge, but an academic one.

After the now-famous, whisper-like, “I’m sorry,” Debi mentally slipped from the ice back to studying medicine at Stanford. She neither explained nor com- plained. And unlike so many athletes, she was prepared for the day when her athletic prowess, phys- ical grace, and the hand clapping crowd would not be a part of her everyday world. In her head, she hung up the figure skating shoes and reached for the long, white, physician only coat. Though we didn’t ac- tually see this transition occur, we knew that she was about to become an- other star, this time in the classroom.

We salute you Debi for your many achievements in your short twenty year lifespan. Those on and off the ice. Most of all, we congratulate you for rising, every time you fell.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

The Campanis Comments

What does Al Campanis have in common with Mount Calvary and Watergate?

Before we comment on that, let's look real closely at what he said to cause all the talk about major league baseball and its lack of "front office color."

Well, Campanis said that blacks do not possess "the necessities" to become field managers or front office material. For those of us with ancient memories, we know that there have been three black managers in baseball. So, what's the fuss?

Simply put, it is just inappropriate for anybody in any kind of position to say anything about the inadequacies or the inferiorities of blacks. We are still wondering what propels people "in high places" to speak so candidly about the always-sensitive subject of race. What inspiration could provoke the front office vice president of the most celebrated and most progressive team in baseball to make such an untimely, and insensitive statement?

Jerry Price is kind and usually tries to find some "decent" explanation. He attributes Campanis' comments to his age. After all, the former Dodger executive, is threescore and ten (70). However so, it is still hard to understand why someone would make such a statement, when others have been fired for doing the same. Any man of any age should know better.

Another aspect of this unfortunate mess was when Campanis added a little more insight to his topic. He not only spoke of the lack of "necessities," he made matters worse by informing the audience that blacks couldn't float either—as if being able to swim is a requirement to be considered for the front office in baseball. And for all that, Ike Gardner says Campanis should have been fired, as it was with Earl Butz.

But from Japan to Jersey City, it has got everyone talking again—about affirmative action or the lack of it. Jesse Jackson is talking to the baseball commissioner. And Joseph Lowery, in town for the SCLC board meeting, talked to Royals' owner, Ewing Kauffman. And, it's working, too. And, pretty soon, all twenty six teams will flaunt the fact that they now have a black assistant field manager and director of community relations. And some of us will forget that all of this came from a 19th century, terribly-biased comment from an old man, nearing retirement. Something good from something bad.

And, that's what the name Al Campanis will have in common with Mount Calvary and Watergate. Something good came out of those events, too. Members of the Monday Niters are Charles Allen DDS; C. Ashley; Paul Booker; Tom Brown; Carl Clark; Isaac Gardner, Jr.; Ollie Hubbard; Walt Livingston; Jim Nunnelly; Jerry Price; and Ed West.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim Nunnelly

Instant Replay: Second Look, Second Chance, Or Second Opinion

While only a few people know that violent crime went down 15 percent in Kansas City, everyone knows that the cry for instant replay in the National Football League is up this year. And, with the Super Bowl coming up next Sunday, there'll even be more talk about how non-officiating affects the outcome of any game. Let's look at some calls, then the officials, the decisionmakers and finally our opinion.

First, a look at the calls. On any Sunday, 60 million fans can see that a player stepped out of bound before crossing the goal line. Call touchdown. And, if you're at Arrowhead, the giant replay scoreboard can detect interference or a flagrant personal foul. No call or penalty. And, even though San Diego changes are in the Super Bowl, few believe they would have gotten there without some help. The officials.

The Decisionmakers

What about these seven guys in control of a multi-billion dollar industry. They don't work full time. They load up every week from all over and run this program called the NFL. They're insurance agents, doctors, former high school coaches and millionaire executives. Some step up from the college ranks and still fewer, come from the Canadian and now-defunct world football league. And, for this part-time gig, they rake in around $2,000 per game. But, lately, there magnificent seven have invited the use of millions of fans, with their mistakes in judgement. And that sets off another discussion about instant replay.

Now, the owners, not the coaches, nor the players, have clearly stated, "No instant replay." The decisionmakers say it takes too long to decide, causing the game to go too long. And, of course, the longer the game, the less pleased the networks and football widows will be. And, of course, they say that instant replay isn't instant, sometimes delaying the game up to ten minutes or even longer. So, what do we say about the instant replay with Super Bowl Sunday, just around the corner?

Our Opinion

Well, one way Ike Gardner wants instant replay to return as a permanent component of the game. This on again, off again talk is unnecessary and insulting to the fans. And, with technological advancements, both inside the networks, and inside the stadium, there is really no excuse to debating the "pros and cons" of instant replay. The time has come for the owners to listen to the fans and work out something to address the length of the game, at the same time, ensure playing calling accuracy. The fans deserve a second look and second opinion and the officials need a second chance. As for more on the reduction in violent crime, call the Jackson County Prosecutor's Office.
