Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly

Minnesota Vikings Vs. Kansas City Chiefs Sunday, Dec. 12, At 7 P.M. Arrowhead Stadium ESPN And KCFX 101.1 FM

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Dr. Charles Allen is a prominent dentist here in Kansas City. Back in the fifties, he and his dad operated a chicken place at 18th and Vine. Later, he and his dad reopened the Birdland, a renowned jazz club from the old days in the jazz district. Thus, he is self reliant, self assured and definitely a self starter. Consequently, each week, no matter what the circumstances are, he picks the Chiefs to win. Even though the Chiefs' running game is inconsistent, Grbac will throw his predictable interceptions, Allen still goes with the Kansas City Chiefs. That's because he knows life is full of vicissitudes (predictable ups and downs), and if you stick it out, remain positive, and stay prayerful, things will work themselves out.

Just as Allen, Greenfield, Clark and West believed, the Chiefs are now back in the play-off race, standing at 7-5. They've watched the Chiefs' defense rise to the occasion time after time. They've seen Bam go back and forth and Derrick Thomas rise and fall like the holy Roman Empire. And on top of all that, they cringed at the start of every fourth quarter, knowing the Chiefs go erratic in the last stanza of each game. But still the Chiefs are in the play-off race and each of them think the Chiefs will find a way, again, to win this Sunday against Minnesota.

But, there's another side, the side that is represented usually by Ike Gardner and Paul Booker. Both were respected and very capable educators in the school district. In fact, many praise Ike and Booker for their effectiveness as educators, especially in the handling of children with special needs. However, when it comes to football, they assert the Chiefs are not beating play-off contenders, just beating up on lowly San Diego and falling needlessly to Chicago. They see the Chiefs as "too iffy" and think that the Chiefs will return to a 7-7 record, before a national, prime time television audience. For sure, these two venerable educators have reason to believe the way they do. That's because the Chiefs have consistently, game after game, let us down. And it's never a total victory when they do win. What's the point?

Again, the Chiefs will have their backs against the wall. Here we are, well into December, and we are trying to "get there." And to make it into the playoffs, we still have Pittsburgh, first place Seattle, and revengeful Oakland, waiting after Sunday. It's going to be hard and no one can tell for sure how it will at all turn out. Like Gladys Knight, somehow the Chiefs will make it through. So I'm with Charles, Carl, Greenfield, and West...the Chiefs will be 8-5 on Monday morning.

Here's what we think:

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly

Dallas Cowboys (8-5)

vs.

Kansas City Chiefs (5-8)

Sunday, Dec. 13, At 3:15 P.M.

Arrowhead Stadium

Commentary, Conversation And Chatter

ABOUT THE CHIEFS: Fresh from a respectable loss to the undefeated Denver Broncos, the Chiefs have to face the fact that 1998 will not be a winning season: 8-8 is the best the Chiefs will do, assuming we beat Dallas, the Giants and Oakland. Because of the consistent play of Rich Gannon, Bam Morris and Derrick Alexander, the Chiefs have scored 30 or more points in the last three games. Too little, too late, but refreshing. On the defensive side, linebacker Donnie Edwards, safety Reggie Tongue, and linebacker Anthony Davis led the team in tackles. All this has helped save a disastrous season. Most fans were set up to believe that the Chiefs were better than any team in the NFL. They were, but they collapsed somewhere and the objective now is to beat the Dallas Cowboys. And they will by 10 points.

ABOUT THE DALLAS COWBOYS: Dallas leads the NFC East by two games. Their 8-5 record includes two straight losses to New Orleans and the Minnesota Vikings. Michael Irvin is still the favorite target of veteran quarterback, Troy Aikman. And the evocative Emmitt Smith still is and will always be Dallas' most prolific runner. Currently, he has rushed for 1,113 yards and 11 touchdowns. Not bad for a strong running back on a struggling team. Defensively, tackle Leon Lett and end Greg Ellis will tamper with the renewed Chiefs offense, and last but not least, is Deon Sanders, the punt returner and cornerback extraordinary. He will be "the show" out at Arrowhead. Now dedicated to Christianity, Deon still is the fastest, most gifted and talented player of the Dallas Cowboys. He is one player who can do everything he says. That, alone, is worth going out to Arrowhead.

Inimitable, The ‘Word From Grand Dad’

Listen tomorrow to Carl Boyd's Generation Rap on Hot 103 for the “Word From Grand Dad.” Though another word was promised on the show last week, this week's word will be inimitable. See how we use it in a tribute to Carl Boyd.

Farewell To Carl Boyd At Friendship, August 27

Ruth Hill, vice president for The Art of Positive Teaching, announced a farewell event for Carl Boyd. Boyd and his wife “Wonderful Wanda” are moving to Phoenix at the end of the month. Thus, on Friday evening, Aug. 27, starting at 6 o'clock, friends and associates are encouraged to bid farewell to them at the Friendship Baptist church, near 35th and Van Brunt, just south of the Veterans Hospital. Boyd began teaching at J.S. Chick school back in 1980 and launched The Art of Positive Teaching consulting firm in 1986. He is well known throughout the country as a lecturer and educator. In 1986, he began the Generation Rap program and has nurtured it to a natural part of Kansas City's morning culture. Any other questions, call 363-5270.

Pre-Teen Basketball Star From Ottawa

We would not expect you to know the name, Shana Wheeler. She's not even in high school and the media doesn't usually chase after middle schoolers. Nonetheless, she's an up and coming basketball star from Ottawa, Kas. Already 5'11", the eighth grader led the Kansas Belles to a national AAU tournament title in Springfield, Mo., recently. Seventy-two teams entered the under 12 meet, and Shana rose as one of the tournament's top scorers. Not only was she the sole African American on the team, she was the third tallest, yielding to two teammates just over six feet. She won praise from area coaches, after seeing her hold her own against older, more experienced high school players. Shana is the the niece of Monday Nite Footballer, Ed West.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

Chiefs Struggling Offense

It is apparent that the Chiefs still have problems with their offense. Gunther Cunningham says it's their offensive line. They've even tried to blame poor Bam Morris who was their only hope from last year's dismal display of professional football. The newcomers, Shehee and Cloud, are yet untented and can not even be counted on just yet. But, everybody in town knows the problem is in the quarterback, and particularly with Elvis Grbac. When Grbac threw the first interception in the Jacksonville game last Thursday, there was a collec tive sigh of disbelief. That's because no one can believe that we still believe in Grbac's ability to lead this offense. Even Warren Moon may not be the answer, for he is right up there with the "Montang Medicare quarterbacks” -- too old, too slow, and resting on past performances. Why we continue to be a retirement center for “used up” quarterbacks is beyond reason. Any way, tonight the Chiefs will try to ignite their “midwest coast offense” by allowing Grbac to start the show. Look for another sigh of disbelief.

Greg Hill, The Real Deal Is In Detroit

You have to remember Greg Hill. He’s the one we drafted and then sat him on the bench. He had speed and had set all kinds of rushing records in college. The Chiefs said he stumbled and was too fast for his feet. Any way, Hill was expected to fill the unfillable shoes of Marcus Allen and he just didn't do it. Now, after a short sting with the St. Louis Rams, he is now expected to replace the irre placeable Barry Sanders at Detroit. Let's all hope he will get a better chance with the Lions and with the right coaching and the right motivation. Hill could have the 1,000-yard season he never had here in Kansas City. Look for Hill's revenge when he comes to Arrowhead on September 26, dressed in "motor city silver and blue."

Bill Clark Doing Well

The venerable president of the Urban League, Bill Clark, is recovering well and is expected to return to duty on a limited basis soon. Clark has been ill for several weeks and is recovering at home.

Significant Potpourri

"Swinging" Sammie Sosa is ahead in the homerun duel with Mark McGwire of St. Louis. The Cubs' outfielder has become known for his patented "skip" when he realizes that he has hit another ball out of the park. As of Wednesday morning, Sosa has hit 55 and is on pace to set another homerun record set by McGwire last year at 70. Keep on doing it, Sosa....Evander Holyfield and Lennox Lewis have set the rematch date for November 13. In their last contest, they fought to a controversial draw, quite unusual in heavyweight championship fights. It may be noted that Holyfield failed to wear his robe with the Bible verse in his last outing. The New Orleans Saints are hurting. Their number one draft choice, Rickey Williams, is still recovering. Three other potential starters are out and questionable for the season opener. Sep tember 12. Deion Sanders' toe is getting better and he is now expected to play opening day.

Thirty Years For Monday Nite Footballers

Next week, we will celebrate 30 years of predictions, football talk, and as a fan club organization. In fact, we may well be the oldest, organized fan club for the Kansas City Chiefs. More important, the same 10 guys have met each Monday night, watched football on television, and never have departed as friends. We have watched our kids grow up and become parents themselves. We have watched each other go from young Turks to mellowed middleagers and what's more, we've all become more health and family minded. As educa tors and community leaders, we assist with children and youth at all times. We are gravely concerned about the issues in public education, and will be watching the charter school development closely. As important, we want to do everything to insulate our children from the ravages of drugs, and will pro mote and support any organization that protects and develops our children. Thus, next week we'll reveal our predictions and opinions just like we have for the last three decades. Thanks for listening to us.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

Kansas City Chiefs Vs. Denver Broncos Sunday, Dec. 5, At 3 P.M. Miles High Stadium KCTV-5 And KCFX 101 FM

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Ed West helps people select and install their personal computers. He has a knack for that kind of stuff. But he also is a decent prognosticator, for he, along with Lloyd Greenfield, Carl Clark, and the venerable Charles Allen picked the Chiefs to beat Oakland. We who correctly picked Kansas City had doubts but thought the Raiders also had weaknesses and if our defense played well, and our running backs would run, then we had a chance. Both of those wishes came true. Chris Dishman earned Defensive Player of the Week honors for his fumble recovery and interception touchdowns. And out of the clear blue sky came a quick hitting running back named Mike Cloud who scampered for about 60 yards in eleven carries. Though these two weren’t the only heroes from last Sunday’s game, they represented what we’ve always said: When the defense scores and if we get a first-class running back, then the Chiefs will win consistently.

Thus, consistency should become the Chiefs number one objective. A team cannot win by defense alone. We still have not matured offensively. There’s our garage sale quarterback Elvis Grbac. Though he played okay against Oakland; we all cringed again when he threw his predictable interception. And we have to throw the ball far enough to reach the first down marker on third down. And for Pete’s sake, we have to find all the receivers, not just the primary one. Lastly, when will we get somebody to return kick-offs? Consequently, if we address these inconsistency issues, then we can talk about beating Denver in Denver.

As usual, Ike Gardner and Paul Booker thinks Denver will beat the Chiefs. both are retired, fussy, and cantankerous. And, they have a right to think the Chiefs will again be inconsistent and lose, especially up in the mountains of Colorado. But Denver is not the same Denver as they were a year ago when they wrapped up their second consecutive Super Bowl victory. Terrell Davis is out and of course Big Bad John Elway has retired and the Broncos have been indecisive about who is to replace him. Even though, they appeared crippled, the Broncos still have a lanky receiver named Ed McCaffrey. Not a Shannon Sharpe but a dangerously deceptive receiver who consistently gets open, no matter what the coverage. So, in addition to being consistent, we have to watch and cover McCaffrey. Even though Dishman played well this past week, he is not the one to cover McCaffrey. What’s the point?

Like always, the Chiefs will fumble and stumble along and they will make enough mistakes to make us all join Ol’ Ike in his cynicism. But they will also find a way to beat archrival Denver. It may be Shehee this week. Or it might be Derrick Alexander who will hold on to the ball. And maybe Tony Gonzalez may break one. And these unknown maybes are what makes Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, and Ed West think that the Chiefs will be 7-5 on Monday morning. I’m with the “maybe” guys. Here’s what we think:

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Well, the critics are right....the Chiefs are not going to make the play-offs and face the reality of another losing season. But, the diehard fans are just as right...the Chiefs will not be the losingest team in the National Football League. Truthfully, the Chiefs are either the best of the worst or the worst of the best. Either way, they are 5-5 and it is no longer news that Sunday's game with the 5-5, Oakland Raiders may be in jeopardy as well. We say that for a number of reasons.

First, Rich Gannon will be trying to show the Chiefs that he really should be in Grbac's shoes, running our garage sale offense. Even Gunther Cunningham is rethinking his decision to go with "concrete feet" Elvis. Everyone knows Gannon put the fire in our dull, midwest coast offense. But that's not all. Secondly, Oakland is a longtime rivalry. In the older days, it didn't matter what Kansas City did, as long as they beat Oakland. Simply, the goodness or the badness of the season rested entirely on how we played against the "silver and back." Certainly, that feeling will come into play Sunday for sure. But there's another reason this game will be vitally important to both teams. Each is fighting for respect. Indianapolis, Miami, New England, Tennessee, Jacksonville, and even Buffalo are expected to be playing beyond January 2. Oakland nor the Chiefs are currently in that number, save for some unapparent miracle. The next six games are not shoe-ins for either team. Thus, each team faces the stark possibility of losing every single one of those games, replacing San Diego as the last place team in the AFC West.

Obviously that team would be tragic. And Ike Gardner, Paul Booker and Ollie Hubbard would enjoy having told us so. While Charles Allen, Carl Clark and Lloyd Greenfield would strongly disagree, they nonetheless know the Chiefs have one more opportunity before they give up entirely. Along with Ed West, these loyal, dedicated, diehard fans have been ridiculed week after week, believing in a team that has not proven itself to be effective or viable. So, the Chiefs must beat Oakland Sunday for these brave believers and the many of you out there who continue to wear red, no matter what. As for me, I am still a believer and you should look for the Chiefs to be 6-5 and well on their way, to re-earning and resurrecting their respect. Here's what we think:

ABOUT THE CHARGERS: San Diego is 4-6, just like the Chiefs. In fact, the loser of this game will have sole possession of the AFC West cellar. Under new coach, June Jones, the Chargers have beaten Baltimore and Philadelphia and lost to Denver and Seattle. Craig Whelihan is a so-so quarterback and he'll throw to two so-so receivers, Jones and Still. But, the real threat on the offense is big, bruising Natrone Means. If he plays, he will again test the Kansas City's weakening defense. Also, the Chargers will again showcase All-Pro linebacker Junior Seau. He'll be awesome, and so will Norman Hand, a big, big tackle.

ABOUT THE CHIEFS: This year's edition of the Chiefs is the worst under Schottenheimer and Peterson. In fact, Ed West says the Chiefs are the "best of the incompetents." Who's to blame for this demise? Is it Marty? Or Carl? Or Jimmie Raye, the offensive coordinator? Or is it free agency? For me, it's all about expectations and pre-season hype. We all knew Elvis was shaky at quarterback. And, we urged management to obtain a first-class running back. And everybody knew that many times our special teams received breaks that could have easily gone the other way. So relying heavily on last year's 13-3 record, Derrick Thomas, who is suspended for this Sunday's game in San Diego, and Jason Whitlock started us thinking about a 16-0 season. And once Sports Illustrated and ESPN started talking about going to the Super Bowl, it was natural for us to start talking about it, too. It was there that our disappointments originate. Ike, Harry, and Booker have been telling us the Chiefs are not "all that." In fact, Ole' Ike predicted 8-8 way back in August before the season began. While we are all sad, we should be thankful that we learned the truth soon enough to do something about it. Chiefs by three.

BARRY NOT COMING TO ARROWHEAD ON SEPTEMBER 26

While most of the Kansas City area sports fans are just going bananas over George Brett’s induction into the Hall of Fame, we football fans everywhere are wondering who will take the place of the “irreplaceable” Barry Sanders. Sanders, who has not talked to the Detroit Lions since the end of the 1998 season, called it “quits” Wednesday, sending us all into shock. Sanders, who has relatives in Kansas City, was fourth leading rusher in the NFL last year but, no matter where he ranked in rushing yards, he was considered the best running back since Jim Brown. Lighting quick, Sanders was always All-Pro and respected by all coaches and defensive players throughout the league. Unassuming and short in stature, his legs and magical moves did his talking. When he ran, everybody watched, even those who had just a casual interest in the game. But for now, it appears that local fans won’t be blessed by Sanders’ magic in late September. The only one that is happy with the retirement news is the weak defensive line of the Kansas City Chiefs.

OTIS FOR THE HALL OF FAME

If you go into Maxine’s Fine Foods restaurant, you’ll see Buck O’Neil or you might run into Reverend Hartsfield, or you might even see the seldom-seen Jason Whitlock. But, you’ll always see Otis Taylor, arguably the greatest wide receiver to ever put on a pro football uniform. Now, Taylor spends most of his time, either at Maxine’s or helping his son develop into a respectable athlete. Even so, Taylor still has a hankering to put closure to his NFL career, marked by the Chiefs only Super Bowl victory and countless touchdowns catches, especially in the old Municipal stadium. Ollie Gates, Monday Nite Footballers, Maxine herself, and all the restaurant regulars wonder why Taylor can’t get to Canton (home of the NFL Hall of Fame). Retired now for over 20 years, Taylor’s fate rests with a faraway, unknown selection committee. If you want to help, call or go by Maxine’s.

FOOTBALL SEASON UNDER WAY, FIRST PRE-SEASON GAME AUGUST 15

The first pre-season game here at Arrowhead will be against the Tennessee Titans on August 15, with the season opener scheduled for Chicago on September 12. The next week, September 19, the locals will open their home schedule with the Super Bowl winning, Denver Broncos.

Lloyd Greenfield is a retired firefighter and current real estate professional, and he thinks the Chiefs are just playing good football about now. Ed West and Carl Clark praise the Chiefs' game-winning defense. Well, this week will be the supreme test for Kansas City as they board the plane for Indianapolis to take on the Colts. Fresh from a convincing win, the Colts will have Peyton Manning running an offense that features both running and passing. On top of that, the Colts sport the same record as the Chiefs (5-2). Both teams have good defen- sive personnel and it will be tough for either team to mount and sustain any, long, offensive drives. Derrick Thomas will again lead the defense, along with McGlockton and company. Jerome Woods, James Hasty and Eric Warfield will be ready to see if they really are the best secondary in the American Confer- ence. Thus, Greenfield, West and Clark are all right in their assessment about Kansas City. But let's look in closer at Sunday's 12 noon game.

Everyone in town will be glued to their big screens to see if the Chiefs can squeeze another road win. The Nap Town crowd will loud and definitely a fac- tor in the game's outcome. And while we don't like to say it, the Chiefs do tend to fold on the road, like at Chicago and in the first game at San Diego. You will note that except for Baltimore, all the Chiefs' wins are at home...where 78,000 screaming and booing fans make Derrick Thomas raise his arms in the air, sig- naling for the crowd to "get busy."

Even though there won't be a hometown friendly crowd, the Chiefs will get major help from Stoyanovich who will earn his pay by kicking decisive field goals. And, of course, big-play man, Derrick Alexander, will again rise to the occasion and lead the team in receiving yards. No real help cometh from the running backs save for a decent showing from Rashaan Shehee. And as always, this all depends on unpredictable Elvis Grbac, Kansas City's garage sale quar- terback. If he doesn't hurt us with interceptions and unnecessary time-outs, the offense will have a chance to stay on the field. Admittedly, no matter how good they have been, the defense cannot do it alone. Derrick Parker over at Seven Oaks Barber Shop says that Elvis must perform decently, just like he did out at Arrowhead against San Diego. Bottom line.

If the "iffy" Chiefs can remain mistake-free, they will keep their shared hold on first place in the AFC West. If not, they will fall victims to the fast emerging, well coached Seattle Seahawks, also 5-2 and first place co-occupants. Do Charles Allen believes in these Chiefs. I do, too, and look for Kansas City to win, even though it will be very, very, very close. Here's what we think:

Now what? That's the question the Chiefs are asking their season-long critics. Since falling needlessly to Chicago way back in mid-September, the Chiefs have been busily correcting the problems and now find themselves alone, atop the AFC West. Their destiny for post-season playoffs lies solely in their hands. Since beginning this topsy-turvy roller coaster ride, here's what these amazing Chiefs have accomplished. The critics said, "Throw more to the tight end." Tony Gonzalez has caught six touchdown passes during this recent winning streak. "Concrete feet" Grbac has to stop throwing game-losing interceptions. He stopped. They said further, "The Chiefs have to open up their offense." They've been throwing more on first down and even Derrick Alexander ran an 82 reverse to demolish Pittsburgh. It seems the Chiefs have solved every major problem put before them, including threatening to bench Vanover and Derrick Thomas for poor playing habits. So, the question is...now what? Well, the last criticism is getting the kickoffs into their opponents' end zone. Those who go to the games see how the opposing teams get good field position because Stoyanovich just can't kick the ball very far. So, this week, ol' problem-solving Gunther went out and got a kicker who can supposedly accomplish this task. Again and again, the Chiefs have steadily addressed all these concerns. And because of that, the "weak-in-faith" have gradually returned to talking more favorably about these strong-willed Chiefs. And assuming the newly-acquired kicker can perform, the Chiefs are in good shape to complete the rags-to-riches odyssey. But the question still remains, now what? Of course, accurate prognosticators like Charles Allen, Carl Clark, Lloyd Greenfield, Ed West, and Walt Livingston all say the Chiefs are ready to beat Seattle. Even Paul Booker and Ollie Hubbard, predictable critics of the Chiefs, have changed their minds and are now convinced the Chiefs deserve to be AFC West champions. But, ancient and obstinate Gardner still thinks the Chiefs will fold. Finding nothing to criticize, he says there will be a Y2K glitch and Seattle will topple Kansas City. Of course, Ike has lived a long time and I'm sure he has reason to know that the unexpected can be expected. While that's true, this is not the time or the place to apply that adage. When someone asks "now what," we should all answer in unison...The AFC Conference Championship. Here's what we think:

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Honoring Carl Clark, The Sports Fan

Carl Clark: High school quarterback. A 1952 grad- uate of Lincoln High. A Navy Air Traffic Con- troller. A Journeyman machinist. A husband. A 32nd degree Mason and Shriner. A traveler to 150 ports. A father. And a first- class sports fan.

Before we say exactly why we are honoring Carl, let's look at Carl's life at home; his secret thoughts; and his life as an unique sports fan with the Monday Nite Footballers.

First, Carl at home. There, he's quite a con- versationalist, comfor- table and sincere. He and his Mary Lynn talk end- lessly -- about the lottery; the elderly; taxes; the Feb- ruary school levy; his fam- ily reunion this summer; And on and on. They talk as if they were old friends meeting again after a long absence. And the truth is they've been married for 26 years. Congrats to the both of you.

Now, let's look closer and examine what makes Carl Carl -- the secrets, those little known facts, mostly from his early life. First, he had to give up playing football his senior year to help out with family expenses. Secretly, he want- ed to become a mechanical engineer and attended col- lege briefly to pursue this dream. Then, too, Carl didn't see his father nor knew of his whereabouts for 22 years. These thoughts propel him -- every day in every way. They helped make his a journeyman machinist at Colgate, the first black there in that position. These thoughts also helped him make friends easily and helped make Carl Carl.

But, the one thing that stands out in Carl is his love for sports -- a real sports fan, we are honoring him today because he may be the only person in Kan- sas City who correctly pred- icted the final two teams in Sunday Super Bowl. He said he knew that New Eng- land would be there. No other Monday Niter voted for the Patriots. But, Carl did. In addition Carl was a founder of the Monday Nite Footballers. He wants the group to do more for the community. Currently, he sees a new maturity in the group, and feels that it is only fair to share our- selves with others.

In issues, he sincerely believes that high school athletes should perform at a "C" level before being allowed to play sports.

And that's Carl Clark -- tough-minded, commun- ity-oriented, and family- centered. We're proud to have you as a part of Mon- day Nite Footballers -- the greatest sports fans anywhere.

It is something to think about.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Ray Crockett knows a lot about cornerbacking. That's why he has earned a spot in the Chiefs' secondary. He came here, showed up well in the pre-season, and now is a quiet force in the Chiefs' respectable defense. He's got cool . . . mainly because he's one of the few current Chiefs that has ever been to the Super Bowl. But, this week, he is more than all that. It's because he knows about the Denver Broncos. After all, up until this year, he was their starting corner. And, our "sometimey" Chiefs will face Denver in Denver's new Mile High Stadium.

Okay, so what's the big deal about Ray Crockett? For one, it will be a reunion with all of his old friends from Denver. More important, he will be covering the likes of Rod Smith, as he has many times in practice. But, this time, it will be for real. With Denver coming off a loss against Grbac-led Baltimore and the Chiefs beating up on Marty's limping Redskins, this game is key for both clubs. Is Denver again beatable? Are the Chiefs finally jelling, after months of Vermeil promises? Is it true that San Diego is emerging as an AFC West force?

Well, all three of these questions will be answered by Sunday evening. The Chiefs' offense looked decent last week. Priest Holmes showed some promise and the receivers showed signs of excellence at times. More important, Trent had a little time to throw and he did well. But, Denver is not Washington, in any shape or form. They never seem to run out of running backs. More important, they have receivers like Mike Sours Southern's Rod Smith that just love to go deep and leap. Thus, the Chiefs will be tested. We, Monday Niters, are split on which team will win. Of course, Ike, Booker, Ed West, and even Carl Clark believe Denver will light up the new stadium and the rest of us . . . Charles Allen, Lloyd, Ollie and me . . . think the Chiefs will win big.

Let's call Ray Crockett. He has a better idea than anybody.

Alicia Keys has a song and a video out, called “You Don't Know My Name”. And that's exactly what some people think about this nameless Super Bowl game, coming up Sunday afternoon. Without a doubt, this game promises to be among the most boring and least-interesting, in the entire 38-year history of the event. More people will be watching for the new commercials and Janet Jackson, rather than the game, itself. And of course, we, Kansas City fans, have a special, non-interest in this game, as well.

First, our beloved Chiefs faltered at the altar. Started out winning nine consecutive games and then, they played like the Chiefs we all know...the best mediocre team in the National Football League. Enough potshots. Actually, we all suffered a letdown, when the Indianapolis Colts kicked or behinds, out at Arrowhead, in early January. Just like they did before. And the Colts victory drained us of our remaining enthusiasm and energy. Yea, the Colts did it to us.

Speaking of Indianapolis, there is another reason why this game will be boring. Even after the Chiefs failed us again, we were hoping that just maybe an African American coach (Dungy of the Colts), would be matched up with an African American quarterback (McNabb of the Eagles). As a tribute to Martin Luther King's 75th birthday anniversary. And when both teams lost in their respective championship game, a low-ebb interest, became even less interesting. Bottom line, this game will have no meaning whatsoever, especially to us African Americans in Kansas City. So, what are we to do, then?

Well, first, take a look at what your Monday Niters have predicted. You will find that almost all the guys, are "feeling it" for New England. That's because of their long, consecutive, homefield-winning streak; and, after all, the Patriots did beat out every other, AFC rival. But, last year, Tampa Bay surprised everyone and convincingly beat Oakland in Super Bowl XXXVII. "Vicissitudes"

This word means the expected changes and variations. The weather and the stock market are examples of vicissitudes. Now, our beloved Chiefs have adopted that same philosophy. They start out strong, scoring many times on the first possession; then they fade a little in the middle; and just plain peter out in the end. So many times lately, we Monday Niters have witnessed that. And because of the Chiefs’ likely outcomes, we spent last Monday night, talking about movies, rather than the Chiefs. While some of us are in denial, we are all disillusioned optimists. Conse-quently, our left-over verve from last year is gone and all we can talk about now is classic, big-screen talking like "Imitation of Life" and "One Eyed Jacks" and "The Godfather" and on and on. For 35 non-stop years, we have ceaselessly talked about the Chiefs and Monday Night Football. And after the New Orleans debacle, we might as well change our interests to movies. For there is not much to talk about these days, when it comes to our Chiefs.

But, wait! Didn’t the Chiefs beat Baltimore, earlier this year, on a Monday night? Yes. And didn't we once beat Buffalo, a Super Bowl contender, here at Arrowhead a decade ago? Well, doggone it. The Chiefs, after many vicissitudes, might just top-ple the high-flying New England Patriots. Maybe. Or else we'll change our name.

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

What does Jesse Jackson have to do with last Sunday's Oakland-Kansas City game? I'll answer that after I assess the Chiefs, now just over the half-way point in the season.

First, the Chiefs survived. Now, let's face it, y'all. A win is a win, no matter how ugly. And Sunday's win against Oakland was not pretty, except for the first and last Kansas City drives. The in-between was horrible, even though it was Trent's first day back.

However, I must say that we could not have done it without Trent. Ed West flat-out confirms that. Further, he feels that Trent's presence won the game, only because Huard could not have shepherded that last drive.

And we all agree that Sunday was the appropriate time to re-insert Trent, even though Huard had done a respectable job. Now, tomorrow will be Trent's real test. He and Kennison will have to be "connected at the hip", for the Chiefs to have half a chance. If we should happen to beat Denver, that will put us in good shape for a playoff spot. We know that Denver is vulnerable because San Diego beat Denver and we beat San Diego. In fact, last Sunday, LaDain-ian ran like crazy. . .just like Jesse did in 1988.

Speaking of Jesse Jackson, he really did have something to do with Sunday's game. But before I tell you, let me reminisce for a minute. Back in 1988, I received a call at the (Samuel) Rodgers Health Center. It was from Fuzzy Thompson, wanting a place to have a rally that featured Jesse Jackson and some farmers from Chillicothe. I agreed but Fuzzy was unsure if it would take place. . .so I kept it a secret from our staff. . .mainly because I did not want to face it, if Jesse failed to come.

Well, Jesse did come and so did all those farmers who were about to lose their family farms. Tractors, thru-sites, and a string of cars paraded right down in the middle of the Wayne Minor projects. And peo-ple came from everywhere, to hear and see of Jesse. And when he donned the back door, he immediately started planning who would talk, when, and at what point he would enter. Details. Details. Right there on the spot. I immediately saw how Jesse operated. . .great organizing skills, clear instructions, which included a tribute to Dr. Rodgers and a "thank you" to me for helping out. All within a ten-minute plan-ning session. Jesse stood tall in my books and one could see why he is important to all of us, especially us sports fans.

On the way out, I mentioned to Jesse that there were no black head coaches in the National Football League. Jesse, who had been a classy running back at Illinois, told me that he would work on that. A few years later, Jesse started meeting with the owners in the NFL, asking them to include one black coach in the final interviews, only when head coaching vacan-cies occurred. The owners agreed and today, some 10 years after, there are seven black coaches, proudly running things at various franchises. As of this date, the illustrious field of head coaches now include Lovie Smith (Chicago), Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati), Tony Dungy (Indianapolis); Dennis Green (Arizona); and Romeo Cremmel (Cleveland).

The remaining two were on the field Sunday, out at Arrowhead. . . Herman Edwards (Kansas City) and Art Shell (Oakland). And that is what Jesse had to do with the game, last Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Thanks, Jesse. You always know what to do and say.

Happy Thanksgiving To All Of Our Readers!

12:00 p.m. KCTV 5

Chatter, Commentary and Conversation

When Paul Booker makes up his mind, nothing can change it. Most of the time, the “Bootheel” native is rather mild-mannered and soft-spoken.

But, on a few items, he is clear and unequivocal. And then I’ll tell you how all this relates to the Chiefs, after we peek in on of Booker’s journey to Kansas City.

At the urging of his wife, Booker attended and graduated from Lincoln University in Jefferson City, way back in the ‘50s. Working at the Capitol building, he actually put himself through school. In fact, it was at Lincoln that he met Dr. Charles Allen, one of his football cronies. Eventually, Booker and his stately wife landed in Kansas City, and started teaching for the Kansas City Mo., School District. Majoring in elementary education, he managed to stay in the classroom for about 10 years. At that point, he made up his mind to request a transfer to a Home-School Coordinator’s position. And, man did he enjoy helping students get glasses, clothing, healthcare and jobs. He loved it and according to school officials, Booker was “one of the best”. He did this job faithfully until he made up his mind to retire in 1996.

Everybody thought of Booker would waste away but he found time to visit around town. He now had time to visit small businesses, like the 6902. In fact, some say the Mayor appointed him to visit all evening establishments and report back to her on how many folks were there. Bottom line, he has made up his mind to enjoy life and that is exactly what he does... every day in every way. And that’s kind of where football comes in.

Usually, Booker is one of the “haters”. However, of late, he has been voting for the Chiefs. In the past, he felt the Chiefs were “losers” but under Herm Edwards, he believes the Chiefs may just make the playoffs, this year. Already the Chiefs are 5-3 and are expecting to be 6-3 by Sunday night. And, against Miami, of Booker is clear that Huard ought to start, even though Trent Green is ready to reclaim his quarterback position. You will remember that Trent was injured in the first game of the season and Huard has gone on to guide the Chiefs pretty doggone good. Booker has made up his mind about this and nothing is going to change it.

That’s just the way the “Apostle Paul” is. You can count on what he says. End of story.

Christmas, Thanksgiving, New Year's Eve was all of the above at Dr. Charles Allen’s spatially modern house this last Monday night. He, along with his wife, Beverly, and their daughter Terry Strother (Jowan) and Rondalyn Graham of Los Angeles. And there were eight of us Monday Nite Footballers, reuniting for the 37th time to watch our favorite pastime. And, man, was it worth it all!

Fun and barbs and smart-ass remarks filled the air. As usually, Ike appointed himself Lord of Attention. And Booker, fresh from a recent hip surgery, actually stayed awake, amid all the hot air. And Greenfield and Carl Clark were so glad to see each other. They argued and cussed incessantly about nothing. And, there was the ever strategic Ed West, breathing a wise crack or two into the useless conversations. And Ollie Hubbard only came to see and taste what the Allen team had cooked.

As usual, the Allen team presented their victuals well. Something old and something new. There was fried chicken, a beautifully scorched Allen potato, decked with three different kinds of cheeses, and a marinated salad. But, the “new thing” this year was a colorful spinach casserole. Everybody tasted, enjoyed and returned to that dish again and again. Just like Charles Allen is a perfectionist at his dentistry, he is equally conscientious when planning, preparing, and presenting his food delicacies.

Like at all reunions, conversations galore. Some about football. Still more about Trent Green. But mostly about the Chiefs. The usual haters, Ike, Booker, and Ollie, predicted that the Chiefs would falter again against Denver. Wishy washy Carl and exploitative Ed jumped on that bandwagon. But the host, and always-loyal Lloyd stuck up for the quarterback-less Chiefs They quietly got what the others missed. It ain't about the quarterback. What's missing is big, old Willie Roaf or someone else of his class. No blocking, no running. No line, no winning. Even a child knows that.

Speaking of children, we had our usual conversation with the Allen's bright and personable grandson, Robert. Taking football pretty serious these days, the rather-muscular 12-year old schmoozed around and was able to re-unite with his elderly football uncles. Quietly, but outward he thinks the Chiefs will win Sunday, against Denver. Further, he thinks the team can make it without old Trent. In fact, some great, second-string quarterbacks have emerged, upon inquiry to the first-teamer. I think Robert Allen is right. So much for reunions.

PRAIRIE VIEW, Texas. -- Prairie View A & M's 92-86 victory over Mississippi Valley State Saturday, Jan. 15, broke a 31-game losing streak by the Panthers in SWAC regular-season contests.

Prior to Saturday's win, Prairie View's last conference victory was a 78-69 win over Alcorn State in Prairie View, Texas on Feb. 26, 1990.

Prairie View's previous last win over a SWAC school altogether was a 99-90 win over Grambling in 1991, but the Panthers were ineligible to compete for the SWAC title that year and none of their games counted in the SWAC standings.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim Nunnelly

COMMENTARY

No Chiefs game this week. No red and white Chiefs merchandise to buy. No more red Fridays. Yes, the fat lady has sung for the final time this 1997 season. But, before we look ahead to the Super Bowl possibilities, let's take one final "look back" at the Denver game and cry just one more time.

I guess Ol' Ike Gardner and hateable Harry Sypert had the last laugh. Both predicted the Chiefs would fold last Sunday and blight any hopes of playing any more this year. Sypert said it was Marty's coaching. And Ike reminded us of the 1995 Indianapolis game two years ago under Steve Bono. Why did we lose?

Some say it was the sticky stuff on Denver's new uniforms or Danan Hughes' penalty and two dropped passes. Others say it was the Elway factor. Or the poor clock management. And, most will blame it on Grbac and the horrible defense.

And my personal opinion is that the pressure of the play-offs and the myth of home field advantage both played a major part, and that's because we did not play our usual "make it happen" game. And, there is no such thing as a home field advantage in championship games. In short, where the game is played means absolutely nothing. And, don't let Carl Peterson fool you about the fans being a part of the game. Baloney! Every team has to play half of its game in hostile territory, and they win any way.

Really, it's all about who plays the best on game day. The team that tackles better; passes better; manages the clock better; runs the ball better; and scores more points will win the game.

But, four teams are still alive in the chase for the Super Bowl trophy on January 25 at San Diego. That's Pittsburgh, San Francisco, Green Bay and of course the Denver Broncos. Here's a little something about each:

Pittsburgh -- One of the NFL doormats that has four Super Bowl Championships behind (74,75,78,79), and, even made it again in 1995 to the now-defunct Dallas Cowboys. What difference two years make, especially with the maturing Kordell Stewart.

San Francisco -- Land of gold-plated quarterbacks. Like Joe Montana and Steve Young. And God-sent receivers like Gene Washington and Jerry Rice. And an all-time, all purpose back, like Roger Craig. All of these add up to an unmatched championship feeling and a respected West Coast offense. They too have been to the Big Dance and won five times (81,84,88,89,94). A dominant force in the NFL.

Green Bay -- Lombarde country where names like Bart Starr, Paul Hornung, Willie Davis, Reggie White, and Brett Favre; the frozen Tunda where cold, ice, snow, sleet and all bad weather are greeted as we greet warms sunny days. Green Bay has been in the play-offs 17 times. The Pack is always back. Look for the Pack to go back to the Super Bowl.

Denver -- In spite of Elway, the Broncos have never won the Super Bowl in four trips. They have a solid running game with Terrell Davis. Two pair of good hands from McCaffrey and Rod Smith. This is Elway's last chance at the big dance. Will he get there? Doubtful, but we will all know on Sunday.

The Monday Niters will make their final guesses for the year then. So long until then. Donna Stewart predicts Denver over Pittsburgh and Denver and Green Bay in the Super Bowl!

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

K.C. FINISHES REGULAR SEASON AT 13-3

[Image of Jim Nunnelly, smiling]

JIM NUNNELLY

The trumpet has sounded the end of the NFL’s 2003 season. And the Chiefs actually survived pretty well, considering the number of Ike-led, Chiefs’ bashers out there. It is as if these Ike-like people expected the Chiefs to go undefeated, just because they got off to a good start. After beating the Chicago Bears on Sunday, the Chiefs finish out at 13-3, not bad for a team that has not been in a playoff game for seven years. But another season emerges, this week. It’s called the playoffs. However, all 32 teams are not a part. . . . only twelve get the chance to go to the next round and the other twenty leave with nothing. And no matter what you think of the much-maligned, Chiefs’ run offense, the Chiefs are the number two-seeded team in the AFC, just behind New England with a 14-2 record. So here’s the wrap-up:

Chiefs’ three losses were all on the road (Cincinnati, Denver, Minnesota)

The Chiefs will not have to play this week, being awarded a bye for their second best, 13-3 record.

The Chiefs will play on January 10 or 11 at Arrowhead against either Indianapolis, Tennessee or Baltimore.

Priest Holmes now holds the all-time NFL record for touchdowns in a single season with 27.

Chiefs will have eight representatives to the Pro Bowl, in February, in Honolulu. Except for Shields, Woods, and Gonzalez, the other five were not drafted by the Chiefs. Green, Hall, Holmes, Richardson, and Roaf were all acquired from other teams.

Chiefs offense ranked number one in scoring, in the entire NFL.

St. Louis is the number two-seeded team in the NFC and they will not play this week, either. An I-70 Super Bowl is highly unlikely (but could happen).

Bottom line, stop hating on the Chiefs. Considering where they have come from, they have done well. In retrospect, it was totally unrealistic to think that we would not lose at least three games. Did you ever think we’d see the day when we depended on the Chiefs’ offense, over the defense? Jason Whitlock had a party for Priest Holmes, making up for his faulty prediction that Holmes would be physically unable to perform this year. Ike’s grandson, Isaiah Gardner, was given a cameo shot on the regional television broadcast, during the Chiefs-Bears game. By the way, our newest member, Bubba Cleaver, had the most correct predictions, this year, at 14. He voted against the Chiefs once, against Denver. Maybe that Bubba is being unduly influenced by that Ike. Now let’s all unite and get behind the Chiefs, as they launch the second half of the NFL season. . . the playoffs. We’ll have Super Bowl-related predictions for you again, on January 9, 2004, right here in this column. . . . . .

Go Chiefs!

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

Oakland Raiders Vs. Kansas City Chiefs

Sunday, Jan. 2, At 12 Noon Arrowhead Stadium

Channel 5 And KCFX 101.1 FM

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

First off, we must all bow and congratulate Ike Gardner on his accurate prediction of last week’s Chiefs-Seattle game. He stood alone in forecasting the undesirable outcome, leaving the Chiefs still in pursuit of the play-offs. While Ike generally goes against the Chiefs, he is no less of a fan. Like all of us, he desperately wants the Chiefs to play like champions. As evidenced last week in Seattle, the Chiefs are an “up and down” team, even though they work hard to correct the apparent mistakes. So, Ike’s lack of blind faith is representative of so many of us. You can’t tell which team will show up.

Thankfully, the Chiefs play well at home. In fact, the only team to beat the Chiefs at Arrowhead was ironically Seattle. Six other teams, including play-off contenders like New England and Detroit, fell to the thunderous crowd at Arrowhead. This is all part of the adventurous odyssey the Chiefs have expe- rienced in this 1999 season. Playing well at times and then playing poorly at other times, leaving the fans with the reserved position when it comes to talking post-season play-offs. Ike, Paul Booker, and Ollie Hubbard would say that when it comes to the big game, the Chiefs always fold, especially down the stretch. They point to the 1995 season, when the Chiefs won the AFC West Division outright with a 13-3 record, only to fall to lowly Indianapolis in the second round game at Arrowhead. So, we are all understandably cautious when this part of the year approaches. Is this year going to be different or will it be like all the others? Will we win or will we fold?

Obviously, the regular Chiefs’ supporters say the Chiefs will win and claim a play-off spot, regardless of what Seattle does. Charles Allen, Carl Clark and Lloyd Greenfield unequivocally believe this scenario. Ike, Paul and turncoat Ed West think that Oakland will win and pour once again the Chiefs are a “once and done” team. Both sides have merit and appeal and evenly divided, 50 percent say yes and 50 percent say no. But the answer lies not in the stars. The answer lies in just how well the Chiefs respond to the hometown crowd and play-off pressure. So far, they have folded countless times. However, most people didn’t think the Chiefs would have a winning season, let alone make the play-offs. For certain, the Chiefs cannot advance to the play-offs unless they beat the Raiders. Ironically, all of us Monday Niters believe the New York Jets will beat Seattle.

Bottom line for me: look for the Chiefs to not only win, but become AFC west champions in the process. They’ll be 10-6 and all alone atop the AFC West and Gunther will be American Conference Coach of the Year.

Here’s what we think:

Charles Booker and Ed West nodded. Carl Clark talked. Ike, riding high on his ability to predict accurately, sat in a chair he thought was his throne. To top off the misery, there was, wo, dull teams playing, Dallas and Washington, neither had won a game. Hey, there's something.

It's not in the unfulfilled passing game. It's not in the once-heralded defense. It's not in the Super Bowl-promised coaching. It's in what Marty Schottenheimer has been trying to tell us, the running game. So, let's not listen to Ol' Ike Gardner, let's put our store in the running game. Let's slash left and right, five to 10 yards at a time.

It just might happen Sunday. Priest Holmes may become the first Chief in history to be named American Conference Player of the Week, twice in one year. Is it possible? I'll ask Robert Lee Allen. He'll know.

When COMBAT programs talk about identifying children who are likely to commit crimes and do drugs, they utilize risk factors. These 'red flags' indicate a high likelihood that trouble lies ahead, especially in doing drugs and committing crimes. Things like failing in school; fighting; dropping out of school; getting pregnant; losing a parent to jail; being a child of a drug using parent and even, being sick, are not to be overlooked. Each of these are bad enough alone but when they are all linked together, it surely leads to drugs and crime. In big words, this is called “cumulative adversity.” And what does this have to do with the Chiefs?

Things just keep happening to the Chiefs that prevent them from winning. Poor clock management. Just reflect on the fact we have no time outs when we need them. Then, there's the dropped passes. Alexander, Thomas, and even Snoop Minnis, have missed some passes right in their hands. Not just a few times but 10 that I can think of in their last three games. And what about our once-heralded defense. Missed tackles and there is no more pass rush in Kansas City. And last but not least, there are those interceptions, most of them coming in the critical final quarter or final minutes. What's the point?

Or shall we say, what is the answer? Well, our once-loved Chiefs are not only suffering from these things individually, they are now facing “cumulative adversity.” And that usually leads to a depression. Which leads to poor play. Which leads to more losses. Which leads to less fan support.

Talk about "cumulative adversity.” We have found the enemy and the enemy is us.

The 17th annual Monday Niters New Year's Eve Party went off this past year with a bundle of contrasts; mixing the traditional with the non-traditional.

First the traditional. As always, the party included food for the guests; beef brisket, ham, three bean salad, potato salad, cole slaw and avocados, all nicely planned.

Then there were the beverages. The finest in the land.

And as before, Ed and Celeste West offered their spacious third floor penthouse for the event. The Monday Niters decorated with the traditional "Happy New Year" sign. The Monday Niters and ladies sported the traditional party-costume hats.

Now the non-traditional. The 100 or so guests were entertained with fine music. Carefully sprinkled alongside the latest sounds were old "45" favorites; "Midnight Train to Georgia" and "The Bus Stop" and "Have You Seen Her" and several others. The music was orchestrated by KCDJ Nate Littlejohn.

At the midnight hour, instead of Auld Lang Syne, the Monday Niters chose to play "Friends" by Dionne Warwick, Stevie Wonder, Elton John and Gladys Knight.

The song was a special tribute to those "special ones" and to those guests who had shared many wonderful hours with the Monday Niters.

Another non-traditional aspect was the age shift. Usually the Monday Niters attract the 30-40 age group. This time there were at least a dozen people who were in their early twenties. They kept the party going until around 4 a.m. Some golden agers "hung on" but the majority went to rest right after they played "Friends."

But, all in all, another fine social event. Others paid big money to celebrate New Year's Eve but there was not another place in town where they shared more fun, food and foolishness. Another "winner" and congrats to all those who made it possible.

K. C. Monday Nite Footballers To Greet St. Louis Counterparts

The Monday Nite foot- ballers will entertain their St. Louis counterpart group for the tenth time this week-end during the Governors Cup game be- tween the Kansas City Chiefs and the St. Louis Cardinals.

Each year, depending on the site of the game, the groups entertain the visitors with gifts, brun- ches, music, football talk, movies, and just plain hospitality. This years theme is “No Jive in 85 - Just Class” indicating that the Kansas City group will have to improve on the ac- tivities of last year's hosts.

This year all events are scheduled for Friday, August 30, Saturday and Sunday morning, Sept. 1. Events include a get ac- quainted mixer, and open- air ceremony, a Saturday morning brunch, a “Co- coon” swimming party, fitness center exercises, scheduled time and the Chiefs Cardinals Game. As has been traditional, there will be surprises sprinkled throughout the week-end.

Approximately 18 Mon- day Nite footballers and guests have made reserva- tions to attend the festivities. Most of the events will take place at the Ramada Inn South- east, I-435 at 87th St. The St. Louis Monday Nite football group is an outgrowth of the group here.

Members of the Kansas City Monday Nite foot- ballers include Charles Allen DDS; C. Artis Ashley: Paul Booker; Tom Brown: and James T. Nunnelly; Jerry Price; and Ed West. The group was formed almost twenty years ago with an aim toward sharing their knowledge about football, it started from a few friends watching TV together. Now each Mon- day Nite the group gathers for sports topics and the week’s major sports events.

Besides the St. Louis Governors Cup Weekend, the group sponsors a Super Bowl Party, a New Year’s Eve party and is involved in many community events (including the Niles Home) and is a frequent contributor to the Kansas City Call Sports page. They have been loyally sup- ported by their wives and others.

Monday Nite Footballers Interscholastic League Predictions By Jim Nunnelly

Saturday, Nov. 2 Lincoln Academy 138 - Northeast 0

The above score was the only game in town last week.

Never before in the history of Interscholastic League football has there been so much talk about one game. Everyone is asking, "Did Coach Price run up the score on winless Northeast?"

The whole issue has cast a dubious cloud on Kansas City high school sports. Many, including national newspapers, have considered the game newsworthy and as a tribute to Lincoln Academy. Others, closer to home, say the score will have a horrible effect on the entire football program.

Before we comment either way, let's look at the details. 1. Lincoln Academy scored 18 times against Northeast. 2. Northeast played 13 freshmen, mainly because veteran players were facing discipline problems. 3. Lincoln Academy played 18 players, mainly because it had to reduce its squad because several players failed to make the required grade point average. 4. Coach Charles Harris, Northeast coach did not feel that Lincoln ran up the score. 5. Partly because of the score and mainly because of the lack of upperclass players, Northeast has decided not to play its last game this week. 6. As a result of the victory, Lincoln Academy is the only team from the League to have a guaranteed spot in the state playoffs. They play Lafayette in St. Joseph on Wednesday. 7. The score attracted the New York Times and USA Today and all the local media. Some calls to the District office voiced grave concern for such a game.

It is really unfortunate that we have to talk so much about this game. Sam Cofield, League District Coordinator, has articulated the concerns well.

Basically, he believes that the score could have been held down. Over the years, we have always held that Cofield is one of the most knowledgeable and influential people in any sports program anywhere. And obviously, anything that threatens the existence of high school sports should be addressed (as Cofield has done.)

But, Monday Night Footballers, look at another side. Lincoln Academy has accomplished a great deed. With only 18 players, they have gained a spot in the state playoffs. More importantly, they did it, representing a school district that requires high and tough academic requirements at the school.

Really, the young men can be proud of their success. We, as longtime football fans, have to congratulate a team that has blended athletic prowess with academic achievement.

While the score can be debated, the fact is we have some real winners at Lincoln Academy.

It is something to think about.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Necessary Progress: College Football Super Bowl?

"Progress begins with the belief that what is necessary is possible."

That phrase was the guiding force that led the NCAA Postseason Committee to approve a national championship game. Before we comment, let's take a quick look back to last January 2.

That was the day when Miami took on Penn State in the ratings-minded Fiesta Bowl. Though mythical, the game was one of the few times "an actual game" had been played to decide the number one team. And of course, college football fans loved it.

Individually and collectively, we in Monday Nite Football, support the subcommittee's position. It is a long, overdue concept. Yes, let there be a game to decide which team is the best. While Ed West cautions us about the season being longer, he still wholly supports the notion.

Moreover, Carl Clark specifies his position by saying that a playoff system be employed to decide who’s even to play for the "final two." And because there was no system in place this last year, Ollie Hubbard thinks the present system (voting) is highly inappropriate. He believes that Oklahoma was really the best team in 1986 and because they lost an early, season game to Miami, they were literally removed from the running.

COMMENTARY

Just why did the postseason committee approve a national championship game this year? Especially since fans like us have hun-gered so long for such a "mini super bowl"? Was it the television revenue, and Lord knows there would be plenty?

Was it to save face after the Fiesta Bowl showed the way? Was it a deep-seated desire to copy after the NFL’s Super Bowl? Or was it just plain progress?

Whatever the reason, the time has come for sportswriters and their "unmitigated arrogance" to step aside and let the best team win, on the field and not with the stroke of a pen. That’s what Ike Gardner says and we all agree.

Obviously, we have always favored progress in sports, especially those changes that are fan-inspired. And it is our unanimous opinion that sport fans everywhere will welcome this progressive step. What took the NCAA so long?

Our hats are off to the postseason subcommittee for believing it is now "necessary" to employ an actual game to decide. More importantly we’re glad they now believe it’s possible.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Calling...Buck Buchanan

Working in your calling is half praying. That saying applies to Buck Buchanan, recently nominated by the Legislature to the Jackson County Sports Authority.

Before we explain that proverb, let's examine why we have to write about Buck.

Part of Chief's Heyday First, Buck is a major part of Kansas City Sports history. He was an all-pro defensive lineman for the Kansas City Chiefs—from the first day they came to Jackson County. He is quiet, unassuming and articulate. And, he's one of the few Chiefs around who relates well to the "fan on the corner" or the fan in the stands.

An Articulate Speaker Secondly, Buck is well known today, a decade or so after his playing fame. He can be heard after each Chief's games, explaining inside information to hungry sports fan. There his articulation comes alive. And, as part of his duties as NFL Alumni youth director, he also speaks to youngsters all over the country about the complex world of sports. And, as a special favor to us in Monday Nite Foot-ball, he spoke to a St. Louis group about his days at Grambling and his special relationship with the winningest coach in college football, Eddie Robinson. His funny seriousness kept the audience spellbound for an hour. So, you can see, he's deep, deep into sports.

In Business Affairs Last, Buck is a true businessman, mainly self-employed. And you guessed it, he makes most of his living in sports. Besides being one of the well-known faces in the famous Miller Lite commercials, he owns his own business. One that will imprint anything on everything, especially sports items such as T-shirts, jackets, golf club, etc. Principally called "All Pro Advertising," his business supplements his promotional contract with Miller brewery.

Commentary So, you see, Buck knows, lives and loves sports. More importantly, he has a child-like excitement about the whole, complicated world of sports. Anywhere there's a sports event, you can find Buck. Further, he has risen to a viable role in the black business community, as president of the Black Chamber. He's clear headed, clean and cautious in his approach to his life in sports. We, as an organized sports fan organization and speaking for the "fan-on-the-street," salute the Jackson County Legislature for nominating Buck to the Sports Authority. In addition, we urge Governor Ashcroft to appoint a man, who is deeply involved in the profession he represents.

Buck truly works in his calling. And he's good at it, too.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Instant Replay System: Fans Win Again

Right up there with the three-point shot, the designated hitter and the photo finish, the approval for an instant replay system in pro-football is a major victory for the fans. The NFL will now allow an eighth official to overrule a questionable play from the press box—something that the Monday Niters have long waited for. Particularly, Ike Gardner is tickled pink.

While the whole world welcomes another electronic addition to pro football, many say that it's just too soon to tell what problems this new system will cause. Others say the game will be delayed unnecessarily. Still others assert that "the man" in the press box is not mistake-free. But, the Monday Niters as lifetime sports fans, have only good things to say about it.

Let's examine the rule and see how it applies. And then we'll comment both individually and collectively.

First off, the "high priest" official will have two televisions to watch—one that homeviewers see and the other that provides different angles of the play. They can only rule on touchdown, out-of-bound and incomplete pass plays.

This eighth official will signal his objection on the play and then overrule the ground official. Also, officials may ask the "high priest" for an opinion on "any" play. However, he must respond to both situations in twenty seconds. Simple enough. Let's comment.

Of course, everyone knows Ike Gardner and the Monday Niters have waited a long time for this system. And like Ed West, we know that the ground officials are human and certainly need help from time to time. Paul Booker quickly points out that the referees are "not God" and they can and do make mistakes. All in all, he believes that the system is a "very positive" move for everyone—the fans, the owners, the coaches and even the ground officials themselves.

Another Monday Niter suggests that the system also is a move for "justice". Ollie Hubbard says it will give the fans in the stadium a chance to see questionable plays—like the home viewer. Openly, Charles Allen and C. Ashley state that it will eliminate the controversy and arguments that always follow a questionable call; it may even reduce the number of "unsportsmanlike conduct" calls.

And there you have it. As you can see, we Monday Niters believe in the instant replay system. And while we are grateful, Carl Clarke reminds us that this replay thing can go too far. We really don't want a "Big Eye" approach that can overrule everything.

The three chosen areas are "just enough" to be effective. We don't want to see another layer of decision making on every decision. It must be "viewed" as just another tool, another aid to right wrongs and keep the game alive and responsive to the fans.

Like all other fan-inspired rule changes, it will be both perilous and painful at times. Starting with the pre season, the rule should prove "very positive" and it shouldn't alter the game that much. And pretty soon, like the three-point play, the designated hitter and the photo finish, we'll accept it as just another rule, to keep us interested in the game we love.

It's something to think about.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Doug Williams: Testing His Limits And Succeeding

To dream anything that you want to dream. That is the beauty of the human mind. To do anything that you want to do. That is the strength of the human will. To trust in yourself, to test your limits. That is the courage to succeed.

Those last two lines... "To trust in yourself, to test your limits. That is the courage to succeed." Could very well become the theme for Doug Williams, the Washington Red skin quarterback, as the starting Redskin field gen eral, Doug could become the first black quarterback to lead a pro team to the all-important Super Bowl. While we will see how the theme applies, let's also see how Doug has tested his limits—three times in his life.

First off, his days at Grambling State univer sity. There, Doug had a fruitful and rewarding col lege football career. His "test" rested in the fact that his older brother had played baseball there be fore him and "little brother Doug" was tested and un afraid, but not knowing quite why. Under the steady, guiding hand of Coach Eddie Robinson, starting in 1974, Doug went on to toss 93 touchdown passes and become a viable candidate for the Heisman Trophy four years later. Not bad for a tall, lanky kid from Zachary, La, (near Baton Rouge). Now, watch Doug go on to take a bigger test.

On college draft day that next April, Doug was se lected by the last place, lowly Tampa Bay Bucca neers. Two battles faced him—being a black NFL quarterback and playing for a cellar-doomed team. To the contrary, Tampa Bay went on to participate in three post season playoffs, all under the rifle like arm of Doug Williams.

After a contract dispute with Tampa Bay, Doug left in 1983 to join the now defunct United States Foot ball League.

When Doug had to look for someplace to play, the Washington Redskins signed him away from the "needy and greedy" Los Angeles Raiders. To date, he is a seven-year veteran in the National Football League and has passed for 84 touchdowns (almost equalling his Grambling output). In addition, he has had eleven 300 yard games and single-handedly resurrected the 1987 Red skins by taking over and pushing them into the playoffs. Testing his lim its—all the way! Go ahead, Doug! Now for the third test.

The final time Doug had to test his limits was also in 1983. This time it was not professional—it was personal. Unexpected and unplanned for his college sweetheart and wife, Janice, passed away from a shocking and surprising brain tumor. That sad event left Doug a widower, with a 3-month old daughter, Ashley. Of all the tests faced by Doug, this was the hardest and the most painful test to experience. With every pass of the football and every passing day he re-members her and contin- ually forces himself to an other and different test— another and different limit.

Commentary

Obviously, we Monday Niters far too well know the Doug Williams story and we have known it for a long time. But, all this past week, after the Wash ington victory over Chi cago, sportscast and sport caster's have offered Doug's name—some in awe, but all in respect. They are finally realizing his contributions to and greatness in the NFL.

We, on the other hand, look at it a bit differently. In fact, Ike Gardner wond ers what Doug would have been like, if he had good receivers—to the likes of Miami or San Francisco. All of us knew that Doug was great, given half a chance. And. Joe Gibbs, the Washington Redskin coach, was an important play in Doug's hard-earned "game of chance."

Once again, this Sunday, Doug will trust in himself to test his limits. And, if his history continues to repeat itself, he will master the courage to succeed and lead his team to and through the Super Bowl, come January 31.

With and without Janice.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Ed West Guest Columnist

Super Bowl Sideline: Past And Present

It was January 15, 1967 and Super Bowl I was about to begin. The Green Bay Packers and the Kansas City Chiefs would battle for the first championship crown. The National Foot- ball League and the Amer- ican Football League had been bitter rivals before both leagues merged to form today's NFL with two conferences.

From game one to this day, some of the most ex- citing action of Super Bowl I had occurred off the field in the days leading up to the gala event. The night before Game I, Max McGee of the Packers violated the great Vince Lombardi's curfew to pay his respects to a blonde he had met the night before. Since he hadn't played for six weeks, he knew there was no chance he was going to play unless Boyd Dowler got hurt. He waddled in about 7:30 in the morning and could barely stand up for the kickoff. On the bench, the injured Paul Hornung asked Max what could he do if he had to play and Max said, "No way, there's no way I could

make it."

They were sitting there discussing Paul's wedding when Lombardi suddenly yelled McGee, "get in the game." Max almost faint- ed. Dowler was hurt and McGee played the rest of the game and caught seven passes and scored two touchdowns. After the game, old Vince came up to Max and said, "Nice game." Max replied, "Most anyone could have done it" to which Vince ans- wered, "I know it."

Television rights to the first Super Bowl game were awarded to the CBS Net- work with 365 outlets and the NBC with 225 outlets. Estimates of the number of television viewers ranged up from 60 million. One-minute commercials sold for $75,000 on NBC and $85,000 on CBS. Members of the winning team received $15,000 each and the losers share was $7,500 per man.

Super Bowls II and III The only exciting thing about Super Bowl II was the rumor circulating that Lombardi would resign as coach of the Packers after

the game. Following a 33- 14 victory over the Raiders, Lombardi did just that.

Super Bowl III was the "Broadway Joe Show." Three days before the game, Joe Namath ap- peared before the Miami touchdown club, tossed a few footballs into the au- dience, jested briefly and then, growing serious an- nounced: "The Jets will win on Sunday, I guarantee it." Such a pronouncement was almost without prece- dent in American sports, especially because the speaker represented a team that was judged an 18 to 23 point underdog against the Powerful Bal- timore Colts. After a 16 to 7 victory, Namath said, "I'm only talking to the New York writers. They're the only ones who believed me."

Chiefs In Superbowl IV For Len Dawson, the 1969 season had been a night- mare. He sat out six games with a knee injury. His father died and when the Chiefs lost their final reg- ular season game to Oak- land, a cloud of controversy swirled about the veteran quarterback because of a questionable game plan. Harassment grew so for- midable that the Dawson children were reluctant to

go to school and Mrs. Daw- son hesitated to mingle so- cially. Dawson thought that nothing more could heap further grief on him and the Chiefs but on Tuesday before Super Bowl IV, news broke that Dawson was im- plicated in a federal sports gambling investigation. Af- ter spending a restless week, the Chiefs pounded out a 23 to 7 victory over the Minnesota Vikings. One representative of the AFL, remembering that the Chiefs had finished second in the Western Division, quipped, "Imagine What one of our first place teams would have won by."

Commentary

From those early begin- ning to the present, the sidelines have produced some exciting stories. The appearance of the first black quarterback in the Super Bowl has to be the most significant news item heading into Super Bowl. Doug Williams will lead the Washington Redskins against John Elway and the Denver Broncos. Based on Jimmy the Greek's anal- ysis of the black athlete's superior breeding, this should be an exciting game on the field. Good luck, Doug. Black America will be pulling for you.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

From Smith To Bias and Rogers: Where To Next?

Remember Lonnie Smith. A couple of years ago, he stated that cocaine was an everyday “treat!” for professional athletes. Easy to get to. Ample supp- liers. That revelation (the Lonnie Smith story) set off a series of sports head- lines that would cast doubt, shadow, and shame on our sports world.

Dozens of pro athletes made headlines. “Star Play- er Busted for Cocaine” or “Star Player fails Drug Testing.”

My, at times, it appeared that a new Olympic sport had been created—coke- ball. But, thank God it was all arrests and suspensions.

But in the last month, Len Bias and Don Rogers came to pay the ultimate price for cocaine. These healthy, undeveloped su- perstars fell victim to the fine white powder. Prior to that, we Monday Niters had not commented on the drug problem in sports. But we can no longer stand aside and remain silent. Let’s look at all this and see what we can say ‘to clarify the issues. What are the solutions?

Well, first the drug test for the athletes.

Without a doubt, Paul Booker and Jerry Price support drug testing for athletes, especially the pros. Though an invasion into privacy, the drug test will at least send a strong

message to young, aspiring athletes that drugs are not condoned and will not be tolerated in the pro leagues.

And Carl Clark goes furth- er. He says we’d better start checking the youngs- ters, too. He reminds us that every child, seven or eight, is a potential “coke” victim. No one, but no one, should be exempted.

The message to youngs- ters would appear and re- sound even stronger. Ob- viously, Coach Jerry Price would be pleased for this approach would not single out only the athletes, but would include all students. Ollie Hubbard would also support this “early detec- tion” approach.

Does it sound like we’re searching for answers? Well, we are and some go beyond the classroom. Like Ed West feels, we should go after the suppliers, some- body, somewhere is making a lot of money preying on these successful, talented and rich young athletes.

Where are they? Is this enough? When and where do we start? Who should be first or second? Should managers, nurses, law- yers, bookkeepers be drug tested? What should the parent’s role be? The Coach’s role? The prin- cipal’s role? The commun- ity’s role?

As you can see, we don’t know all the answers. We don’t even know all the

questions. We sure don’t know where to go next. We only know that athletes should be tested and maybe education ought to start way down in grade school. Further, we don’t know how or when or even if it will work at all. But let’s talk more about this timely subject for a while, ask more questions and see what solutions may devel- op.

Where to next? Members of the Monday Niters are; Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clarke, Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West.

AND HE SCORES . . . Hopeful and often is this thought for the Chiefs inconsis- tent offense as they travel to play the N.Y. Jets, Sunday, at the Meadowlands.
