Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Isaac Gardner Jr. ...................................................

A Testimony of Courage

Usually this column discloses the views of ten men who rarely agree on anything concerning sports. The ladies of the Monday Night Footballers infrequently get the credit and recognition they fully deserve. The following episode is a testimony of the unquestioned courage of one of the Monday Night Ladies.

Late last Thursday afternoon, Mrs. Janice Nunnelly, known to the group as Grun, received a call from her bank. The caller indicated there was a lady at the bank trying to cash a check, claiming that she was Janice Nunnelly. Grun rushed to the bank, "faster than a speeding bullet." The imposter was being detained by bank officials, still maintaining that she was Janice Nunnelly after Grun brought forth a veritable plethora of identification, clearly establishing her identity. Meanwhile, the imposter feigned thirst, pretended to go to the water fountain, but then fled through the door. Grun, observing the get away, followed the imposter in hot pursuit.

The imposter made it to her car, but Grun, the Swift, was upon her as she started the car. As the car was pulling off, Grun reached through the door and grabbed the imposter. While the car was in motion, Grun, with the grace of a ballet dancer, wrapped her legs around the mirror of the car. The car sped down Prospect with Grun persistently holding on to the neck of the imposter. After a sojourn of seven blocks, Grun finally decided that discretion was the better part of valor and managed to disengage herself from the car and the imposter's neck. Grun escaped with minor scratches.

Grun, now more persistent than ever, searched the neighborhood on foot. With Grun's information, the police apprehended the imposter, who is presently incarcerated.

We constantly hear that members of our community do not want to get involved. The incident described in this article clearly belies that notion as a generality. There may be some who would question Grun's judgement in pursuing the imposter. However, no one would question her courage. The Monday Night Footballers are proud to have her as a member, and I feel honored to be in a privileged few who have her as a personal friend.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

Brian Tobey: Full of Roots

By Jim Nunnelly When Alex Haley wrote Roots, he was telling us that the farther we can trace our family tree, the better our chances are be- coming successful.

And, when the Kansas City Chiefs drafted Brian Tobey is round eight on Monday, they didn't realize that Tobey had something extra. Let's see how his chances for a successful pro careeer are enhanced as a result of his many “roots.”

First, Brian Tobey al- ways wanted to play pro football. All of his 23 years and 250 pounds have been directed toward this very practical dream-of one day playing in the National Foot- ball League.

According to his father, Lawrence, Brian did ev- erything conducive to as- suring himself a chance to play-by training hard, eating well, and choosing the right “mentor.” That leads us to the second set of “roots.”

Served Under Eddie Robinson Brian chose to play col- lege football at Grambling, under legendary coach Ed- die Robinson. With his jun- ior college All-American status, Brian could have gone to Syracuse or Iowa or even Clemson. But, the strict rules of conduct, the simple offer of a challenge, and a good education, at- tracted Brian to Louisiana. And, Brian met the chal- lenge. Here’s his report card under Robinson.

Third in the league with 769 yards rushing. Six touch- downs. Ten receptions. Se- lected second team All Southwest Athletic Confer- ence. Only Lewis Tillmon of Jackson State, who shat- tered all of Walter Payton’s rushing records was ahead of him. And, Brian achieved all of this in spite of the pass-oriented offense at Grambling.

Grambling’s athletic of- ficial, Stanley Lewis, went on to call Brian a “classy young man”-not only be- cause of his willingness to block, but also because of his outstanding char- acter and his achieve- ments.

Let the record show, Bri- an is hardly a typical black athlete entering the pro realm. He has all the family support he needs.

Outstanding Heritage Brian Tobey is of African and Indian heritage. And, his family can trace their roots, back to Cape Verdan, West Africa. To go deper, Brian's family can claim another distinction, the fam- ily business.

Opening in 1865, his fam- ily’s sanitation business is the oldest, longest-run- ning, black business in Hyannis, Massachusetts. And, maybe, even, in the entire country. That busi- ness has been operating for nine generations and has always been led by a Tobey.

And, this very successful business has withstood all business fluctuations and temporary temptations to sell. Even severe financial emergencies were some- how overcome. Employing 21 people, this business has endured, when many others have failed. And, whatever else Brian may become in the NFL, he is assured that he has another option. Sort of a prepared place. He can always run the family business. All because of “these roots.”

Final Commentary

So, you can see Brian Tobey is just “full of roots.” He comes to the Chiefs, with a good, playing record, a comprehensive Robinson- education, and a solid fam- ily background. He’s drug- free, clean-cut, and goal- oriented. Because of all these roots, Brian Tobey will achieve and become successful in Kansas City. Look out Palmer and Okoye!

Though he dosen't have the publicity or visibility of a Rodney Peete or a Derrick Thomas, he will be a major force in pro football here. We predict immediate success.

Alex Haley should meet Brian Tobey, another “roots” case history.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Lynette Woodard, The First Woman Globetrotter

While it may seem strange that the Monday Nite Footballers would be writing about a basketball player right in the middle of the Super Bowl Playoffs, our interests in athletics and academics is well documented and Lynette Woodard exemplifies one of our key ideals, to assure that athletes also do well in school.

On January 11, the Globetrotters brought their 60-year-old act to Kansas City. Having performed in 10 countries before Kings, Presidents, Shahs and just everyday folks, there was something different this year.

There was Lynette Woodard, the first woman Globetrotter. But there is something real special about Lynette that interests Monday Nite Footballers.

But first, let's look at her career, her performance at Kemper and then we will look at what's "special" about her.

Everyone knows that Lynette Woodard was an All-American at the University of Kansas. There, she scored an amazing 3649 points, surpassing both Wilt Chamberlain and Jo Jo White. She was the captain of the 1984 American Olympic team that won the Gold Medal.

Apparently a diligent community worker, she was honored as NAACP "Woman of the Year" by her native city, Wichita, Kas. She has actively been involved in the American Cancer Society and the Big Brothers-Big Sister program. It was 1984 at the Charlotte, N.C. Globetrotter camp, that she achieved history. She was chosen to be the Globetrotters "Sweet Georgia Brown." Affectionately referred to as "Wood," "Little Sister" and "Baby Girl," what court contribution does Ms. Woodard make to the Globetrotters? In Kansas City before a near sellout crowd, she made four layups, blocked one shot, and performed a 6-minute, magic pass routine with teammate—Sweet Lou Dunbar. Not bad for the lightest, shortest, and prettiest Globetrotter. All good but there is a lot more to Lynette.

You see, Ms. Woodard is also a star athlete who has done well in the classroom. Of all her many honors and media attention, seldom is it mentioned that Lynette Woodard was an Academic All American at Kansas University. That means she maintained a B-plus average in college while being a starter in a major sport.

Every young lady and young man in the world should know this fact about Lynette. Unfortunately, most of us will only know that she played at Kemper Arena and she is the attractive star of the Globetrotter television commercial.

We Monday Nite Footballers, honor Lynette Woodard for she has demonstrated that you can do well both on the court and in the classroom. In an exclusive interview with us, she simply gave this advice to young, aspiring athletes. "Get your education first, and everything else will fall into place."

It is something to think about.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim "Grand Dad" Nunnelly

Kansas City Chiefs (6-9) vs. Oakland Raiders (8-7) Saturday, Dec. 26, at 3 P.M. Oakland Coliseum

KCTV-Channel 5 And KCFX-FM 101

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

We'll all be home for New Year's. That's the cry for our Kansas City Chiefs, once thought to be the AFC's entry to the Super Bowl next month. Well, it didn't happen and the once 13-3 Chiefs are now 6-9, and may even go to 6-10, i.e. if Oakland overtakes the Chiefs Saturday.

What happened, you may ask, especially in the last half of the year. The only thing that did not happen negative to the Chiefs was there was no drug-related incidents. And, we who are associated with combating illegal substances are eternally grateful for that. But, there were many other football aspects to ponder and talk about. Here are a few:

First, the Chiefs wavered every week about who the starting quarterback would be. That took away from the stability every winning team must have.

Cornerback Mark McMillian, size notwithstanding, became every passers' target. His 5'7", 145-pound stature invited opposing quarterbacks to pick on him. They did again and again. We never fixed this glaring gap.

Until Bam Morris, we never established a running back. Donnell Bennett was chosen and that was it. The last time you heard Kansas City mentioned as the home of a first-class running back was way back in 1967. Remember Mike Garrett. No running, no playoffs.

Kansas City literally set Derrick Thomas up for failure, by making him a "falcon" linebacker (a down lineman). Because of his small size, big 300-pound linemen just tossed him around. Speed is good but size is still a major factor in sports. Derrick made All-Pro as a linebacker, utilizing the quarterback. We ruined his effectiveness.

Then, too, there were other things off the field that distracted the Chiefs as well. I'm not sure how much all of this affected the play. I just know since the October 4 rainy win over Seattle, the Chiefs have spiraled downward and outward. Whatever the reasons, we all will be watching and pulling for Minnesota, Denver, Miami, San Francisco and Atlanta. The Chiefs will take their place as one of the 18 NFL teams whose season will end this week.

For the rest, the second season starts in January.

Predicting Since 1970

Charles Allen (9) Chiefs by 3 Chiefs will take a bite out of Bengals

Bubba Cleaver (8)

Lloyd Greenfield (9) Chiefs by 3 All is well that ends well

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Cincinatti Bengals

12:00 p.m. KCTV 5

“Forks In The Road”

Obviously, this game is it! The Chiefs have come to the fork in the road…either they win or we will all turn our attention and support to Tony Dungy (Indianapolis) and Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati). But if Detroit should happen to beat Pittsburg and Denver wipes out San Diego and the Chiefs beat up on Cincinnati…. then, the Chiefs will make the playoffs. Folks, it is that simple. And complicating matters is the hovering speculation that Vermeil will retire…one way or the other. Clearly, Vermeil is at the fork in the road, too. Win and stay in! Lose and sign up for Medicare! Folks, it is that simple.

And we, Monday Niters, are facing another kind fork in the road. Monday Night Football, as we know it, is over. ABC will no longer broadcast the perennial ratings-winner. In fact, we all said our goodbyes to Al Michaels and John Madden and reminisced about all the history-making plays witnessed on Monday night television, everywhere. In 1970, we were all younger, quicker, and immature. Now we are older, slower and fall asleep, with no warning. It takes us longer to do EVERYTHING, including selecting our teams. Some of us even have to ask “who’s playing”. The fork in the road for us is that we are just getting old and things are changing, with or without our support. But some things will never change….Ike, Ollie and that Booker will always be haters of the Chiefs. Cleaver, Carl, and Ed West will go with the wind. And faithful, fine people like Lloyd Greenfield and imminent Dr. Allen, will always support the Chiefs, just like on New Year’s Day against North Division champions, Cincinnati. We know the Chiefs will win by at least ten and Vermeil stays in. We never have been at that fork in the road!

Like each of you, we will have to switch the channel over to ESPN and carry on as usual. Booker, Ollie and Ike will continue to hate the Chiefs. Let's take a glimpse of what's been happening over the past two weeks in sports. Everybody knows that Michael Jordan is back in the NBA. Wonder how much they are really paying "Medicare Michael" to put his 38-year-old body back out there on the hardwood. And we all have been crying good tears for the Mighty Yankees were shut down. And by the unexpected, youthful Arizona Diamondbacks. And Arizona did it to the Yankees just like the Yankees did it to them . . . in the bottom of the ninth inning. And what about those University of Missouri Tigers? Kareem Rush is on the front cover of Street and Smith magazine. And the national coaches' poll ranks the Tigers eighth, behind fifth-ranked Kansas. And there is only one senior on this year's squad . . . that's Clarence Gilbert. He and Rush will team up with Paulding, Johnson, Gage, and some new freshmen. Perhaps this just might be the year that Mizzou goes forth.

Speaking of going forth, how about those Chiefs, last Sunday, against the San Diego Chargers! Ike and his football Taliban predicted that San Diego would just beat our Chiefs to a pulp. Didn't happen. And, while the Chiefs almost gave the game away, Kansas City even held on in the waning moments, to preserve the victory. It was not like in weeks past, when ol' Spent Trent would throw an interception. Or there wouldn't be enough time outs. Or the special teams would allow a big touchdown to turn the game around. It was as if the Chiefs were managing the passing and running game, effectively.

That word, running. You will remember that Priest Holmes has been showing out lately. In fact, against San Diego's high-ranked run defense, he skipped, dipped, and ripped through Junior Seau and his friends as if he had called "psychic hotline" on them. Scampering 181 rushing yards, Priestly Priest has helped us restore faith in the running game. "Trent Bono" can't pass. But Priest Holmes can run. That is the same thing Marty Schottenheimer has been telling us. I can see ol' Marty standing over there on the sidelines, calling repeatedly for the run. Maybe his "ghost" was still there.

And that's just what apparition means. It means a ghost or an unexpected appear-ance. With Marty now winning three straight at Washington, we should be glad to have ol' Marty around . . . even if it's just his apparition. He apparently know what's best. A running game in hand is worth a St. Louis-style passing game in the bush.

Green Bay Packers, NFC Champions vs. Denver Broncos, AFC Champions

Qualcomm Stadium, San Diego, Cal. Sunday, Jan. 25, At 5 P.M. KSHB-TV, Channel 41

John Elway: Take A Bow On Super Bowl Sunday

John Elway is after all used to being on center stage and at the center of attention. That's understandable. He was the first athlete picked in the 1983 draft. He passed up a chance to play baseball for the Yankees. For starters he's brought Denver back to 40 some victories after being behind in the fourth quarter. All that and he's never won a Super Bowl and time is running out on the 15-year All-Pro quarterback.

Before we review Elway's chances for victory in Sunday's Super Bowl game against Green Bay, let's look back and see how "other" quarterbacks have upstaged Elway in past Super Bowl games.

First off, there was Phil Simms at Super Bowl XXI... when the New York Giants quarterback completed 22 of 25 passes that afternoon in Pasadena. Then in Super Bowl XXIV in 1990, Joe Montana passed five touchdowns, helping the 49ers to a 55-10 trouncing of the Broncos. There's more.

But, it was the 1988 Super Bowl that "Big John" will vividly remember. That was "Doug Day afternoon." Quarterback Doug Williams will put on a record setting passing clinic for Elway and African Americans everywhere. Being the first black quarterback to start a Super Bowl, Williams threw four touchdown passes in the second quarter, one for 80 yards to Ricky Sanders. And when it was all over, the Washington Redskins had dwarfed Elway and the Broncos 42-10. Grambling's favorite son and now coach was selected the game's Most Valuable Player, again upstaging one-way Elway. One more time.

But, like many of his comeback victories, Elway has another chance this Sunday. In fact, Ollie Hubbard, Paul Booker, and hate nable Harry Sypert believe it's Denver's time this year. Some others are just loyal to the American Football Conference, who hasn't produced a winning Super Bowl since Marcus Allen starred for the Los Angeles Raiders, way back in Super Bowl XVIII.

Will John Elway once again be the "other" quarterback, taking a back seat to Green Bay's Brett Favre? Carl Clark puts it best when he says Elway has a "cast of supporting characters" and that makes Elway a star and a winner this time.

In years past, he didn't have a Shannon Sharpe, or a running back like Terrell Davis, who on any given day can equal Barry Sanders. And, most of all, he didn't have a young, big-play, wide receiver like Missouri Southerner's Rod Smith. Mix that in with defensive giant Neil Smith and Keith Traylor and you will see clearly why John Elway will get the last bow on Sunday. San Diego will not be able to hold all the hoopla when the Broncos package the Packers. And Dear John will be on the shoulders of his team mates. The TV cameras will be all in his face. He would have done it, won the "big one." And he'll take a bow, right out in the middle of field, where 134 million people will clap and stand up.

Take a bow, John. This Bud's for you.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly

Super Bowl XXXIV

Tennessee Titans vs. St. Louis Rams

Georgia Dome In Atlanta, 5:18 P.M., Sunday

KMBC-TV, Channel 9

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Being Super Bowl week, these last few days surely have been eventful. Not only did we get some sad news about Derrick Thomas, we have not fully recovered from the last minute, last quarter incomplete pass call against our beloved Tampa Bay Bucs. That was in the championship game at St. Louis, and we are left wondering why there was even an instant replay review in the first place. That one call erased Tony Dungy's chances of taking his team to the Super Bowl this Sunday in Atlanta. Really, it should be Tampa Bay vs. Tennessee rather than St. Louis vs. Tennessee.

Any way, let's look at the lucky St. Louis Rams. For us in Kansas City, this will be as close to a hometown as we will get. However, because of reasons we'll explain later, most of us will not be pulling for the Rams. By now, we all know just how talent-loaded this "arch-city" team really is. Even if one forgets about Kurt Warner and his 40-plus touchdown passes this season, you still have to contend with wide receiver Isaac Bruce. As if that's not enough, one has to also consider the venerable Marshall Faulk, formerly of the Indianapolis Colts. Now, there's a difference maker...solid runner, great pass receiver and steady four quarters. While the Rams are generally known for their overpowering offense, they are just as respectable on defense. Thus, they will be task-ready for the Tennessee Titans. Let's now turn to the team, formerly known as the Houston Oilers.

Certainly not as powerful on offense as St. Louis, the Titans do have a franchise player named Steve “Air” McNair. A rifle-armed passer, he was good at Alcorn A.M.&N, and he's been great in the National Football League. Paul Booker is betting on him to win the game, just like ol' Doug Williams did back in the eighties Super Bowl. On that day, MVP Doug Williams did everything for Washington. He completed so many passes that the records still stand. That includes John Elway's last stand two years ago. Thus, Doug and Steve McNair are the reasons why most African Americans nationwide will be rooting for Tennessee on Sunday. It will be about ethnic pride that comes with playing in a championship game, with the eyes of the entire world watching you perform.

For us, Tennessee is the best team that has worked the hardest and overcome the most adversity to make it to the top spot. Not just the politics of playing an African American quarterback, but in moving a struggling franchise in a big city (Houston) to a thriving powerhouse in a small city (Nashville). And taking a young team and wielding it into a contender in a very short time. You see, all of this mirrors the migratory struggles that so many of us have faced and thus, we can identify largely with Tennessee.

So, at 5 p.m. Sunday in Atlanta, look for the Titans to be on top all the way. Here's what we think:

You all don't know Robert Lee Allen. He's 7 years old and he attends elementary school out in Martin City. Young, gifted and black, young Allen sums it up quite adequately when he says, "The Chiefs are good. . . sometimes." From the mouth of babes flows the truth.

Fresh from a surprising victory over the lowly, winless Washington Redskins the week before, our Chiefs fell hard to Denver in their new stadium. Denver ran through our run defense as if they had stolen the Chiefs' playbook. And Trent Green threw well, except, he threw it four times to Denver's record-setting cornerback. And, Priest Holmes, was lost without the blocking of Tony Richardson. All in all, the Chiefs made young Allen sound awfully good because their overall performance against Denver, was at best, "sometimey." What adjustments need to be made against the Pittsburgh Steelers?

Well, first off, the run defense needs to do double time. That's because Jerome Bettis, fresh off of attaining 10,000 yards, will test them to the max. Secondly, Trent and his receivers must spend time, just throwing and catching the ball. There is a severe loss of fundamentals in the passing game. And, it has to be fixed before the Chiefs can win another game. Thirdly, the special teams smell. Like the offense, there is something missing. . . blocking, running, just plain aggressiveness. And all of this must be in place before Sunday. That is, if we are to become 2-3.

A record of 2-3 or 1-4, there is not much difference, especially if we don't fix the aforementioned problems. They won't go away. And young Allen will be watching and wondering if Sunday will be the day when the Chiefs get on the win side of "sometimey."

We ought to pay attention to this 7-year-old. So should Dick Vermeil.

I’ve been talking to Robert Lee Allen. You will remember him because he is the very bright, 7-year-old grandson of Charles and Beverly Allen. And, occasionally, he attends our Monday Nite football meetings. Robert seems to think the Chiefs are really a good team, even though they have not been winning. At first, this seems like a paradox. . . good, but not winning. Let’s explore this wisdom, coming from this talented, Martin City student.

First, it is true that Chiefs have some talented players. They don’t come any better than Tony Gonzales. Robert agrees. And Priest Holmes is showing signs of becoming the next Barry Sanders. Robert agrees with that, too. And, no one is quite sure why Vermeil keeps talking about the mediocre play of the receivers. For the most part, they are ok . . . at least most of the time. So, what’s wrong? Why aren’t these Chiefs winning?

It’s really very simple. It’s not the defense, even though they are not playing optimal-ly. Not the special teams, even though they played only one good game (Washington). Well, what is it? It is simply Vermeil’s refusal to admit that “Bent-Trent” IS the prob-lem. The man leads the NFL in interceptions; has lost four games in the final minutes; and will invariably hold on to the football as if he is trying to get sacked. God only knows why Vermeil holds on to what Ike calls the team’s “weakest link.” But, that’s why Robert Lee Allen is saying that the Chiefs are a good team . . . without Trent. Will we win?

Definitely. Those Chiefs are going to win Sunday. Not because Trent will play bet-ter. And, like another youngster told me . . . you can’t sell tickets to a circus that’s got sick elephants. Number 10 is one of ‘em. From the mouth of babes!!!

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

There are two important similarities to the two teams in the 2003 Super Bowl. First, they both proudly wave Pirates' flags, one calling themselves the Buccaneers and the other known respectfully as the Raiders. Then, too, each share the influence of a common head coach, Jon Gruden. There, the similarities cease. On the other hand, the Raiders sport the NFL’s Most Valuable Player in quarterback, Rich Gannon. On the other hand, the Bucs have Warren Sapp, arguably the best defensive lineman to ever play the game in recent times. The Raiders will place their receiving hopes in their Medicare acquisition, Jerry Rice. And the Bucs will put their trust in Deion-like, Keyshawn Johnson. All of this makes for a competitive and entertaining Super Bowl, assuring that each team has a reasonable chance to win. Of course, most Monday Niters believe in Oakland. That's because they are 'Raiders worshipers,' never mind go, of the fact that the Chiefs let this rugged individualist star get away. Plus, they feel some faulty pride in that the AFC West produced the most competitive division in football. Nevertheless, the Bucs have a coaching genius in Jon Gruden, and it is my belief that this "coaching factor" is a real key. While some may rightfully argue that the coach will not wear a uniform, leaving player performance as the most important outcome factor on Sunday. But, any respectable football fan, knows that in close games, the coaching effect reigns supreme. Things like clock management, blitz-timing, and playcalling are all decisions directly influenced by the head coaches. So, given that this will be one of the closest Super Bowl games, Jon Gruden becomes the "difference maker" out in San Diego, giving the Bucs the vital edge and their first championship ever.

In order to obtain relief from pressure, individuals and organizations sometimes do real badly at first. Then, when they improve slightly, everyone thinks of the improvement and forgets the horrible start. Sound familiar? I never thought of Herm Edwards would dip that low.

Whatever the rationale, Of Herm finds himself trying to explain why the Chiefs are off to another bad start. I can tell you that Chiefs fans are not going to take another "dumbing-down" ruse (trickery).

Face it, y'all. The Chiefs looked like a "rag-tag" team out there against Pittsburgh. From start to finish and all points in between, the Chiefs just played to the lowest level ever. The corners didn't cover. The defensive line didn't tackle. The running backs didn't run. And the offensive line didn't block. The only positive thing that emerged last Sunday was the punter. That boy, "Croquette" boomed that ball 70-plus yards. The only bright spot on an otherwise horrible display of Kansas City football. But let's not get sentimental.

Without regard to Herm’s motivational explanation, it still boils down to the Chiefs not playing. Period. Now, he can play that "dumbing-down" card, if he wants. But we old fans know that game. The message is simple: Win against San Diego Sunday or get out of the way!"

ABOUT THE CHIEFS -- In the last seven games, Kansas City has sacked the quarterback 33 times and forced 13 turnovers. Still, the Chiefs defense has not yielded a touchdown in the second half for 10 consecutive games. The Chiefs played the Saints last in 1994 and beat them 30-17.

ABOUT THE NEW ORLEANS SAINTS -- The Saints have been victorious in four of their last six games, beating Oakland, Seattle, Carolina and Arizona. The Saints will start their fourth quarterback, Billy Joe Hobert.

GANNON OR GRBAC? -- The moment of truth is here. Do you dance with one that brung ya? What if they both dance and have brought you forward? Grbac, before he was injured, won seven games, losing only to Denver and Miami. Gannon took over in November and won five games, losing only to Jacksonville. Neither is great, but with a defense full of stars, they are operating well above mediocre. And, we are the AFC West Champions...with two able quarterbacks.

Point is, what do you do when you have to make a choice between two healthy quarterbacks. We discussed this whole thing at length last Monday over at Paul Booker's house. After we feasted, first on Carol's cornbread and her famous friend chicken wings, the following thoughts on the subject emerged:

Schottenheimer is hard headed, he won't do the right thing.

Grbac needs to be brought along gently.

If it ain't broke, don't fix it.

The rhythm of the team is with Gannon.

Gannon is a backup and now must take a back seat.

Plain and simple the Monday Niters think that Rick Gannon should start. He's the leader; he's got the team in rhythm, and he's won against tough teams like San Francisco and Denver. He's done all the right things. BUT...Marty will probably return the job to Elvis Grbac. Carl Clark says backup quarterbacks are still backup quarterbacks. Don't look for Marty to elevate Gannon. Once a backup quarterback, always a backup quarterback.

Commentary, Conversation and Chatter

The Derrick vs. Neil Friendship

This game on Sunday is not about the Chiefs and their many new faces. And it’s not about the Chiefs’ flimsy, questionable offensive line.

It’s not even about Elway and his 26-touchdown pass season last year. Neither is it about Shannon Sharpe, Denver’s All-Pro tight end, who caught 80 of Elway’s bullets for 10 scores.

This game is about the special friendship Derrick Thomas and Neil Smith shared before the salary cap reality set in.

It all started about a decade ago, when Neil Smith showed up from Nebraska and Derrick Thomas came in from Alabama. One a tough, defensive lineman and the other a quick-of-foot linebacker. They didn’t know each other and they only would have the red and white uniform in common. But, they lined up together, talked a lot and went on to become the most feared sack duo in the entire National Football League, racking up 182 sacks together. Most of all, they became real good friends, even making a famous telephone long distance commercial together. Powerfully protective, and inseparable friendship, on and off the football field.

That is until the divisive, third party, impersonal salary cap entered. Last year, Derrick asked for and received a big contract from the front office. He even lobbied for his partner in crime, Neil Smith. The front office said no and sent Neil off to Denver to play for the Broncos. In the process, a wall had been built between the superstars.

But come this Sunday, that wall will come tumbling down. Derrick and Neil will line up on different sides, kind of like brother against brother. And for the first time in their starlike, professional careers. Though they won’t be on the field at the same time, they will battle each other in a different way.

Derrick will be chasing Denver quarterback John Elway. And Neil will be giving untested and untried Elvis Grbac a fit. Each watching and respecting each other. In spite of other buildups, this game will be about how many times Derrick gets to Elway and how many times Neil gets to Grbac. And they’ll be counting, too. It’ll be the “falcon” defense put on by Derrick against the post-sack homerun display, coined by Neil Smith when he was here in Kansas City.

And after the game, they’ll meet and greet at midfield. They’ll laugh, joke and talk and even hug. You’ll see Neil stretch out one of those long arms and you’ll see Derrick with that innocent, disarming smile. And we will all know that in spite of salary caps and short sighted front offices, that the Derrick versus Neil friendship is still intact.

Greg finally gets his chance after waiting for Marcus to break the touchdown record. Now, Greg will step in and make the fans forget Marcus. He will add speed, quickness, and a lot of yards to a rather dull and questionable backfield.

Of course, Kimble Anders will play his usual quiet but effective game. You can look for him to lead Greg Hill to his first 1,000-yard season since being at Texas. Speaking of blocking, that brings up the rebuilt offensive line. Grunhard and Shields will be okay. But, don't look for a lot from Szott and Jenkins. In short, the blocking will be better in the backfield. However, should this group jell, Hill could even sprint 1,400 yards.

The receiver corps is strong individually. Andre Rison and Brett Perriman will help us over the middle. And the aging Lake Dawson seems ready to play. Certainly, first round pick, Tony Gonzalez is a sure bet for the Rookie of the Year in the AFC. However, good this group may be, we still have to consider the King Grbac and how good his arm really is. Fans, we have the receivers...but do we have the passer and the blockers?

So, what does it all mean? Last year, ol' Ike Gardner was right when he said the highly rated Super Bowl picked Chiefs would go only 9-7. This year, Ike says they will win only seven and lose nine. Paul Booker, mayor of the 6902, thinks the Chiefs will settle in at 8-8. Don't pay any attention to optimists like Carl Clark and Dr. Charles Allen. The Chiefs will be lucky to escape a losing season, let alone qualify for the playoffs. In short, this is not the year that the Chiefs' sundial comes out of the shade. Do like I am going to do...wait till next year.

GLOSSARY OF FOOTBALL TERMINOLOGY

As the high school, college and professional football seasons start, there will be terms being used that may be unfamiliar to you. While the list is not exhaustive, it does include some you will hear used by commentators and officials. We, Monday Niters, want to start you off with the right stuff:

AUDIBLE -- This is a change of play, shouted out by the quarterback at the line of scrimmage. Do not confuse this with the usual calling of the signals. BLITZ -- This is a pass rush conducted by the defensive backfield. Since the line usually rushes, it is considered an expected surprise. RED ZONE -- The area inside the 20-yard line. In short, if the offense moves the ball to the red zone, they are expected to score a touchdown. SACK -- When the quarterback is tackled behind the line of scrimmage, while attempting to pass. SHOTGUN -- An offensive formation where the quarterback lines up five to seven yards behind the line of scrimmage, usually to pass.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

College Basketball's Three Pointer: Change And Motion

Well, 1986 will be remem- bered as the "year of the change in sports." The Gardner and the Monday Niters saw the instant re- play become a reality in pro football. And now from Dallas, the NCAA has al- lowed a three-point play in college basketball. What about this change, or is it just movement for motion's sake. Let's examine the potential effects.

First off and most im- portant, it will put the 6- footer back in the college basketball game. Conser- vative coaches, like Norm Stewart of Missouri, will have to "move" the game from the "paint" to the "arch." Outside sharp- shooters can now have their territorial markings on the court. Fans like Tom Brown welcome this aspect. But let's look closer at college recruiting and how that may change.

Well, those same college coaches who go from sea to sea to recruit the seven footer, the six niner, will now "blend and spend" time paying some attention to the lowly high school guards—those that just han- dle the ball well and pop a few from the top of the key.

And, really the 19-foot, 9-inch distance from the center of the basket is just not that far. And of course, the motion of the game will change too. And here's where only the up close fans (like Monday Nites) will really see the effects. Picture late in a tight game. Instead of a keen

defensive-minded special- ist entering the game, you will now see the sharp shooter line up at the shoot- er’s table. Where only layups used to be permit- ted, now the three-point arch will draw all the at- tention. And maybe that's what we want. But, there are still questions about all of this. What are some?

Is the NCAA just pro- tecting its share of TV re- venues by implementing such a bold change? Do the college fans really want to see everything dupli- cated from pro sports? Will missing or making a three pointer make or ruin the chances for professional admiration?

And what about the spill- over effect—will we see an instant replay rule in college football in the years to come? And will high school adopt a 24-second clock?

Like everyone else, we Monday Niters don't know for sure. We only know that the world of sports is constantly changing and it won't be long before all rules will in some way be relaxed. Some will like it,

others will hate it. But, that's change for you.

All we can say right now is all change is not growth and all movement is not forward. Let's wait it out a the arch.

It's something to think about.

Monday Niters members are: Charles Allen, D.D.S., Jim Nunnelly, Ollie Hub- bard, C. Ashley, Issac Gardner, Ed West, Ter- ry Price, Carl Clark, Paul Booker and Tom Brown.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Rozelle Takes Stand For New Victims

Last week, we promised that we would continue to weigh and balance the use of drugs by professional athletes. And supporting that goal were Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ollie Hubbard, Jerry Price and Ed West. All felt drug testing was the right thing to do, especially when two topranked athletes met their deaths because of cocaine.

Well, we hardly got the words out of our mouths before Pete Rozelle, the commissioner of the National Football League, released a drug testing plan — for all NFL players. What’s in the plan and how are others viewing it?

First, every football player will be drug tested three times a year — once in training camp and twice randomly throughout the regular season. And, unlike other drug testing programs, this plan will be supervised by a NFL-hired physician. What a bold move! No longer will the teams be in charge of the testing — the League office will be. Go ahead, Rozelle.

But, just why did Rozelle “harden” the NFL’s position. He had already agreed to a plan approved by the Players Association sometime ago. So why did he “get tougher” in the middle of the stream. Did the two deaths get him to react bolder? No, Rozelle says he’s worried about all the victims caused by drug users. Not only the athletes who use drugs, but also “those injured by those taking drugs”. Talk about a new victim.

At first defense for this new “victim” seems a “little loaded” and farfetched. But, when you talk to those who are victimized by drug users on the field, the issue is not so “way out”.

After talking with a few agents, we are impressed that there is absolute fear among those who don’t, of those who do not use drugs. Drug-clean athletes are expressing concern that they cannot compete fairly against those “hopped up” athletes.

Not only are the victims physically abused by the drug-inspired athlete, but when the victims are exhausted after playing three tough quarters, the “inspired” athletes are still playing as if it were the opening kickoff.

Well, whatever your position, it’s clear that Pete Rozelle is no longer willing to “pussyfoot” with those who use drugs. He’s coming down real hard and the message is clear — “papa don’t want no drug using in here”.

And in a rather creatively informative (some judgementally opinionated) way, Rozelle has enlightened the public by addressing the concerns of those who have to play against those who use drugs. It is certainly a step in the right direction.

Now, where to next. Let’s keep exploring.

Members of the Monday Nite Footballers are; Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Tom Brown, Paul Booker, Carl Clark, Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Bobby Sumler: Working...Graceful...Lucky?

Diligence may well be the mother of good luck. That old saying is true and may well apply to Bobby Sumler. That's the little floor general from the Washburn Ichabods, winners of the NAIA Tournament Tuesday night at Kemper. The Topeka-based college outlasted West Virginia State, 79-77.

But before we get into Bobby's on-the-court contributions, let's look inside Bobby Sumler. First, he's from San Diego, and he kind of likes the change Topeka offers. A Communications major, he doesn't know there is "life after the NAIA championship."

And he even encourages his younger sister to choose the right college and do well. Now, let's get back to his court performance. And see just how his diligence may pay off for him.

HE DID HIS THING On Tuesday, 8,137 in-house and millions of ESPN television fans watched Bobby steal the show. He wasn't the highest scorer; he didn't win the Most Valuable Player trophy; and he didn't lead the team in rebounding.

But he was diligent, nonetheless. He did score 18 points, second highest. And he handed off a dozen assists. And he connected three out of four times from the three-point arch. Yes, he dazzled fans and pro scouts alike, performing with the grace of an Olympic figure skater. Bobby was "the show from Topeka." Working to shoot. Working to pass. Working to win.

WON HUSTLE AWARD That kind of working earned him the coveted Charles Stevenson Hustle award a place on the All-tourney's second team. Well, so what?

Well, the real fact is that Bobby Sumler is a big man—at 5-foot 7-inches. But he plays like a seven footer. More important, he operates on the floor as if he was the only "chosen" star in the sky. Flashing here. Sparkling there.

WILL HE BE LUCKY? We, in Monday Nite Football, know all too well the odds are against Bobby progressing beyond the college ranks. That would be the case even if he weren't built close to the ground. Even so, it is refreshing to see a Calvin Murphy or a Spud Webb "in the making." He'll need lots of work. And, in case good luck does pass his way, we'll have the pleasure of knowing it all started down at Kemper.

By Isaac Gardner Jr. Guest Columnist

Recently, Jim Nunnelly and I were privileged to enjoy a fun evening at Arrowhead with some of the Chiefs, their staff, and supporters. Given that Commissioner Nunnelly and I have opinions that are consistently at variance, I will catagorize the people I met, the activities, and present my opinion of the evening.

Head Coach, Frank Ganz—articulate, good P.R. skills, astute, political savvy, knows how to work a crowd. Time will tell whether he was sagacious in focusing on running backs and not giving the same attention to the offensive line which was exactly that, offensive.

Management—Les Miller and Don Steadman— positive, cordial, both seemed interested in establishing a positive relationship with the black community.

Former players Otis Taylor and Buck Buchannan— legends in their own time. My high regard for both men has escalated as I have observed them decades later in the real world.

Current player Stephonne Paige—super athlete, candid, honest, realistic, intelligent. I was highly impressed with Stephonne, not because of his athletic ability, but because there are obvious indications that he will do equally well after he leaves my favorite sport.

Food and beverages— plentiful, palatable, having been conditioned by Gates Bar-B-Q, I find that his competitors have a hard act to follow.

Film Highlights of 1986— entertaining. I have been and still am a loyal supporter and ardent critic of the Chiefs. I enjoyed viewing some super plays by some super athletes. However, what the films didn’t show may have been more revealing than what was shown. Granted the specialty teams did an excellent job under the leadership of Frank Ganz.

Though I am not a supporter or fan of John Mackovic, I think it was callous and in poor taste not to mention the contributions of Mackovic and the rest of the coaching staff during the 1986 season.

The overall event—very good managerial and administrative effort. The Chiefs, like the Kansas City, Mo., School District, have had their difficulties but are now on the upswing.

Chatter, Commentary and Conversation

Nostalgia... that's when you want to go back in time or you are homesick or you moan for the good times gone by.

Well, that's how the Monday Niters spent their evening, over at Ed West's posh, Raytown-Kansas City home. Though the surroundings were colorful, we all found ourselves drifting back in time. . . you know, the good old days. And the liveliest journey back-in-time was remembering old movies. Here are some of the favorites. . . Imitation of Life. . . To Kill A Mockingbird. . . Gone With The Wind. . . Island In The Sun. . . Godfather. . . To Sir, With Love. . . Liberation of Lord Byron Jones. . . Patch Of Blue. . . From Here To Eternity. . . and on and on. We literally forgot about the Dallas game and spent our time seeing who could recall the all-time great big screen productions. And further, these guys, well into their '60s and '70s, could recite with clarity. . . some of the most unforgettable lines, first stated in these classics. Nostalgia lives in us, big-time!

And finally we turned our attention to the Chiefs. Last week, the Chiefs held on dearly to beat San Diego, just by three. And we saw something there that we hadn't seen for a long time. . . relentless pursuit of winning. So many of the recent Chief teams just gave up and failed to give us "60 minutes of excellence." And then again, that nostalgia thing set in. All of us remember the Chiefs in their heyday.

Buck Buchanan, Willie Lanier, Jerrell Wilson, Otis Taylor, Mike Garrett and on and on. We remember the alacrity that each player displayed after every play. We remember that even in defeat, they held their heads high, not needing to explain anything. For they knew that their "absolute best" had been given. Nostalgia. . . homesickness. we want to go back to those times. . . oh, so badly.

Can ol' Herm take us there? That is the question. And we don't know the answer. Fans still are shaky when the Chiefs get ahead early in the game. There is this "little voice" inside us all, knowing full well that the lead could evaporate before the game ends. That would have never happened in the glory days. As "apostles of nostalgia", we remember that Ol' Warpaint (horse mascot) would keep running and running, up and down the sidelines, signaling another Stenerud field goal or another Otis Taylor touchdown pass. If you were born after 1970, you don't know what I'm talking about.

Sadly and full of nostalgia, we remember there was a time when the Chiefs were feared, respected, talented, and could beat anybody. They just kept on "matriculating down the field". Monday Niters want those days back. . . before we all die!

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

The Strike And The Big Numbers

Seventeen million. Two-hundred thirty thousand. Sixty-three thousand.

At the second meeting of the season, we, Monday Niters kept repeating those numbers. But, before we explain the underlying significance of those numbers, let's look closer at what we think about the strike. And then we'll return to numbers. When Gene Upshaw of the Players' union announced the strike Monday night during the halftime of the Jets-Patriots game, the endless opinions began, starting with a local dentist.

In his infinite and quiet wisdom, Dr. Charles Allen spoke for the fans. After thoughtful and careful consideration, he felt that we could look for a rise in ticket prices, if not now, then next year. Coach Jerry Price countered by stating that money was not the "real issue" in this strike. To the contrary, though Walt Livingston, an elementary educator, said, "The owners just don't want to give up the money."

And the strike chatter prevailed. Here and there. And on and on. Until Carl Clark, an inspired union man, spoke with renewed enthusiasm in favor of the strike. He asserted that the rest of us did not understand union issues and indicated a strong need for unrelenting unity among players. And, then the clatter turned to a vote.

And when those favoring the strike were counted, there was a slight majority, favoring the players. But, shortly after this, another dimension arose. Another side. An unexplored position—neither favoring the players not the owners. Led by Ike Gardner, a school district supervisor, some felt that the entire mess was strictly uncalled for. The owners' and the players are at fault for allowing this dreadful thing to occur, in the first place. And, though Ike is usually a maverick and an apostle of obstinance, this time he hit the nail on the head. Nothing is good about this strike. Consequently, no one should feel sorry for either group—neither the owners or the players. Obviously, the owners come from a plantation mindset. They own; therefore they decide and control. And the players, with a less understandable position, come from an I-have-been-taken-advantage-of kind of mindset. Both sides need to take a lesson in public relations. Or better still, a semester-long course in Consumer Affairs, because neither side has won the public's support. To go even further, neither group will ever be seen standing in the food stamp line. For, they all make aplenty.

Over and above the money they make from ticket sales, the owners get an average $17 million from television. And, the players average income is listed at a handsome sum of $230,000. In fact, one Monday Niter reported that John Elway, rifle-armed quarterback of the Denver Broncos, makes $63,000 a game. That's just not something one should strike about.

But, as history has repeatedly shown, both sides will squabble and squander, until both sides get more. More of this or that. But, more. And, we fans will have to pay more. Or spend more. Or wait more. Moreover, wouldn't it be delightful that when both sides agree, the fans would go on strike. Let them play to an empty house. Now, that's big numbers.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Okoye...Is Okay...

The greatest thing in this world is not where he is but in what direction he is heading. The "he" is Christian Okoye and he's heading for the Arrowhead Stadium. On Tuesday, the Chiefs selected him as their second-round draft choice. Let's look this newcomer over—his career, his life, and his strengths and weaknesses, and his direction.

First, his college career. By now, everyone knows that he was an All-American from a little-known college called Azusa Pacific. And unless one is completely blind, one can tell he's big, weighing a giant-size 253 pounds and standing 6'1". On top of all that, he's fast, clocked at 4.4 in the 40 yard dash. Go on. Go on.

And just think he didn't even play football, prior to three years ago. That's because he came to the United States on a track and field scholarship. His specialty—a worldclass discus thrower. From Nigeria. The remainder of his family, a father and three sisters, are still in the homeland.

Now, to the football field. Even though he has never played a down on artificial turf, he scored 21 touchdowns on natural grass. But, he doesn't consider that a big problem—at least, not an unsolvable problem. And in his final two years, he gained over 3,000 yards rushing.

Okay, Okoye, okay. And he's really serious about doing well, owing to his older-than-usual twenty five years. So what makes Ike Gardner (fresh from the marriage altar) say, "let's wait and see" when asked about Christian Okoye?

Is it what the critics say? They say he fumbles a lot. And they go on to caution fans that he runs "straight up," thereby making Okoye unreliable and injury-prone. Okoye answers his critics by simply chalking it up to his first years of football. However, he quickly points out that that was a "yesterday" problem and all is well, now.

And from the way Kansas City fans have been singing his praises and raising their 1987-season hopes, they believe that he has corrected those rookie jitters, too.

Jerry Price is one of those. He's convinced that Okoye is the "sleeper" of the entire NFL draft. Jerry expects Okoye to rise to the status of a Gale Sayers or a Mike Garrett, both former Chiefs' draft choices. In fact, most are thinking that he will be better than first-round pick, Paul Palmer of Temple. So what's the bottom line.

The bottom line is this. Okoye is okay. He has size, speed, a proven career, intelligence, and modesty. And we, Monday Nites, like the fact that he doesn't murder the English language, when interviewed. After all, he's one of the few athletes that will have earned a college degree—before entering pro football. What else can we say, except that we wish him the best that Kansas City can offer. And, like Avis, he'll try harder. And that trait alone makes him all right with us. As we said earlier, he's heading in the right direction. Or again, Okoye is okay.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary. By Jim Nunnelly

Tracy Cherry: Moving Up and Giving Back...Uniquely

"Moving up and giving back." That was the theme for last week's Women's Day program at St. James Methodist church. More personally, the theme applies neatly to a brilliant, unusual, athletic, and unique lady, Tracy Cherry. Let's see how.

First of all, Tracy was a scholarly athlete at Ursinus college, a small private Jesuit school, just outside Philadelphia. There she earned a 3.5 grade point average, majoring in Physical Education, with a minor in Business and Psychology. As if that was not enough, she was awarded All-America honors in field hockey. Now, how's that for being unique, a black female All America on the Dean's List. Moving up. Let's go on.

After graduation from this well respected institution, she was flown to Kansas City—to interview for and accept a teaching position at a local school, Barstow. There she became the first (and only) black faculty member in the entire history of the well-known school. Besides teaching Physical Education, she was appointed Coach of the Girls' field hockey team. Under her tutelage, the team overtook archrival Pembroke, in September 1982. Moving up, farther. What about this field hockey?

Simply put, field hockey is her "pride and joy." But her touchstone and strength is centered around her family. Being the only girl (again unique) with a cast of four brothers has entitled Tracy to a special position with her father. It was with his encouragement and guidance that she was able to select her college early, in the seventh grade. He wanted her in the best school, academically and athletically. Ursinus offered both. And her love for field hockey was sustained. Thanks, Dad for helping me move up.

But, when she talks about her mother, a heavy and deep-seated, emotional high settles on her face. That's because she's really close to her mother. Not only because of her "only girl" status, but because she respects her mother's undying devotion to her—and her four brothers.

Her mother made sure that all of them went to college, in spite of the fact that her parents were divorced when Tracy was twelve. Her mother helped Tracy "move up." Let's get back to Tracy's real love.

And, again she smiles widely and sincerely when she resumes talking about Field Hockey. She emphasizes that the sport is unique and requires a set of unique characteristics...strong legs, rangy wrist work, and the finesse and grace of an Olympic figure skater. And Tracy possesses and wears these attributes—in a pleasant and charming way. Enough of moving up, let's see what and how she gives back.

Not only does she sing in the choir at Mount Vernon Baptist church, she also teaches aerobics at the Linwood YMCA. And besides attending all her brother's football games (Deron Cherry of the Kansas City Chiefs), she finds enough time to talk to her mother every day, long distance from Palmyra, New Jersey. Talk about giving back. And the list could go on.

Finally, anyone can see that here is somebody who knows how to be somebody. Of all her honors, on and off the field, she believes deeply, in moving up and giving back—at the same time. Only a few people are ever treated to a set of unique experiences, academically and athletically. But, Tracy is a unique person, and she wears it well.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By James T. Nunnelly

Ed West: A Lesson In Little Extras

A native of Osawatomie, Kas. Graduate from Pittsburg State. Married, three children. Taught basic electricity, Lincoln high school in the early '60s. Likes to fish in the Kansas reservoirs and the Canadian clear lakes. Enjoys tinkering with all kinds of electronic devises. An avid sports enthusiast. All of these phrases describe Ed; but none better than the last one—an avid sports enthusiast. Let's see how.

First, Ed clearly remembers two championship games in his lifetime — one in high school and the other in college. One in which his team lost, the other in which his team won. And, he put together a valuable lesson and philosophy from both.

Like many athletes, Ed remembers vividly the day his Osawatomie high school team lost to Rosedale of Kansas City, Kas. That was

way back in 1957. And his memory is still impeccable. And a sound of disappointed excitement still permeates his voice. That day Ed, his four other black teammates, and the rest of his team went that “little extra.”

But, they lost.

And Ed still talks about it with a yesterday's freshness. Rosedale, a big school, beat Osawatomie, a little school. By one point. One lousy point, but, a big point. And Osawatomie had beat other big schools. But, that day David could not slay Goliath. From that, Ed learned that even you can learn from a loss. Especially, when you give your all and all; and even with that little extra, you can still lose. But, a real champion never gives up.

And he didn't give up. Two years later, as a sophomore corner back at Pittsburg State, he found himself in the first NAIA

championship game—Pittsburg State of Kansas verses Hilldale of Michigan. That day he was a star, especially on defense.

He intercepted two passes and returned a punt 60 yards, setting up a key touchdown. Pittsburg was proud that day, winning 26-21. And Ed was too. He and his team gave a little extra. And you can hear him smile about that game any time he talks about it. It was like a lesson. For extra credit in life.

Speaking of life, Ed holds a creative and steady interest in his work—as production supervisor at AT&T. Besides being athletic, he’s alert, ambitious, attentive and articulate. He has to be right; because his department produces 30,000 electronic circuit breakers daily. And every single day, he experiences the agonies of defeat and the thrills of victory, just

like in those championship games.

But, he gives that “little extra.” For he knows that his company and his superiors are concerned that foreign competitors will force them out of the marketplace. And the American companies must ban together. He’s proud to be a part of this happening. You can detect that challenge, deeply rooted in his smile. Just like in those championship games.

And, that little extra emerges, again. It’s evident that he’s learned well from his early athletic experiences. And, maybe that’s what sports teaches us, that the difference between ordinary and extraordinary is that “little extra.” And, that you must apply that “little extra” to everything you do, especially your chosen field.

Just like Ed does.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

Choice, Not Chance, Determines Destiny

San Antonio, Philadel- phia, New York, Portland, L.A. Clippers, and Atlanta had something in common on draft day—Monday June 22. They all wisely drafted seven footers in the 1987 talent-rich, NBA draft. Even the first team to pick, San Antonio, drafted David Robinson, a seven-foot, three incher out of the Na- vy.

But, one team used their first round draft pick to take a 5 foot 3 inch guard from Wake Forest. Yes — not seven foot, not six foot, by a five foot, three inch midget. What would compel the Washington Bullets to waste a precious, first round choice to pick a very short basketball player? Before we comment, let’s look in on the player and see if we can find some reason for this madness.

Bugsy's Background First of all, the honoree is a “little ant” named “Bugsy” (real name Ty- rone Bogues). Besides be- ing the shortest ever to be drafted into the giant- dominated NBA, he was once the teammate of Reg- gie Williams, 6'7" first round choice for the Los Angeles Clippers.

Okay, that was at the Dunbar high school in Bal- timore.

And recently, his claim to fame was his outstanding showmanship at the World Championship games in Spain. There he dazzled the international fans with his fancy footwork and pre- cision-like, bullet passing.

Okay, so what do the Mon- day Niters think?

The Fellows Are Reserved

Well, the guys all have expressed “guarded” res- ervation about this “gutsy” move on the part of the Bullets. Both Paul Booker and Ike Gardner think that it's just a publicity stunt and no more. Already, Washington has the tallest player at seven foot eight. And, with “Bugsy” Bogues, they would have the tallest and the shortest players.

Again, so what?

Ike further quips, that to be successful, Washing- ton will have to play Atlanta every week, suggestng that the only player that Tyrone can adequately guard is the Hawks Spud Webb (he’s 5'7"). Ed West says that he'll have to be able to jump through the moon. And, who knows, maybe he can. However, publicity or jumping ability do not win games.

Commentary

To win an appropriate share of one hundred and sixty two games, a team should choose the best play- ers possible. Thus, every game and everybody counts. Can you imagine little Bugsy trying to guard 6'9" Magic Johnson? How silly, point guard or not, it's just plain foolish. And, it tends to make a mockery out of this serious game of professional basketball.

But, even more seriously, we advise Washington to use choice, not chances, to determine their destiny. Put the best, most-likely- to-succeed players on the floor. Competition among the pro teams is fierce.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnely

Bo Jackson: Money Maker And Mind Changer

Vincent Edward Jack- son. Bessemer, Ala., Bo. Auburn university. 1985 Heisman Trophy winner. Three sport letterman at Auburn, football, baseball, and track. Drafted by the Tampa Bay football Buc- caneers. Signed with the Kansas City Royals, in- stead.

Said he was going to play forever for the Royals. And he signed a football "hob- by" contract Tuesday, July 14, with the Los Angeles Raiders instead.

And that's when the stuff hit the fan.

Amid all the opinions on whether Bo Jackson is trust- worthy or not, it does sell newspapers. And it does capture the attention of all sport fans, everywhere. Most important, there was

a clever escape clause, in his contract. That clause allowed him to change his mind and play football in- stead.

And "Ole Bo" has had a history of changing his mind about one thing or another.

So what's the beef? What's the harm? Let's explore.

Simply, the beef is that the players and fans feel ostracized and betrayed. Bold Bo announced his in- tentions to pursue a "hobby in the NFL." And, they holler as if the world of baseball is not business- like and brusque. And most Kansas Citions were both confused, surprised, and touched by his decision. But, we Monday Niters sup- port Bo's mind-changing,

money-making tactics. Ike Gardner curtly says, "He ought to earn all he can, while he can." Paul Booker follows by stating that Bo is only exercising his contract. Paul further states that others would like to do as Bo has done.

But, none of us really know what was or is on Bo's mind. We all know that here's a guy who is equally good at two major, high-paying, sports. Some submit that Bo is just going home to play his first and only love, football. For base- ball lovers, he certainly has achieved a fair enough record. In his second season as a Royal, he has hit 18 homers and drove in a team- leading 45 runs. That's com- mendable for a sophomore.

Well, no matter what's really on his mind, we know he had made a lot of moves-both on and off the field. He had been blessed.

and enabled to choose one, or the other, or both sports. Cleverly and legally, he got what he wanted, when he wanted it, like he wanted it. And, we have to support Ole' Bo, for covering, and then stealing all the bases.

Ollie Hubbard said it best, when he said, "Let Bo run." This time for the Los Angeles Raiders. Who knows, maybe in 1988, he'll run track-instead.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Who Is Better: Bird or Magic?

Basketball fans every- where have compared “the Bird” with “the Magic.” And, if you listen to the daily Superfan show on radio, you’ll hear it repeatedly. Bird is better. Magic Johnson is better. And on and on. And the fact that their respective teams are again competing for the championship, just fuels the “war” even further.

Well, we Monday Niters usually don’t talk about basketball—it’s off season for us. But, because everyone else is rendering opinions so freely, it does deserve our commentary. We’d like to talk mainly about salary and floor ability. Then, after that we’ll add some insight to this controversy.

Now to the bucks.

Both Are Paid Well

L a r r y Bird makes $1,800,000 annually, on a seven year contract, from the Boston Celtics. His counterpart, Magic Johnson enjoys a lucrative pay- check from Los Angeles, making a cool million each year, for twenty-five years, regardless of how long he can play. So, Bird makes more sooner and Magic makes less longer. Let’s move onto the court.

On The Court

On the radio show, whites generally pick Bird and the blacks pick Magic. And, it’s been that way since they met in the now-resurrected Indiana State-Michigan State game of some years ago. Now, let’s ask the fellows what they think.

Ed West quickly points out that it’s a “tough comparison.” He says Magic is better, mainly because of his all around ability. He adds that Magic can win tough games. And fair-minded Carl Clark agrees. But, Carl thinks that the Bird is just as important, and plays just as well. Further, both players take control of the game—Bird from forward position and Magic from the guard. Even, Carl says. And what about scoring?

Jerry Price is kind of like Carl Clark, when he addresses the shooting issue. “Bird is a better perimeter (outside) shooter and Magic is the best inside shooter.” Obviously the two have demonstrated countless times how true this statement is. And all of us know that Bird won the Three-Point Competition outright. Decided yet? Let’s wrap up.

Commentary

Tough comparison. It certainly is. But, it appears Magic gets the nod. Not only for his personal contributions, but for his “knack” for making his fellow Lakers rise to greater heights of excellence. He’s smart, courtwise, and he knows where everybody is, all the time. That is rare talent, anywhere in the NBA. And year after year, he is among the leaders in assists. Face it, he’s a bonafide floor general, without peer. A true leader. And on top of all that, he is a “picture of excellence”—unknown to and unbelieved by many. He is truly magic.

Ever watch "House" on television? It's a reality show, where the title character is slightly impaired and occasionally is seen as human i.e. he may not be the know-it-all that doctors seem exude.

But, it is an excellent show because through it all, humanity triumphs. Well, that also explains the plight of our Chiefs. Through it all, they manage to somehow triumph. Last year, they actually made the back-door-playoffs.

They stumbled and fumbled but somehow they made it to the first round. . . but were immediately eliminated. This year, this same reality is going to set in. Just one glance at the schedule confirms this assertion. The Chiefs have to play both Super Bowl teams. . . Indianapolis and Chicago. Then, the Chiefs will have to play San Diego twice. That's four losses, already. Add in a split with Denver and an improved Oakland. Figure in five blown games, where the Chiefs fail to tackle; then throw a series of unexplainable interceptions; and then, cop penalties that no one can fathom. To top it off, Cincinnati and Tennessee will just flat out kick the Chiefs' butts. That means the Chiefs will only win five games. But, Houston is one of them. Dr. House, can you heal thyself?

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly Predicting Since 1970

Super Bowl XLI

Miami, Florida Indianapolis Colts vs. Chicago Bears 5:25 p.m. KCTV 5

Urban American Outdoors

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Mary Lynn Clark said it best. This has been a relatively quiet Super Bowl. Not much said about “all” of it, because these two black coaches have broken through the “glass ceiling”.

Or is it because there just isn’t much to talk about? Well, let’s see if we can raise the “talk” bar. As you have noted, we predicted the “coaches” this week rather than the teams. That’s because this is moment in history where everyone wins. Either way, a black coach wins Super Bowl XLI. OK? And America wins. Because we can proudly say that a black coach has won and coached in a Super Bowl. And, last, Caucasian announcers will find it a bit awkward for either over-emphasizing or under-stating it. It will be interesting to see how they handle it.

But, back to Mary Lynn’s comment about the “quietness”. Now, from where I sit, every African American is talking about it, proudly. But the television sponsors are not. They ought to be talking about this like they talked about Elway, finally making it to his first Super Bowl. The way it appears, is that Manning will replace Big Bad John as the hype center piece. And that is an affront to all of us black people. This two-team black coaching thing is right up there with Jackie Robinson, breaking into base ball way back in the ’40s. And it should be on every media outlet in the country. Foreign countries ought to know that America has made good on some of its promises (not all of them, just some).

But, reality tells me that the giant media outlets will treat this rather “low-key”. Thus, I predict that very little media will be said about all of this on Sunday and the days leading up to it. And quite frankly, I mad about it. Whatever you do, don’t say that it doesn’t matter. Sunday may be the last time, for a long time, when we all can sing “We Shall Overcome” with meaning and purpose. After all, to me and to you, Super Bowl Sunday will be a sacred part of Black History Month (lest we forget).

Let all rejoice and be glad in it. Thanks, Mary Lynn for nudging us to speak up.

Monday Nite Footballers Predictions

By Jim Nunnelly

Chiefs Offense Not Good Enough To Think Super Bowl

Two weeks ago, the Monday Niters gave you an early indication of how the Kansas City Chiefs will do. And, we also reminded you that the Chiefs won the AFC West Championship, even though the Denver Broncos won the Super Bowl over Green Bay. As promised, this week we'll look at the offense and evaluate all the unspent potential as they began the pre-season in Tokyo last Saturday. The Chiefs lost to Green Bay 27-24.

QUARTERBACK: Elvis is back for the second full season and he should feel secure as the number one signal caller. That's because Rich Gannon is the backup again, in spite of his 5-1 record last year (Grbac was healing from a broken collarbone). While Grbac is no John Elway, he will guide the Chiefs "Midwest Coast" offense. Rating: Average

RECEIVERS: Andre Rison, the Spiderman, is back after a 1,000 yard receiving season in 1997. And, everyone knows he made Grbac look awfully good, especially with his third-down, over-the-shoulder catches. What's more, he has plenty of help from tight end Tony Gonzalez and newly acquired Derrick Alexander. If Elvis Grbac can throw it, these guys can catch it. Rating: Excellent.

OFFENSIVE LINEMEN: 1998 first-round draft choice Victor Riley will be on the bench. That's because Center Tim Grunhard, guards Will Shields and Dave Szott are still in place here, still stabilizing the most improved part of the 1998 Chiefs. Tackles may be weak on the pass rush out but overall the Chiefs line is as good as any. Rating: Above average.

RUNNING BACKS: Medicare Marcus Allen is gone, leaving behind several NFL endurance records. He won't be missed because Kimble Anders is the best of the Committee backs. He catches, he runs, and is rarely injured. Besides Kimble, no one has a for sure starting position. MIZZOU fans would like to see James Blackwell make the team. Rashaan Shehee, this year's draft choice, is still untested and unproven. We will all have to wait to see what happens. Don't look for much. Rating: Average at best.

FIELD GOAL KICKERS: Pete Stoyanovich, the Miami transplant, is making us all forget Nick Lowery. He was the NFL's most accurate and no one will forget that long field goal that beat Denver. Rating: Very Good.

KICK-OFF AND PUNTER RETURNERS: Even though someone said Tamarick Vanover will be used as a running back, don't believe it. He'll be running back kickoffs and giving the Chiefs' offense good field position. Most important, he's an exciting runner who needs more space than a regular backfield can give him. He's the real prime time. Rating: Good.

OVERALL OFFENSE: Because of the quarterback situation and a cast of unproven running backs, the Chiefs cannot even think about a Super Bowl appearance down in Miami. The truth is they must get better as the season progresses. Even though it's the same offense that won 13 games, they still need some help. The Monday Niters believe the Chiefs' chances all lie with the Chief's great defense. And, next week we'll look at that defense.

ON SUNDAY'S GAME -- First off, the Las Vegas oddsmakers have made San Francisco five-point favorites over our luck-filled Kansas City Chiefs. And, we can't win much more by defense alone. Everyone knows that the Thomas-led defense holds all opponents to scoreless second halves. Has the luck really run out? Donna Stewart thinks not. As we have done faithfully for over 30 years, the Monday Nite Footballers sat down at Carl Clark's house for a regal "Marylyn meal." While there were many positive comments about the royally-presented meal, everything said about the Chiefs was negative and critical. Over and over, the word was about how the Chiefs were embarrassingly defeated by an inferior team, the San Diego Chargers. Miraculously, the Chiefs actually beat the Chargers...first down, passing yardage, rushing yardage, etc. But somehow the Chiefs became penalty prone, and with Grbac throwing four interceptions, the game slipped slowly into the hands of the Chargers. What's the problem? Simply put, it's the woefully poor performance of our quarterback, Elvis Grbac. While we can forgive Grbac for his interceptions, calling a time-out with only a minute or so into the third quarter is just plain unforgivable. The boy's lack of clock management skills ultimately was key to the Chief's loss.

So, what do we do? Clark and Dr. Charles Allen say "dump Grbac." More over, his lack of consistency overshadows the emerging fine play of Rashann Shehee, replacement for the injured Kimble "the no longer nimble" Anders. All of these reasons are significant enough to demote the king. However so, Grbac will remain number one.

And with that in mind, it is difficult for some of us to even consider the Chiefs as winners on Sunday. The New England Patriots will be ready to take advantage of our dwindling self esteem and failing offense. And Ol' Ike Gardner has Ed West and Paula Booker predicting against the Chiefs. Only Allen, Clark and Lloyd Greenfield think the Chiefs have a chance. Specifically, Clark alertly believes the 78,000 fans in the stadium will make a difference in the outcome. He's right because the Chiefs have managed to win both home games over Denver and Detroit. SUPER BOWL SUNDAY, an undeclared national holiday: More friends will gather on this day than on any other Sunday of the year. There’ll be big screens and big fun. Some will decorate, some will just watch the game. But most will watch all the new commercials being released for the first time. A 30-second spot will cost $1.6 million. Mastercard, Pepsi and Oldsmobile will be among the 25 major companies showing off their new creations. Look for Anheuser-Bush updates on the lizards and the famous, field-goal kicking Clydesdale horses.

ABOUT ATLANTA: This is the first Super Bowl appearance for the "dirty birds" of Atlanta. The Falcons won the NFC West after a 14-2 record for the 1998 season. But, it's the fourth trip for Head Coach Ed Reeves to the Super Bowl. As you know, the Atlanta Falcons were a 3-13 in 1996 and won the next 22 out of 26 in 1997 and 1998. The main "dirty bird" is running back Jamal Anderson, who set a Falcon's rushing. Look for the Falcons to "fly right" over the Broncos in a close one.

ABOUT DENVER: Will it be Elway’s last game? Yes. Will it be Denver’s second consecutive Super Bowl victory? Probably not. But, Denver’s got it all…Terrell Davis at running...Shannon Sharpe at tight end...John "Medicare" Elway at quarterback...Smith and McCaffrey as the receivers...Smith and Atwater on defense. And, remember they beat the Green Bay Packers last year in Super Bowl XXXII. Denver will have their hands full on Sunday.
