Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnely

It's That Time Of Year

It’s That time of year again when we sports fans start talking about our sometimes Kansas City Chiefs. What will they do this year? How many games will they win? Are they going to the stardom of the sixties and seventies?

We Monday Nitears, take the challenge to predict the “shaky” destiny of our last year’s last place Chiefs. Is this the year? Let’s rate the 1986 edition of the offense and defense. Afterwards, we’ll predict where the Chiefs will end up in December. Here goes.

SPECIALTY TEAMS Kicking game is very solid. Nick Lowery is the best. The Chiefs need a lot of work on punt and kickoff coverage, however.

DEFENSE Defensive Line: Art Still is strong and is one of the best in the entire NFL. Ike Gardner has always considered this area number one.

Linebackers: No comment at this time.

Secondary: Potentially the defensive backfield is one of the most explosive. Kevin Ross, Deron Cherry, Albert Lewis may be the “crown princes” of the NFL secondaries. Just plain awesome at times.

OFFENSE Offensive Line: Ed West, Ollie Hubbard and Carl Clark all agree that there is “hope” for the front line. For once, they did protect the quarterback. At various points, last Saturday, they did open holes. Jozwiak, Lutz, Budde, Adickes and newcomer Eatman will be tested; too new to say.

Receivers: There’s none better in the NFL—Carson, Paige, Hancock and Marshall represent the “alpha and the omega” in pro football. We rate this crew as "unstoppable”; there’s none better. If Todd Blackledge can get them the ball, they can do the rest.

Running Backs: Here we had mixed feelings. Ollie Hubbard says Ethan Hor- ton is not the power threat we need. Ed West feels that the “Purdue Pair” of Pruitt and Green are refreshing and promising. We’re for ever looking the shaky and the coaches shaking. We’re forever looking for running backs and it never stops. Too shaky to say.

Quarterbacks: All of us are still a bit shocked at replacing Bill Kenney with Blackledge. Most of us fans believe that with stronger line protection, either could do well. Blackledge will make a difference, depending on his ability to connect with the best receivers in football, whereas this area is still questionable.

OVERALL RATING Here’s where Ollie Hubbard, Ed West, Carl Clark and Tom Brown all agree: No Super Bowl for the Chiefs, winning 500 or less (at best). All feel the Chiefs will place third in the Western Division, behind L.A. Raiders (Oakland) and the Denver Broncos. It will be the defense that will get them that far.

Except for the receivers, the rest of the offense is either “too new” or “too questionable” to predict.

COMMENTARY As always, hot or cold, we’ll be there in Arrowhead (especially Ike and Carl) on a someone’s house each Sunday afternoon from September to December. Hollering, screaming, argu- ing—just like we’ve been doing for over 20 years. Yes, it’s that time of year. And, we’re glad about it.

Members of the Monday Nitears are: Charles Allen, Paul D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnally, Jerry Price and Ed West.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly ...................................

Larry Holmes: He Knocked Em Out When He Said...

While Monday Niters don't claim to know about everything in sports; however, we do represent the over-30, long-time, innercity black sports fans. What those fans think, coupled with a little history, always makes for solid commentary. But, last Saturday night, all Kansas City was shocked to see the SpinksHolmes rematch reduced to a joke. And that's our feeling too. It was a joke.

These same fans commented in a disbelieving sort of way; "Spinks didn't do anything." "They (the judges) took the fight from Holmes." "Ain't no way." "Holmes made folks mad and that's all..."

They were shocked that Spinks was declared the winner. Let's look backward for a moment before commenting further.

Going back to an earlier moment, Holmes said he wanted to fight Spinks again to regain "his respect". Certainly it was not for money, for Larry Holmes can buy Libya and a few other countries. He admits that everything he says is turned around and he ends up having to apologize. Well, that respect was returned to Larry Holmes Saturday night. But, not as the winner. His respect came in another way.

Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Ed West, Ollie Hubbard's father, Sandra Inez Hubbard and Janice Nunnelly all thought Holmes had won the fight. It was not surprising that it was a split decision—but not for Spinks.

The way we see it Holmes won two thirds of the fight—the earlier and middle five rounds and Spinks won the last five. Even Sugar Ray Leonard couldn't even agree with the unexpected decision. Only Judge Joe Cortez had the guts to render a reasonable decision in favor of Holmes. Well! Well! What now?

Well, no doubt Holmes is through in the ring. He even states "I can't win no more," not only in the ring but in the role as a champ. For some odd, unknown reason Larry Holmes was never accepted as a champ. He possessed neither the humility of a Floyd Patterson nor the charisma of a Muhammed Ali.

They just didn't let Larry Holmes be. He fought winners and he won. Now he leaves the profession winning 48 and losing only two—both times to Spinks. And maybe really only losing once. We clearly believe Holmes won Saturday night. But the question is—did he get his respect back and just how?

In the usual post fight interview, Larry Holmes calmly admitted, "I got a big mouth" referring to his many comments about the judges, Rocky Marciano and others for which he eventually had to apologize. But, the more he talked in that interview, the more he stopped apologizing. Finally, he got his respect back.

He knocked them out when he told the judges, the promoters, the press and all his critics, "You Can Kiss Where The Sun Don't Shine". Larry Holmes knew he had won—Holmes gained the respect of sports fans everywhere. It's something to think about.

Members of the Monday Nite Footballers are: Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Artis Ahsley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly, Jerry Price and Ed West.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Instant Replay: A Second Look

Editor’s Note: This is the second time the Monday Niters have addressed the 1986 instant replay system. Most of the information was supplied by Ed West, Ike Gardner, Carl Clark and Charles Allen, D.D.S.

Come next March, the NFL owners will have to take a second look at the limited instant replay sys- tem, employed this year for the first time. Is there too much delay? Does it add to the length of the game? Before we com- ment, let’s first look at just how the system is sup- posed to work.

First, let's talk about what the replay system set out to accomplish. Es- sentially, the club owners felt that on certain plays, the telecast (as viewed by fans at home) should be used to correct an error made down on the field. From the press box, the “high priest” replay of- ficial was to “overrule” an on-the-field decision. Only when there is “in- disputable visual evi- dence” to warrant a change.

Now, here’s how the step to step procedure works.

The replay official watches two monitors from the press box.

The replay official watches two monitors from the press box.

If he believes an error has been made, he will contact the umpire through a belt-worn walkie talkie.

If he believes an error has been made, he will contact the umpire through a belt-worn walkie talkie.

Then, he will complete the review of the play on one of two monitors in about 15 to 20 seconds.

Then, he will complete the review of the play on one of two monitors in about 15 to 20 seconds.

Then, he will relay to the umpire his decision. Afterwards, his decision will be announced to the stadium fans by the ref- eree. Sounds simple enough.

Then, he will relay to the umpire his decision. Afterwards, his decision will be announced to the stadium fans by the ref- eree. Sounds simple enough.

But, if you have been to some of the Chiefs’ games, you’d know that it didn’t work out according to the plan. There have been several miscommun- ications between the replay official and the umpire, resulting in an unearned touchdown. And, at the Sea- hawks game last Sunday, the replay official stopped the game at least five times and reversed three calls, taking much longer than the planned for 15-20 se- conds.

Does all this mean that we should shelve the press box replay system? No way. Monday Niters have waited since the early sev- enties for this much needed gadgetry. In spite of the terminology mix up, and the slight delays, things will improve over time. And for once, we agree with John Mackovic in his belief that all the “kinks” will eventually be worked out.

More importantly, when we think, of all the calls that were missed, prior to the acceptance of the replay system, we are even more supportive of and con- vinced about the need for the system. We recommend only that a second look at certain items be taken. The system, as a whole, represents progress. And, we’re glad about it.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

The Outside And Inside Of Ollie Hubbard

Ever been around 18th Street? Or 28th and Wabash? Lincoln university? Bingham Junior high? Or Frances Willard elementary school? If you have, then you have to know Ollie Hubbard.

Friendly, vivacious and always wearing a Santa Claus smile. His countenance suggest he's running for county office. Yes, that's "Big Ollie"—all 270 pounds of him.

Except for a few very close friends, like Monday Nite Footballers, few would know that Big Ollie carries inside his head, deep-seated views on the issues surrounding his profession. For 20 years, Ollie has been an educator in the Kansas City, Mo., school district. And, he has cleverly blended his love for his profession.

First, Ollie is quite concerned about all grade point requirements being placed on athletes. He strongly feels this approach is sound and just. He's constantly telling his young, sixth graders that "you must learn the curriculum to the fullest".

Further, he is always telling those same elementary school athletes that sports is secondary to a good education. And, with today's classroom requirements for athletes, Ollie accepts his role as elementary teacher and strives to prepare the young athlete to work hard in the classroom. And, he does it, feeling inside that he could influence all sports-minded, elementary students at an age where it would be easier to emphasize the classroom.

Is this the Ollie we know? Let's look further inside and see how Ollie feels about other things. What about reading and test scores.

A little nervous, but emphatic, he says the District is improving the students' reading skills. He likes the Hough-Mifflin method, for he knows that reading is to the mind what exercise is to the body. And he smiles proudly when talking about how the test scores have steadily improved over the years. But, he changes that smile and says bluntly, "we can't let up".

From there, he goes on and on about the school levy and tells how it can affect the teaching of young, innercity children.

One, he really wants the District to regain its AAA, top rating. He knows that the teachers and the students are working harder than ever "to keep improving" and that the levy must pass, for the children need it now more than ever.

Now after 30 minutes of his inside, Big Ollie returns to his usual jovial self. His athletic frame sighs back to normal. His friendly, vivacious, Santa-Claus smile reappears. And he starts again, talking about sports and the upcoming football season. His outside regains itself.

Yes, he's more normal now. But, we all know that this little trip inside Ollie's world helps us to know there are dedicated teachers, who truly believe that all children must learn, regardless of their athletic ability.

Ollie happens to be one that knows the "inside" of both worlds and that we must start young if we expect athletes to perform well in the classroom—in high school and in college.

We truly do salute your efforts, Ollie. For we know, with your inside input, the young athlete will know that their performance inside the classroom is just as important as outside on the field.

A Pleasant Illusion Is Better Than A Harsh Reality

Editor's Note: The fol lowing column takes a look back in Chiefs' history with quarterback Len Dawson when the team won a wild card berth in the NFL playoffs and went on to win the Super Bowl.

Sure glad we did it. We came back. And we showed the world that we're as good as any in the National Football League. Number One.

That's a guy named Len ny talking. It's about 6:00 p.m. on January 11. in New Orleans. After the Big Game.

And just think they paid fifteen bucks to see us. And at Tulane Stadium. How much will the share be? Doesn't matter, we got a big ring coming.

And, boy did the offense click. We went right over number eighty eight, Mr. Eller. And Mr. Page, we took him, too. So now! What do you think, you Minnesota Vikings? We beat you. We beat you. We beat you.

Score: Kansas City 23, Minnesota 7.

Sure glad that gambling thing didn't get in my way. And a week before the big gest game in my life. Super Bowl four. Just think, I was selected Most Valuable Player.

And the best part about it, I'm now up there with other Super Bowl MVP's. There was Bart Starr in Super Bowl I and II. And Joe Namath in number III. Ain't that something. Till now, all MVP's have been quarterbacks. And I'm up there with them.

And we beat Oakland to get here 17-7. Holmes, Mar sails, Buck Buchanan, Cur ley Culp.

23-7. Can you believe it! Can you believe it!

Emmitt Thomas, Jim Kearney. Wow.

And wow! Did that game plan work? That Stram. Ain't he something? Short, play-action passes. And run right at 'em. You Vikings, you were just out classed today.

Yes, me - the MVP. Su per Bowl IV.

Probably should have been Otis or Mike Garrett. That was the smoothest five yard touchdown I've ever seen. And, that catch and run by Otis Taylor. 46 yards. That broke it open. Yes, that one broke the Vikings' back. Yes. Yes. That one was the one. I can still see that little kick as he crossed the goal line. And, that guy named Ralph Brown from Kansas City knows it. He's here today.

Hey, wait a minute, I almost said it. Otis should have been the MVP. Wo wie!

I'll think about that, tomorrow, January 12, 1970 or maybe, I'll think about it years from now. Maybe in 1986. It'll sure be a while before we'll be back here. Or anywhere near the playoffs.

Let's enjoy it. Enjoy, en joy........

Reality It's now December 12, 1986. -

And the harsh reality is right now. Will the Chiefs make it to the playoffs again as a wild card team? And then go on to win the Super Bowl twenty one? Maybe? Probably, not.

You make the call.

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

The Relentless Cherry

Certainly the Chiefs have taken their ups and downs this year. With a coach in his "year of challenge" and a seemingly easy sched-ule, the Chiefs have won the big ones and lost to the little ones. And, way back in 1981, Deron Leigh Cherry was kind of an up and down too. But before we elaborate, let's look at Deron's football career.

Born and raised in Pal-myra, N.J., Cherry was signed by the Chiefs as a punter in 1981. Beaten out by Bob Grupp, Deron switched to the secondary and played behind All Pro Gary Barbaro.

1982 didn't bring much. He didn't make one inter-ception. Not one. But, he stayed on as the fifth back, especially on third down passing situations. Key phrase—no interceptions. Wait until next year.

Yes, the 1983 season start-ed the upswing for Deron. Amid all the hype about first round pick Todd Black-ledge, Deron made All Pro—at safety. He inter-cepted seven passes for 100 yards. And, the All Pro status seemed to fit. It sounded good. Deron—All Pro. Deron—All Pro.

For the next three years, Deron would wear that re-vered title, even in spite of the Chiefs 8-8 and 6-10 seasons. 1984 and 1985 were still banner years for Deron. Further, he became one of the Chiefs most con-sistent tacklers. Now, to-day.

After fifteen games this season, Deron has im-proved over his previous All Pro years. Monday Ni-ter Ed West says Deron's record speaks for itself. Ike Gardner adds that in a large measure, Deron is responsible for the Chiefs' playoff hopes and Carl just says "he does his job". Let's look.

A league leading nine interceptions, two in the key game against the LA Raiders. That's 30 in a four-year period, not counting what he is likely to do in Sunday's game against the Steelers. And his durability is unquestionable, having started every game during the past four seasons. And like Walt Livingston, Carl Clark, and Tom Brown pre-dicted, the players, on Wednesday, voted Deron All Pro again, for the fourth consecutive year. Not bad. Not bad.

No, it's really pretty good. For you see, Deron was signed on as a free agent punter in 1981, from an eastern school called Rutgers. And, as the Chiefs have often made many mis-takes this year, they made a more dramatic one in 1981. They actually re-leased Deron during the final cutdown, just before the beginning of that sea-son. Can you imagine they actually cut an All Pro "in the rough". True, they did.

But, after a few key in-juries, Deron was reac-tivated a few games later. But, you really do have to wonder what went on in the Chiefs' minds—to release a potential four-year All Pro.

Thanks, Deron—for your good season. More impor-tant, thanks for staying around Kansas City, es-pecially when it appeared you were not needed. But, a really big thanks for being relentless and hanging in there and waiting for your "down" to come "up". We salute you.

This is an opportunity to update their individual success with the first Predictor's Scoreboard of the 1993 season. Ike Gardner is off to a 2-0 start in hopes of defending his 1992 season title. Remember Super Bowl I? Representing themselves and the American Football League in the first championship match with the National Football League, the Chiefs played well for one half before bowing to the supremacy of the Packers at the Los Angeles Memorial Coliseum on January 15, 1967. The contest was billed as the AFL-NFL Championship game. It would be a couple of years before Chiefs founder Lamar Hunt would actually put the name “Super Bowl” on pro football’s premier game: the name came from his children’s toy “Super Ball.” The game would feature 13 Pro Football Hall of Famers: Hunt, LB Bobby Bell, DT Buck Buchanan and QB Len Dawson for the Chiefs, and Packer head coach Vince Lombardi, RB Jim Taylor, T Forrest Greg, QB Bart Starr, LB Ray Nitschke, DB Herb Adderley, DE Willie Davis, S Willie Wood and DT Henry Jordan for the Packers.

By Jim Nunnelly

Championship Predictions

Jacksonville vs. New England Green Bay vs. Carolina

Post Season Advice To The Chiefs:

BECOME AN EXPANSION TEAM

It seems only yesterday when everyone in Kansas City was talking about the Chiefs. Coming off a 13-3 record in 1995, and feeling like we were just a step away from the New Orleans Bowl, we bought up all the red sweat shirts, missed church on Sundays and bragged about our Chiefs, as if we had become the mecca for the National Football League.

And, then it was here...the 1996 season. And, bam...off to a 4-0 start and even after eleven games, we had managed to win eight...some in the last minute and a couple in overtime. But, we were winning, and New Orleans was still in our radar.

And after that, things went downhill. Even the ever-venerable defensive led by Neil and Derrick and Tracy, began to crumble. Players began to talk openly. Some even accused the marquee players of being more interested in lucrative commercials, business interests, and good times rather than playing football. What used to be Super Bowl pursuit became a barely audible cry for the playoffs. You know, we didn't even make that, losing to Buffalo.

What is the answer and what advice should we tell the Chiefs as they prepare for San Francisco, Carolina, Jacksonville, St. Louis, and Miami in 1997? Not to speak about Denver, San Diego and maybe a new improved Seattle.

Here is our advice:

QUARTERBACK: Jeff George is available. Take him. It's better than nothing (Bono). We now know we can't make the playoffs without an effective quarterback.

DEFENSIVE END: Neil Smith is about to go on Medicare. The 1996 season was his worst and it signals a coming decline. For once, the Chiefs should get ahead of the parade rather than behind it.

RUNNING BACK: Congratulations to management for announcing that Greg Hill will replace Medicare Marcus. Ike Gardner questions the wisdom of doing this so early mainly because it may dampen the preseason competition. Maybe you're right Ike. But, at least, we made a move.

OFFENSIVE TACKLE: John Alt should join the NASA space team. The Chiefs offensive line is falling down.

RECEIVERS: We let Willie Davis go to Houston and we've been struggling ever since. The early season injury to Lake Dawson didn't help matters. Chris Peen tried. LaChapelle is overrated. So, our advice, get two top receivers from the draft or the free agency pool.

DEFENSIVE SECONDARY: Hold the line here. Help Mark Collins get over his mad spell. Continue to work with Hasty, send Stargell home. And, start looking real seriously at our linebackers. May need some new faces here.

COACHING AND MANAGEMENT: Well, Carl Peterson has been here eight years and he has put the hope back into Kansas City. He hired Marty, prayed for Joe Montana, and sold out Arrowhead week after week. But for some reason, there has been no announcement that he has been rehired. Obviously, it's Carl's call. He can go anywhere he wants. After all, he did turn the franchise around. Owners, fans, and the front office staff are happy. We'll see. Come on Lamar, tell us...is he in or out?

Well, that brings us to hard-headed Marty. While Marty cannot be blamed for the sub-mediocre performance of his players. He can, however, get them out of the game before they hurt the chances of winning. He can revise the whole play-calling process. And he can stop telling us we're "Super Bowl ready" when obviously we're not. Clark, Booker, Ed West and Walt Livingston have doubts about Ol' Marty. But, consensus says Marty will stay and realistically address some of the concerns the fans have raised.

FINAL COMMENTARY: Most of us in the Monday Nite Footballers have watched a dozen or so coaches promise the rain and deliver raindrops. We've seen the defense carry the offense. We've seen a packed and empty Arrowhead. All of this leads us to why we are fans. We love these Chiefs like parents love their children. Very unconditional. Good or bad. Under or over. Behind or in front. We are going to be there for these Kansas City Chiefs. Are we going to stop talking about them? NO. Are we going to remind them of the failed Super Bowl return promise?

YES. Are we going to sit in subzero weather next year to see a possible promise turn sour. YES! YES! YES!

And now, one final piece of advice. Since we're still trying to get to the Super Bowl, it is possible we need to take a look at the two-year expansion teams, Jacksonville and Carolina. They both seem to have the ingredients to expedite and implement a sound plan...maybe Kansas City ought to become an expansion team.

Then everybody, including the players, would be happy!

Monday Nite Footballers

By Jim Nunnelly

...On Needed But Unliked...

The Monday Niters just can't understand it. Why Todd Blackledge is the number one quarterback over "seasoned and proven" Bill Kenney. While we are known "never to agree" totally on anything, we got some good advice for John Mackovic, head coach of the Chiefs. Here goes.

After four pre-season and three regular season games, the Chiefs offense has not produced to the fans' expectations. But, before we get "too deep" into commentary, let's look back at Todd's past and then analyze the present problem (as we see it).

Now, Todd has a fine history. Born and reared in Canton, Ohio (home of Pro Football's Hall of Fame); son of a college football coach (his father is now offensive line coach for Pittsburg Steelers). High school and college All-American. First-round draft choice. 1983 Kansas City Chiefs (the worst according to Charles Allen). Though "armed" with a strong resume' and solid pro potential, Todd right now, should not be the start-ing quarterback. Why? Let's listen in on the Mon-day Niters.

Ed West, former pro pros-pect, says Todd just "can't read defenses". And, he and Ollie Hubbard point out that the problem is not for "lack of receivers". It's just that Todd is not having the season Bill Kenney had at the same time last year. That's what Tom Brown says. Just that sim-ple. But let's keep on.

Mostly, the real problem lies in the way things were handled. Before pre-season, Big Mac (Chiefs Head Coach, John Mackovic), announced that Todd Black-ledge was his number one quarterback before the first 1986 football was thrown. No competition, just BIRTHRIGHT. He didn't have to prove him-self. He just appointed Todd "number one". Let's go on.

In a heated discussion, Paul Booker asserts that Chiefs front office may have dictated that Black-ledge become number one. However so, or whatever the reasons, the Chiefs can't win with Blackledge at the helm, not right now, in the "year of the chal-lenge". If one watched the Hous-ton win, one would know it was all defense. The of-fense, under Blackledge, just "failed to thrive". Six completions out of twenty three passes. Very unim-pressive. Ike Gardner put it best "man can't live by bread alone and the Chiefs can't win by defense alone".

Now, there it is. As long time fans, we deserve more than we're getting. And, choosing a quarterback is one example of a decision made earlier, that has to be corrected. And, we Mon-day Niters, with over 170 years of combined and or-ganized fan experience, just can't buy the whole Blackledge affair. Our ad-vice to John Mackovic is simple: give the Chiefs' offense a boost; restore Kenney to number one, put Blackledge on the bench.

But, in the words of Lord Chesterfield, "advice is seldom welcome, and those who need it the most, like it the least".

Members of the Monday Niters are: Charles Allen, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nun-nelly, Jerry Price and Ed West.

Chatter, Conversation and Commentary

The 'Hands Off Bono' Policy

EDITOR'S NOTE: The Monday Niters made their comments on Bono prior to the announcement of the quarterback change. The Monday Niters strictly want to see quarterback Steve Bono benched and replaced with Gannon. He's not the charter to prove that he can rise up from a backup at San Francisco to a starter here with the Chiefs. So far, he has yet to prove it.

It's not because he arose on the heels of the now-departed Joe Montana. It's not because he hasn't had sparks of brilliance along the way. In fact, sportscasters often remark of the Chiefs' winning percentage when Bono is quarterbacking. The reason we advocate the big change to Gannon is that Bono has yet to show that he has the "right stuff" to lead us to a Super Bowl somewhere in the future.

Marty Schottenheimer and Carl Peterson promised us a Super Bowl visit in five years. Well, we can see put our losses and readjust our goal by first replacing Bono. Some will say, that Bono is not the only reason we are in a weak second place in the AFC. We agree, but not the most glaring spot needing front office attention is quarterback Steve Bono.

Dr. Allen, Ollie Hubbard, Ike Gardner, Ed West, Lloyd Greenfield and certainly Harry Sypert--all have called for Bono to resign. They constantly are on the move that five column appearance especially now that each game is so critical to our doused Super Bowl hopes. Walt Livingston is now predicting a loss for the Chiefs whenever Bono is the starting quarterback. Don't they have us?

Well, that's our column for this week. As you look at our predictions remember they were made before yesterday's game against Detroit. Of course, we all like and support the Chiefs but we have been so disappointed with "Hands-Off Bono" policy. Carl Clark put it best when he said, "Once a backup quarterback, always a backup quarterback."

And this time, Carl is right.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

Okay, okay, we must let it go, so we can grow.

We must, for sure, get over that first, Vermeil-engineered loss to Oakland. Write it seems so long ago, it was really just two weeks ago, out at Arrowhead. There was a "sea of redness" out there. Everybody, 79,000 strong, came out to see the Vermeil edi-tion of the Kansas City Chiefs. The media had talked about how Trent Green would make us forget about Elvis Grbac. And, Priest Holmes, would add punch to a punch-less running game. And what was once called a great defense, had somehow dropped away the last half of the game. So, what do we, as fans, do? Give up and prepare for another series of letdowns, especially at the end of the regular season?

No, absolutely not. There is something good about an early season loss. It allows a team to match reality with expectations. Each year, we an Monday Nitors, begin to think that this is the year we return to the Super Bowl. Ike remembers that . . . way back in 1969. Hank Stram was coaching and Len Dawson was quarterbacking and Otis Taylor was incomparable in his leaping and pass-catching ability. We had a running game that featured a no-name guy named Tank Holmes. Rangy Buck Buchanan and Willie Lanier and Jim Lynch terrorized opposing teams. While the defense didn't have a nickname, they nonetheless made us feel might proud to be a Chiefs' fan.

All that has withered away now. We have some talent, but not enough to make the playoffs. But, there is opportunity in our early season loss. We've got time to correct those weaknesses and thank God the weaknesses showed up early. The running game looked the same to us. "Bent Trent" looks a lot like Elvis. And the once-held-high defense is just another weak spot among many.

But, it is early. And, like most things, it will take time. So our advice to ourselves and you simple.

Let us go so we can grow.

Carl Clark, Dr. Charles Allen and Lloyd Greenfield keep telling us that the Kansas City Chiefs are going to win more games than we think. Last week, these fearless threesome stood alone and predicted that the Chiefs would defeat the then un-defeated New England Patriots. While some may say the locals were lucky, it is nonetheless their third victory this season, all at Arrowhead. In fact, Greenfield thinks they will continue to win at home, which if true, makes the Chiefs a contender to be 8-8. While 78,000 fans are helpful, the Chiefs have to show vast improvement in certain areas. Clark says the Chiefs have to stop those needless penalties. Ed West and Paul Booker insist that the offense must become more consistent. But, Ike Gardner is a bit more cynical. He wants to buy one-way bus tickets anywhere for quarterback Elvis Grbac and cornerback Chris Dishman. Gardner has never been known to sugarcoat his feelings but, in a nutshell, he may be right this time.

Even though Grbac did not hurt us Sunday, it is clear that he is the key to making the offense work. And most of us Monday Niters think Grbac is not the one to lead us this millennium or the next. But, he is the choice of the coaches and the management and that’s it. So, just relax and hope that the dominant defense continues to emerge game after game. Speaking of defense, that brings us to the Dishman. Every team will we play will go after Dishman. He’s slower, less agile, and no longer invincible. Unfortunately, with receivers having world-class speed, cornerbacks must be young, gifted and acrobatic. Dishman is no longer any of these adjectives and is a constant reminder of how Kansas City is known as a retirement center for former NFL greats. But, there’s still hope.

On next Thursday night, the Chiefs will visit the Baltimore Ravens, after a week off, and just like on the Sunday afternoon game, we won’t know which Chiefs' team will show up...the one who plays hard to win or he one that is determined to lose. All we can say is, Derrick Thomas is playing like he used to. Donnie Edwards and Chester McClockton are tough up the middle. And on the offensive side, Joe Horn and Kevin Lockett have joined Derrick Alexander to become the most productive group of receivers in the American Football Conference. The running backs on a good day can find the wide holes opened up by guards Will Shields and center Tim Grunhard. Even if Grbac is average against Baltimore and Dishman is again vulnerable, we still have the wherewithal to beat the Baltimore Ravens. Again, it will be close but as we said earlier, a win is a win.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

==================================================

Watching Monday Nite Football

What goes on at a Monday Nite Football event? Watch- ing the football game, of course! Before responding further, another question should be asked-what goes on "before" Monday night!

Well, on the preceding Tuesday, it all starts with a little conversation-be- tween the member and his mate, about what to serve. It must be "special" and it must be "the best"- because the Monday Niters are coming for dinner.

By the end of the week, with a special meal planned for ten guys, the next stop is the grocery store. Why do we have to buy snacks? Why do you need club soda? You didn't say anything about hot sauce. Does it "just have to be J & B? That's how the conversa- tion goes. And after 17 years, it's still basically the same. Nervous prep- aration.

Next, comes the meal preparation itself. Usually Monday Niters "never" go in the kitchen to help out. He becomes a "bo- na fide chef" when it's his turn to host "the big event". This is important stuff! And for 10 of the "most important guys" in the world. And, it must be right.

And, that's where the argument starts. The two people who have worked together to plan this event are now "at odds". The Monday Niter himself, un- derstands why there must be a salad. Or why the delicacies and the refresh- ments must be "ready and waiting" when the Monday Niters arrive. Well, why? Because these 10 guys are "special" and they watch closely to see if their "spe- cial treat or seat" is await- ing them. They will get "their way" on this Monday night! No oversight will be tolerated and every omis- sion will be highlighted. It must be right.

Rain or shine, September to December, at 8:00 p.m. exactly, the Monday Nite footballers finally arrive. They enter as if they "own" the place. And they look immediately to see if their "special request" has been honored. And, the two event planners breathe a sigh of relief. Good or bad. Hap- py or sad. "The Monday Niters are here, and all is well. Now, to answer the first question-what happens.

There's always conver- sation. Ceaseless talk. On and on. It's a wonder that it can all be digested, amid

all the activity. The "ne- cessary" argument. The planning. The eatings. The sleeping. The sharing. The harassing. The formal bus- iness. It all goes on "all at once". And, few, if any, really try to understand it all. Why bother, it's Mon- day Nite!

And right in the midst of all "the clatter and chat- ter," comes a made-for- a-king meal, ornamented with several surprise ad- ditions (that only the lady knew about). Magic! Every one stops to admire how special Monday Nite foot- ball really is-as mani- fested by the multi-course, color coded, well-planned feast. Wow-how fitting!

And, after the first taste, the conversation resumes. Why did Big Mac put Car- son in a kickoff returner? Testaverde will win the Heisman. Redskins are on their way to the playoffs. That AFC West is the tough- est division. Chiefs' de- fense, and particularly Tim Cofield, played well. Al Michaels and Frank Gif- ford do well as announcers. Jim Brown, Gayle Sayers and Walter Payton are the best the NFL has ever seen. And Northeast was again beaten by Lincoln Acade- my.

By now, everything is in full swing and the hostess has retired.

And the host has joined the rest-talking. In fact, it's hard to tell the host from the guests. Then, as host looks around at the empty tv trays, he is re- minded and "pleased" that it was all worth it. Glad and happy-it's all over for another year.

About now, it's way after 11 p.m. Where did every- body go? What happened to all that food? And, by the way, who won the foot- ball game? Football game? What football game? To- night was a Monday Nite football meeting. And that's all that really mattered.

Members of the Monday Niters are: Charles Al- len, D.D.S., C. Ashley, Paul Booker, Tom Brown, Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Jr., Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nun- nelly, Jerry Price and Ed West.

MEET THE MONDAY NITERS . . . These guys have been watching football together for over 30 years each Monday Night for as long as there has been a Monday Night Football telecast. Most of what we do is talk, talk, talk, not only about football but social issues, educational goals, and a lot about family. Sometimes we argue, some more than others, but always leave as friends. Football is only a reason to get together, and each Monday night is another opportunity. Here they are, as I see (and hear) them:

Dr. Charles Allen - a private dentist who is extremely dedicated to his patients. Usually quiet and mild mannered. Charles is a Chiefs fan no matter what their record. In 30 years, he remains relentless in helping the Chiefs re-achieve the glory of years past.

Paul Booker - another quiet, unassuming, likable kind of guy. Has helped many young people add structure to their lives. More importantly, is a staunch pragmatist who always calls it like he sees it. Has called the Chiefs heartless and devoid of leadership. Currently leads all Monday Niters in predictions.

Carl Clark - a high school quarterback and a sports car enthusiast. He is quick witted and has many friends in high places. Used to be undaunting in his support for the Chiefs but lately has turned to being practical in his predictions. Is currently a leader in accurate predictions.

Ike Gardner - a retired educator and administrator with the Kansas City, Mo., School district. Prides himself in knowing about football and a host of other subjects. While criticizing the Chiefs and voting against them often, he is still very much a Chiefs fan. A long-time season ticket holder, he blends his sports time with love for his church and pastor, the Rev. Emanuel Cleaver.

Lloyd Greenfield - a retired firefighter, now a real estate professional. Is helping people achieve their life-long goal of home ownership. Lloyd is a practical fan, who just loves the game of football, sometimes quiet and a very deep thinker with a fondness for the arts.

Ollie Hubbard - loves to make original commentary, especially relating his predictions to real life experiences. A good cook and a walking newspaper who knows lot of Kansas City people. Also a former educator, who uses his winning ways to get things done. Is very comfortable not working every day.

Jim Nunnelly - one of the few Monday Niters who is still working. Likes his job as the COMBAT administrator and tries to get addicts needed treatment. He also likes his role on the KPRS Hot 103 JAMZ Generation Rap show as “Grand Dad.”

Ed West - a computer ace who helps people set up their home computers. Almost made it to professional football following a collegiate career at Pittsburg State university. Fascinated by electronics and football. Clearly a gadget fan that likes helping all people. Knowledgeable.

So, there you have the guys who week after week think and talk football. More important, they all love the game of football and never tire of talking about it. We thank each of you for sharing our experience through this column. Our best to you this Holiday season.

Chatter, Conversation & Commentary

The Monday Nite Footballers include current events as part of their weekly agenda. This week the group wanted to discuss the Million Man March in Washington, D.C. Toward that end, the group invited Terry Riley, well known community activist to share his experiences at the Oct. 16 event. Riley, along with Emanuel Cleaver III, drove 16 hours to attend the daylong march. Spending the night in northeast Virginia, Riley was introduced to such dignitaries as Washington Mayor Marion Barry.

During the discussion, Riley asked the Monday Niters “what should be do now that the march is over?” The following quotes are responses to his insightful question:

Charles Allen, D.D.S., “I was quite disappointed that neither Colin Powell nor O.J. Simpson attended. We should forget the messenger and act on the message.”

Paul Booker, “We should motivate the youngsters to work hard to reduce black on black crime. Family members need to do the right thing.”

Carl Clark, “The ones who went (to Washington) have to practice what they preach, i.e., eliminate domestic violence and join church. The time to do that is now.”

Ike Gardner, “We must put into action the philosophies espoused at the historic march.”

Lloyd Greenfield, “We have to re-evaluate the concept of education and realize that education is ongoing. Part of that education includes the black man returning to his family.“

Ollie Hubbard, “We must continue to show the same self discipline and self respect displayed at the march.”

Jim Nunnelly, “We must support all ongoing positive efforts. Substance abuse, child safety, neighborhood restoration, economic development and voter registration must be continually bolstered by the presence and contribution by African American men. For those seeking a place to help, call me at the COMBAT offices, 881-3814.”

Harry Sypert, “Hopefully, like the civil rights marches in the 1960s, the Million Man March will re-energize and resurrect the spirit.”

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

Clay Johnson, Home Again

Note: The following column was written Dec. 16) Kansas City, Columbia, Toronto, Topeka, Evans-ville, Seattle, Portland, Los Angeles and Kansas City again. Clay Johnson has played in all of these cities. As the saying goes: all roads lead to home.

Clay started here at Man-ual high school as an all-stater under Coach Bill Madison and after stints in each of those cities, he comes home as a profes-sional with the local CBA franchise, the Kansas City Sizzlers.

Clay seems to have landed in Kansas City. Not only is coming home a chal-lenge, but his performance with the Sizzlers has been praiseworthy, though not awesome.

But before we examine Clay’s performance with the Sizzlers, let’s look back and see Clay’s career as he sees it.

First his most significant event was his playing days with the LA Lakers. Though not a starter, he did play with the 1982-1983 cham-pionship team; Jamaal Wilkes, Norm Nixon, Magic Johnson, Kareem Abdul-Jabbar. Clay loved the Ca-lifornia life.

Though he felt "good" in California, he felt most productive at the CBA fran-chise in Billings, Montana in 1982, before joining the Lakers.

"I really became a pro at the guard spot there in Billings. I average 22 points a game." Because of that performance and other things, he was chosen to go to the Lakers.

Clay, at 6'4" and 185, also played at University of Missouri, after an out-standing junior college ca-reer at Penn Valley in 1975-1876. Clay was best known for his rebounding then.

Now, he’s back home with the newest CBA franshise, the Kansas City Sizzlers. How has Clay Johnson III performed at home.

He says proudly, "my best game, pointwise was the overtime La Crosse game on December 15."

In that game he scored the winning basket in over-time and hit 19 points and grabbed nine rebounds.

Overall, he seems to be a trail of thirds:

Third in scoring with 13.3 average;

Third in assists with 15;

Third in steals with 6;

Third in field goal per-centage with .478 average;

Third in free throws with .750;

Third in slam dunks with three.

Clay seems to be realistic when it comes to his chan-ces on returning to the Na-tional Basketball Associ-ation. "If the NBA comes, I’ll go. I just want to make a strong hometown con-tribution to the game I love."

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Starting with a few long-time friends watching football together on television, the group came into existence with the onset of ABC-TV's Monday Night Football program. Founding members were Carl Clark, Isaac Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, Oscar Jamerson (now of Cherry Hill, N.J.), Walt Livingston and Stewart Watson (now of St. Louis).

The group officially began in 1970 and except for one Monday night in all those years, has met to socialize, lend support and talk sports. Each member is required to entertain at least one Monday night each year during the pro football season.

Out of the association, other events have evolved:

(a) Super Bowl party every year; (b) Donations to worthy causes, like the Niles Homes; (c) an annual New Year's Eve Party; (d) a bi-annual football trip to St. Louis Governor's Cup Game; (e) weekly sports column in the Kansas City CALL; (f) an annual dinner honoring the ladies around Valentine's Day.

Because the group has a divine interest in academics, the group has always supported the concept that a high school athlete must do also well in the classroom. They believe that tougher grade points should be required before any athlete is allowed to play sports.

Members of the group are: Ed West; Jerry Price, Jim Nunnelly; Ollie Hubbard; Isaac Gardner, Jr.; Carl Clark; Tom Brown; Paul Booker; C. Ashley; and Charles Allen, D.D.S.

Monday Nite Footballers Get

Ready For Mystical Season

No one knows what these Chiefs are going to do this season. Everyone is so righteously caught up in the training camp move to St. Joseph, as if where a team practices wins football games. Nah, it ain’t happening. It still counts what happens on the field, starting the second week in September. But, we Monday Niters are admittedly getting swooped up by the hype.

As the guys gather every Friday at a local club, the Chiefs and the 2010 season dominates the discussion. Of course, the oldies but goodies music helps them reminisce about the good old days, when the Chiefs really were Chiefs. . . not Chefs. But here is some of the pre-season chatter going on at 69th and Prospect.

TRIVIA QUESTION FOR THE WEEK: What teams played in the last Super Bowl?

1) Ed West is steadily teaching his fellow Monday Niters about technology. Armed with a i-phone, Ed is able to respond quickly to any football question that needs a quick answer. Where there once was arguments, now it is a simple...talk-into-the-phone and within a few seconds, there is a bona fide answer. He also got Carl to buy one of those nifty phones. And Ike even bought a laptop. So did Nunnelly. And Booker is actually going to Best Buy every week to look at one.

2) All the talk this week is about the upcoming weekend Hall of Fame game. You know the great Jerry Rice, Emmitt Smith and Floyd Little are being inducted into the NFL Hall of Fame. What else can be said about Jerry Rice that hasn’t been said? That is a heck of a thing..that Hall of Fame in Canton, Ohio. Carl Clark, Dr. Charles Allen, Lloyd Greenfield, and Ike Gardner have attended ceremonies there in years past. They recommend it for everyone.

By the way, Floyd Little wore number 44, just like Jim Brown at Syracuse. He was a Pro-Bowler for five years, while in Denver. Those were the days when the Broncos were the doormat of the AFC. He waited 30 years to get to the Hall of Fame. Good things do come to those who wait.

3) This week’s Hall of Fame game is between the Cincinnati Bengals and the Dallas Cowboys. You might just see Romo hook up with TO. Maybe. And for sure, you will see whether Ocho Cinco still has it. Probably not. That game is Sunday night.

4) Been up to St. Joseph to the Chiefs’ training camp? If you go, pay for your parking. Do not take the “free parking” option. You will walk for miles, getting to the state-of-the-art training facilities. You will be moved by the university’s football field, as well as the Chief’s new digs. Matter of fact, the Chiefs’ store is in a tent, designed for you to visit....whether you want to or not. And given the heat, any additional walking is not good. Remember, take the paying parking. Or else you will be sorry.

ANSWER TO THE TRIVIA QUESTION: Well, we know it wasn’t the Chiefs. But, it was New Orleans Saints and the Indianapolis Colts. The Saints beat the Favre-led Vikings and the Colts beat the Jets to get to the big game. By the way, look for the Colts to make Peyton Manning the highest paid football player ever. He is one of the few quarterbacks who has started every game since joining the NFL. Big, big bucks are on the way, Peyton.

Roberta Flack And Monday Niters: ‘Killing Me Softly’

Y’all remember Roberta Flack. She was quite the talk, back in the ‘60s and ‘70s. Her hit song, “Killing Me Softly”, found its way into all of our hearts and homes. But she sang another song, which is so appropriate for our “sinking fast” Chiefs. That song will be revealed after we talk a little about the New Orleans Saints, the Chiefs’ next opponent on Sunday.

First of all, the Saints came into being as an NFL expansion team, back in the late ‘60s. Their rise to Super Bowl status has been phenomenal, especially since they had a rough, rough start. Some insensitive sportswriters once called them the “Aints”, owing to their winlessness. Now years later, we all have forgotten about those lean days and nowadays, always look to the Saints to be a contender to win the NFC South Division. Routinely now, they always play well against Division opponents like Atlanta, Carolina and Tampa Bay. But as of today, the Saints have not won a game, so far, this season. Some say it is the atmosphere. Recently, the Saints found themselves deep in controversy after the well-publicized bounty scheme. That fiasco left them without their head coach, Sean Payton, for all of this season. That penalty may well explain why the Saints are off to a rough start.

But, what about the Chiefs? What can they say to explain another “shaky” beginning? What can the Chiefs do to make it all better? Actually, we, Monday Niters, believe the Saints will recover soon and start making championship plans. But not so for the Chiefs. Why is that so, you may ask. Well, this is where we talk again about Roberta Flack.

Her “other” hit song was “The Closer I Get To You”. In that song, she is happy with the progress of her rising relationship. In fact, she proudly comments that her lover makes her “see” things more clearly. Well, the “closer” we get to the Chiefs, the more we “see” how inept, how inadequate these bunch of bums are. Starting with the coaches, the front office and their regrettable quarterback, Matt Cassel. You don’t have to “get close” to see that these Chiefs are never gonna rise up. They are never gonna win our division again. They can stay here another 50 years and it will all be same. Full of excuses. Full of explanations. And full of crap.

And crap it is, no matter how close you get. In fact, we should start referring to them as the “Aints”. Because they aint gonna never be nothing. So for Sunday’s game, it will be the Saints vs. the new Aints. And you don’t have to be “close” to predict the outcome. Roberta Flack would sang another song about the Chiefs. The farther I get from you, the better I seem to be.

Tribute To A Monday Niter

Bunny Rabbit: A True Brother Who Had No Brothers

Stewart Watson was a big, giant of a guy, who always wore a smile and had a baritone-like laugh. He was “at home” whereever he went, whether in his favorite club on Prospect or whether meeting you for the first time. He clearly will be missed by all of us Monday Niters for he was one of the founding members of this adult “boys club”.

To most of us, he had been friends for all of his 24,582 days on this earth. But on July 3, of this year, “Bunny Rabbit” left this earth and thus, the basic reason for this column.

In short, we want to say “good bye” in a way that only friends who have known you a lifetime, can say it. So let’s start with “Bunny Rabbit” and his relentless use of nicknames.

“Bunny Rabbit” had a nickname for everyone in our group. That’s because he wanted each of us to know that he liked each of us individually and collectively. Ollie was “Mr. Puddy Pot” and Ike was “Huff”. Booker was “Chocolate Drop”. Dr. Allen was “Pumpkin” and Carl was appropriately called “Mr. Mary Lynn”. And because of my bald head, he started calling me “Mr. Peanut”. I dare say he would have to stop and think of our real names because that was all he called us, every day of his nearly 69 years. We say nearly because he died just three days before his 69th birthday. And there was no denying him access to you. Even though he moved to St. Louis some years ago, he still managed to see everyone at least once a month or just calling them.

Speaking of calling, “Bunny Rabbit” was a phone junkie. Night or day, winter or fall, he would call one of us, out of the clear blue sky. It was as if we were his connection to reality or maybe he just wanted to stay close to his friends. Always the same, joy-induced greeting, “ what u doing boy?”. And then, right from there, the word would spread... “Bunny Rabbit” is in town and somehow, unplanned though it may be, there would be a reunion, right then and there. And it seemed he would always time his visit during one of our Monday Nite Footballers meeting and whatever the conversation on the floor, would give way to his one-man, nickname-calling comedy act. “Bunny Rabbit” was a real friend and the kind of brother to everyone, even though he had no biological brothers or sisters. He exuded brotherhood, at its highest level.

In addition, “Bunny Rabbit” was a lesson in kindness and consideration. He reminded us that we all must live our lives, merrily and among friends. He demonstrated that by always dropping by and diverting you from your daily worries and strife. Yes, Bunny Rabbit was a spark of life to all of us and his light still shines in our thoughts and in our conversation. Ike says “Bunny Rabbit” always checked on him whether sick or well. And Ollie says that he has great memories of “Bunny” all the way from elementary school through Lincoln university.

But, Dr. Charles Allen concluded the stories about “Bunny Rabbit” best.

He simply said that “Bunny Rabbit” extended a hand but he always touched our hearts. What else needs to be said but “Good Bye, ‘Bunny Rabbit’. We are all better off by knowing you and we will wear our nicknames proudly, in your honor.”

Kerry Washington, The Golden Rule

And The 9-2 Chiefs

Long before Kerry Washington became “a Thursday night must-see”, she starred along side Jamie Foxx in the 2004 major flick, “Ray”.

In fact, she played Ray Charles’ wife and stood firm and steady, all through Ray Charles’ ups and downs and yes, even a nasty heroin addiction. We all knew then that Kerry would rise to the top, especially since she possesses the ability to use “all of her” in her acting craft. Eyes. Nose, Forehead. Eyebrows.Facial creases. This woman exudes totality and entirety. Everything about her makes the scene advance. When acting, Kerry rules.

Speaking of rules, let’s talk a minute about the Golden Rule. While many think this is a biblical expression, it really came along, long before Christianity. It simply says “do unto others as you would have them do unto you”. Well, the Chiefs will have a chance to do just that against Denver this Sunday. Beaten badly in the “mile-high” city two weeks ago, the Broncos will come riding into Arrowhead, after a horse-whipping from the New England Patriots last Sunday. Yes, Denver jumped out to a 24-0 lead, only to fall to the “totally magic arm” of Tom Brady. Eventually, the Patriots prevailed with a record-breaking comeback, in the second half. The first half, all Denver. The second half, it was all New England.

That brings us to the Chiefs and Denver. Both teams are vying for the AFC West and a guarantee of home-field advantage in the playoffs. And really, it’s much more.

Kansas City has to prove that it can “hang” with the best teams, a second time. Against San Diego, the offense finally showed up and the defense went to sleep. Jamaal Charles ran like crazy, while Hali and Houston fell to injuries.

And more important, “AWOL Alex” Smith threw for nearly 300 yards. Where in the hell have you been, Alex?

Bottom line, the Chiefs will need everything and everybody to beat Denver, especially since Denver was” totally embarrassed” before God and country.

Like Kerry Washington, the Chiefs will need to express themselves, just like Smith finally did.

Otherwise, the Kansas City fans will consider it a “scandal” and turn the tv off, reminding us of the days-gone-by when they lost all the time. Ahhhh. An idea. Let’s call in Olivia Pope and see if she can fix this scandal or do something to make it right.

No, not just yet. Let’s give the Chiefs one more chance, to put together a complete game, where the offense moves the ball and the defense rides the bucking Broncos. Faith. Faith. Faith. Just a little more faith. And a lot of the Golden Rule.

Research Benefits, Old

Times And The 7-0 Chiefs

This past Monday night, the mood was different than usual. There was no ranting and raving because the Chiefs are winning 7-0. Ike Gardner is now voting for the Chiefs to win. Carl Clark weakly says that the Chiefs will lose, eventually. Sounds boring? No, and here’s why.

We spent a lot of time talking about this research tax, coming up November 5. Right away, the tone moved seriously. For each of being over 70, realized firsthand how research and public oversight has advanced our personal lives and the African American community.

More importantly, each of us has had some major encounter with our health, where advanced medicine has saved our butts. Heart attacks. Stents. Gout. Diabetes. Just like everyone else.

Yes, how can anyone be against something when it offers so much to our community? Yes it is a tax. But the benefits outweigh the tax. Maybe we won’t benefit for any more research. But our children and grandchildren will. African Americans are living longer and better. . .That’s because the research always says we are disproportionately affected by all these ills, like diabetes, AIDS, heart failure, strokes. So every time your doctor tells you that because you are African American, you are more likely to suffer more. That’s research and we are living longer, as a result.

So, we Monday Niters know. . .the more research, the more doctors, the more job opportunities, the longer and healthier our people become. Case closed. Let’s now look in on the Chiefs who are looking and sounding like the Chiefs of old. Even cantankerous Ike admits that. Here’s some of the other discussion: saluted Joe C. Meriweather for his contributions to Kansas City. . .Warren Moon, Hall of Fame quarterback from Houston, once played for the Chiefs...We remembered Pancho King, former KU basketball player, who played for the Kansas City Steers. . .Mizzou is 7-0 and on top of the SEC east division. . .Kemper Arena was a mistake. . . Otis Taylor, former All-Pro receiver for the Chiefs, was once part-owner in a nightclub, called Flankers. . .Municipal Auditorium in 1979 was where Darryl Dawkins shattered the backboard, with Bill Robizine, basketball great here, looking on. . .Cleaver checks in from Washington. He is expected to attend the Urban Summit’s Young Professionals event coming up October 30. . .No one fell asleep tonight. . .Thank God for the discussion about the research tax and we all said “yes” unanimously.

However, we all do expect the Chiefs to win number 8, against Cleveland Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Here’s why:

John Legend, Paul Booker And The 9-0 Chiefs

John Legend wrote and dedicated a song to his recent bride. In that song, he just pours out his heart, telling her that “all of me loves all of you….your edges, your curves, and your perfect imperfections”. John Legend is saying that he realizes her love has some shortcomings, but he loves her deeply just the same.

Now that’s just what most of us feel about our Chiefs. They have a perfect record but most of us are willing to live with their “perfect imperfections”. Some people even say the Chiefs are a fluke and are just waiting for them to show how imperfect they really are. But Paul Booker isn’t one of them. He’s been voting for the Chiefs all year long, despite voting against them all last year.

But Paul is more supportive this year. Always a Chiefs’ supporter ever since he came to town, way back in 1966. A veteran of the Air Force and a graduate of Lincoln university, Paul is a very likeable educator, having taught at Woodland, Switzer, Southeast Middle, Greenwood and C.A. Franklin schools. And he is generally healthy and is recovering graciously from the loss of his wife a few months ago. So these days, he is just trying to “maintain” and keep going. So the Chiefs are giving him the “perfect boost” that he needs.

Paul knows the Chiefs lack a consistent offense, even though the defensive unit is actually carrying the team. In spite of that, Paul expects the Chiefs to beat Denver on Sunday night. Yes, along with Ed West, Greenfield, Dr. Allen, Mrs. Banks and Ollie, Paul is predicting a long-shot victory over the Manning-led Broncos. But his former boss, Ike Gardner and his 6902 buddy, Carl Clark, believes the Chiefs will succumb and lose, out in the mile-high city Paul and the other Chiefs supporters are thinking 10-0 and a sure-shot for the playoffs and maybe even the Western Division championship.

But, not so fast. Ike and Carl are quick to point out the Chiefs’ imperfections. They say Manning will get rushed, even sacked a couple of times..but he will burn the “imperfect” secondary all night.

Conversely, both Booker and Cleaver remember the 100 yard interception return by Sean Smith and say proudly that the “perfect defense” will more than make up for any flaws in the Chiefs’ secondary. In fact, they say that “special D” has held all opponents to less than seventeen points, throughout all nine games.

All in all, all of us love all of the Chiefs. And we know it will be something to behold, if the Chiefs win it all. And if they don’t, we will still support them, in spite of their “imperfectness”. The Denver Broncos will be playing hard because they didn’t expect such an upsurge from the Chiefs. So, all eyes will be on the Chiefs, this Sunday night. National television. Suspect commentators. Possible championships. And like John Legend, we hope that those edges and imperfections don’t show up. At least, not Sunday night:

Romeo’s Leadership, Community, And The Chiefs

This past Monday night, we talked a whole lot. A lot went down this week. Especially with the Chiefs’ victory and the murder-suicide tragedy piercing the nation’s headlines. But, before we get to that, we had some community-minded guests to join us. One was Tony G from Hot 103 and the others were the Go-Along Club and Andre Jackmon, a supervisor in the Behavioral Health department at Swope Health Services. Thus, we want to tell you a little about them and then comment on Romeo’s leadership, through a double-crisis week with his team.

• TONY G. . . for 19years, this young man has led the Toy and Food Drive for all of Kansas City. For this effort, he has been awarded boundless recognition but he still presents as eager as if it’s the very first time.

Many don’t know but Tony G was a credible basketball player in the old Catholic leagues. Moreover, his name carried forwarded to the radio airways and he is featured each morning in “drive time”, delivering a morning start for thousands of metropolitan residents. He has also assisted in the development of the popular teen talk show, Generation Rap on Hot 103. But for Tony, this annual food event is just being a good neighbor. Having grown up near 39th and Prospect, Tony G just gives back to his community. In fact, he demonstrates his commitment year after year, making sure that families have food and kids have toys for Christmas. Big kudos for Tony G.

• THE GO ALONG CLUB. . . This is group of middle-aged men who get together frequently. However, social-minded the group may appear, they raise thousands of dollars each year for college scholarships. They too were on-hand to talk about their community service, especially providing worthy students with money to go to college....raised through their own corporate resources. We cannot say enough about the men who help our community...each and every day.

• ANDRE JACKSON, SWOPE HEALTH SERVICES. . . starting out as just a worker, Andre is now a supervisor in the Behavioral Health department. Andre is involved in establishing counseling and case management services, in and around schools. So far, he has mobilized many agencies and most important, helped his agency get services to those who need it. Andre is one of the rising young men in our community who defy the inappropriate stereotype of young black men. Andre called on us Monday Niters and the others to assist our children, even if just saying an encouraging word. He reiterated that our young people want and need our help.

Well, what a night for Monday Niters. . . to hear and see our men, stepping up to help out in their community. Also, there was a lot of conversation about the Chiefs. Mainly, about Romeo and how he handled a team, wrought with confusion caused by the suicide of one of the their fellow players. One that also included a murder of the player’s girlfriend. And one that required getting that team ready to play a game, the next day. So, we took a poll among our members and guests and commented on Romeo Crennel’s finest moment in his profession. On Sunday, Romeo will take his team back to his former place of employment, Cleveland. Where he once coached the Browns and now will return a national hero. Here’s what we think:

Super Bowl XLIII:

Arm Vs. Hammer

Amid all the opulent commercials, the teams on the field were not expected to be in Tampa Sunday. Oh yea, all of you AFC fans ere thinking that New England would rise again.

It’s Friday and we are still gloating over the Chiefs victory over Denver, last Sunday. It seems like it was a dream and soon we will all wake up to reality. . . that the Chiefs will return to their losing ways, this Sunday against Carolina. By the way, did you notice how ol” Herm hugged Larry Johnson after the last Kansas City touchdown. He whispered in Larry’s ear “thanks for saving my job, man”. And then later, Carl Peterson came out of his suite and said essentially the same...”you gave me one more year, LJ..I apologize for putting you on the bench”.

No doubt about it LJ saved the day for these two. Already the St. Louis Rams have fired their coach. And guess who is next if we don’t play well against Carolina? But last Monday night, we spent most of our time talking about the how our old-time teachers influenced us. Thus, we remembered them.

Donnelly College Offers

Scholarship Information Night

Donnelly Colege will offer a Scholarship Information Night on Monday, Feb. 16, from 6:30-8:30 p.m., in the first floor Meeting Room. Participants need to make a reservation by calling (913) 621-8700.

At the session, students will learn how to get free money to attend Donnelly College. Presenters will explain how to apply for Presidential and Bloch Scholarships which cover tuition, fees and books. They will also learn about federal Pell Grants and Kansas comprehensive Grants for low-income students. All students with a GPA of 2.5 or above, who participate in the Scholarship Night, will be awarded a minimum scholarship of $500 to be used at Donnelly College for tuition not covered by grants.

“Limited financial resources are no obstacle for attending college,” said Sister Mary Agnes Patterson, Donnelly Vice-President. “There are grants and scholarships available for those who qualify. And starting at a local college like Donnelly saves students the cost of living in a dorm and allows them to work part-time.”

SPT. Monday niters

Bright Lights, Big City And The 9-1 Chiefs

Do you remember that old time blues song, “Bright Lights, Big City”?

Well, this was a song that was sung by a man from a small town, who was crying about his girlfriend, who had gone to the “big city” and no longer needed or loved him. In fact, the song says….”bright lights and big city, they went to my baby’s head”.

And that’s exactly what happened to the Chiefs last Sunday night. All the hype and national headlines just messed the Chiefs up. Here’s a 2-14 team last year, hearing daily how they lost all last year and now are on top of the entire National Football League and undefeated on top of that.

That was just too much for the Chiefs to handle. They got caught up in the “bright lights” and lost badly to Denver. Now, let’s hope they return to normal and beat the 4-6 San Diego Chargers. Now, that the lights have been turned off, let’s see if they can win number 10 out at Arrowhead Sunday.

But big praise should go to Ike Gardner and Carl Clark, for seeing through all the hype, They knew the Chiefs would fold, with the eyes of the world on them. So, we all had to kiss their rings for seeing through the “offense-less” Chiefs. .

So the song, “Bright Lights, Big City” set off a conversation about the singers of the fifties, sixties and seventies. It was remarkable how sharp these old minds were in remembering all the artists of days gone by.

Some of those top names were, Sonny Thompson, Lula Reed, Ruth Brown, Etta James “Annie Had A Baby” by Hank Ballard and the Midnighters, ”I Only Have Eyes For You” by the Flamingoes, Lou Rawls, Johnny Taylor and Sam Cooke all former lead singers from the gospel group, the Soul Stirrers, Fontella Bass, Little Willie John and “Fever”, Ike and Tina Turner, Jackie Wilson’s “Lonely Teardrops”, Fats Domino and The Dominoes, The Moonglows. and on and on.

Before you knew it, we were all drifting back so far…that we either fell asleep or forgot we came to watch a football game and talk about the Chiefs and their first loss. Then we woke up and remembered that we had to make our predictions. Here’s how we think Sunday’s game will go.

Nat ‘King’ Cole, The Christmas

Song, And The 10-3 Chiefs

For those of you under 50, you probably missed a treat, not hearing all the songs ever recorded by Nat “King”Cole.

Every year about this time, that smooth, velvety voice makes its way into our ears, hearts, and mind. And for us over fifty, we recall that Ol’ King Cole was the very first African American to host his own tv show. Now, that’s something. Belting out songs like, “You Send Me” and of course, no one can forget, “Unforgettable”.

But, the song that keeps on giving and selling is “The Christmas Song”. Now you may like Mary J. Blige and Johnny Mathis and even Donnie Hathaway. But none can top Ol’ King Cole when it comes to validating the coming of Christmas.

Now for us in Kansas City, we are splitting our time between getting ready for Christmas and getting our Chiefs ready for the playoffs. Baltimore and Miami are like us, waiting for the final word that they are “in the running for the February Super Bowl”. But teams places like Oakland and Tennessee and even Atlanta are singing along, with Nat King Cole and his perennial, “Christmas Song”. That’s all they can sing about, through now.

So for us, the song ought to be a reminder that the 10-3 Chiefs are back and have recovered from a dismal last year. Most important, we have proved that Booker and Cantankerous Ike Gardner wrong. They said the Chiefs would not win more than five games this and already they have doubled that. So, I wish they could create a Christmas Song that would memorialize those kind of doubters. Perhaps we could call it, “Wrong Way Ike” and, “Poor, Pitiful Paul”.

But thank God for the rest who have/had faith in the Kansas City Chiefs. They can now say proudly that “yes, there is a Santa Claus” and croon along with Nat “King” Cole, at the same time. It’s beginning to look a lot like Christmas. Let’s hear from the rest of the guys.

Chiefs Have Taken Us Too Far, Too Fast, With Too Little

Listening to the Chiefs' front office might lead you to believe that

the Chiefs are going to the Super Bowl. Wait a minute. Grant you, the

Chiefs are in first place in the Western Division of the American

Conference. And yes, they have more wins than losses (4-3). And for

you anti-Larry Johnson fans, the venerable Priest Holmes is back (at

least for now). And on an even brighter note, Dwayne Bowe is making

the Chiefs look good on their first-round draft choice. But, let's get

real here.

My advice to the Chiefs is "Don't take us too far, too fast, with too little".

We are not even half-way through the season and we are shouting, as if the Chiefs have already achieved something. Think about it.

In November, the Chiefs still must face Green Bay and Denver at home and the undefeated Colts in Indianapolis. Duh! Now, the shouting

should tone down a little. And after our discussion last Monday night,

there are great differences among us, as to how the Chiefs will fare,

between now and Thanksgiving.

Head Hater Ike Gardner and "that" Booker thinks the Chiefs will lose

all three of those games. That means that by the time we light up the

Plaza, the Chiefs would have slipped to 4-6. But Carl Clark is not

going that far. He thinks that the Chiefs will beat Denver and lose to

Green Bay and the Colts. And just to give balance to the discussion, Ed

West and Lloyd Greenfield think the Chiefs will beat both Green Bay and

Denver and lose only to the Colts. They think the Chiefs will be 6-4

by the time we all sit down to Thanksgiving dinner. Now, the question is

"who's right".

Are the Chiefs taking us "too far, too fast, with too little". As

old-time fans, we've been down that yellow-brick road with the Chiefs

before. Win a few games and get us all excited and then, in the end,

fall slowly to the bottom. But this time, Lloyd and Ed are right on.

The Chiefs will win at Arrowhead and lose in Indy.

That being said, pass the mashed potatoes, please.

When high-powered, highly-paid coaches sign on to become a head coach in the NFL,

there are lots of neat little “perks” that find their way into a contract.

For example, there are additional dollars added, when a team makes the playoffs or avoid a losing season. Well, for the Chiefs, Vermeil and company will not be in the hunt

for the playoffs. But, if they win the next two games, that could mean “bigger bucks”

for the coaches. For while they would not have had a winning season, it would not be

a losing season, either.

This is just a lesson in the “small print” of these big-time contracts. Then, too, Vermeil has some making up to do both with the fans and specifically, with Larry Johnson. This boy can run that ball, equal to or better than Priest Holmes. And Vermeil, because of some personal issues with Johnson, kept Johnson out of competition, even though he was a high-round, draft choice. And to see Johnson perform like he has lately, Vermeil has to save face and let Larry run around, through, and over Oakland. Look for Larry and the Chiefs to save Dick Vermeil’s -- (job). More important, a full stadium on Christmas Day will help save any threatened decrease in season ticket sales. On the other hand, the Chiefs should not expect the fans to pay for an increase in ticket prices. For really, this season has been mediocre, at best. Chiefs 40, Raiders 17.
