SPT- Monday Night Footballer

Shakespeare, Some Common Quotes, And The Chiefs

When the Chiefs are losing, we search hard for something to talk about. And since the Chiefs have won just one game, we found ourselves talking about something odd. . . William Shakespeare, the best known playwright in the English language.

Now, this genius wrote thirty eight plays and many poems, as far back as five hundred years ago. Besides the Bible, his works are quoted more than any other form of literature. His most notable plays include Macbeth, Othello, Richard III, and Midsummer Night’s Dream. And Shakespeare was quite versatile. . . having wrote comedies, histories, character studies and a lot about politics. What would Shakespeare have said about the Chiefs? We will find out, after looking closer at Sunday’s game against the barely-alive Oakland Raiders.

First, a little about Oakland. Like the Chiefs, the Raiders were part of the old American Football League, starting way back in 1960. Right away, the Chiefs and the Raiders became rivals, playing some real close games both at Municipal Stadium and the “Black Hole”. And somewhere along the line, the games would end up with a fight or two. Commonly called the “Silver and Black”, the Raiders have struggled in recent years. But they have won three Super Bowls, way back in 1976, 1980, and 1987.

Surprisingly, they also have 13 players in the illustrious Football Hall Of Fame, in Canton, Ohio. But all that won’t matter when they come marching into Arrowhead at noon on Sunday. The Chiefs’ fans will heckle the pirate-dressed Raiders’ fans and “there will be no peace”. The Chiefs will be trying to beat their division rival because they have only beaten San Diego this year. And Oakland will be trying to remain off the bottom, where the Chiefs currently reside. So while no championship will be at stake, it promises to be a first-rate battle. Shakespeare would have spoken a special quote for this meaningless game. “To be or not to be, that is the question.”

In response, the Monday Niters actually applied many of the famous quotes from Shakespeare to their predictions. Some of them are quite creative, so please applaud these old men for paying attention in school. Ike actually took a college class in Shakespeare. And Loyal Lloyd has acted in a Shakespeare play or two. And Congressman Cleaver has used Shakespeare as part of his many sermons. While the rest of us have unknowingly quoted Shakespeare hundreds of times. So sit back and enjoy finding out that Shakespeare is all around us, even on a Monday night, at a boring football meeting.

Paul Booker, “Romeo, Romeo, where art thou?” Raiders by 10

Carl Clark, Thou has to win, sometimes. Chiefs by 3

Ike Gardner, “Full of sound and fury, signifying nothing”. Raiders by 10

Lloyd Greenfield, “Victoria’s blood, it warms thy heart”. Chiefs by 3

Ollie Hubbard, “Expectations is the root of all heartaches” Chiefs by 3

Ed West, “Not to be, that is the answer”. Raiders by 10

The Hubbards, Opinions,

And The Chiefs

The Monday Niters are full of themselves. They freely share their opinions and don’t care if anyone agrees with them or not. And we’ll share some of those community-related opinions, right after we comment about the Hubbard family and their cooking talents.

This last Monday night was their time to host the Monday Niters. As usual, there was a feast. African cabbage, red beans and rice, chicken wings, catfish, spaghetti, green beans and potatoes, cole slaw, cornbread and rolls. Even their lovely daughter, Kim, dropped by to taste the Hubbard Buffet, But, there was something different tis week.

Usually, Ollie does all the cooking. But this time, it was his sassy wife, Sandra, who did it all. Yes, Sandra. Now, she is no stranger to the culinary arts. Her late mother was truly a great cook. In fact, in Macon, Missouri, Mrs. McElroy was a well-known caterer throughout north central Missouri.

Now, if you don’t know where Macon is. It near Clarence, Missouri. Don’t know where that is. It’s near LaPlata, Still don’t know? Forget it. Neither do most people. But Sandra was the only black child in the school band. And she was the first black child born in the Macon County hospital. And she spent most of her days, living and thriving in an all-white world. But if you tasted her buffet on Monday, one would know she is truly an African American artist, when it comes to cooking.

Even Ike Gardner, the most opinionated African American alive, liked the red beans and rice. So did Ed West, who went back several times. But husband Ollie was present and offered his supervision to the whole process. Bottom line, the Hubbards did their thing, especially on Monday night. . . sharing their cooking talents and opinions.

Let’s start with Ollie and see what he and the other Monday Niters are thinking, about community events and the Chiefs.

Ollie Hubbard: Ollie is concerned about how all this violence is affecting our children. So, he is urging each of us to make sure our children stay in school for their own safety. But he is no longer concerned about the Chiefs. He says the Chiefs will kick the Giants’ butt, on Sunday. Chiefs by 10.

Dr. Charles Allen: Doc is still concerned about Alzheimer’s disease. In fact, he says that the toll is not only on the patients but on the care-givers, as well. Caregivers often think the patient may get better and deny the reality of getting worse. But he thinks the Chiefs are “not in denial”. He says Sunday will be a “Giant killer day” for the 3-0 Chiefs. Chiefs by 14.

Paul Booker: He thinks the city leaders are on the right track, by inviting leaders from other cities to discuss the crime problem. Recent visitors from Chicago are helping us address this violence thing. He feels the same way about the Chiefs’ defense. They will slaughter Eli Manning on Sunday. Chiefs by 3.

Carl Clark: He believes that the sentencing ought to fit the crime. Bad crime, long time in jail. Just that simple. Feeling the same way about the Chiefs. They will be criminal, against New York. Giants by 14.

Emanuel “Bubba” Cleaver: Cleaver just finished his favorite week in Washington, hosting the Legislative Black Caucus. Only a few know how significant he is in Washington. Everybody knows and respects Emanuel Cleaver. And he knows the Chiefs will be like David and slaughter the Goliaths. Chiefs by 10.

Ike “Mad Man” Gardner: Ike is concerned about the lack of jobs. He feels that jobs are the crux of our social structure. Known for freely expressing his opinion. Ike is right about the jobs but wrong about the game on Sunday at Arrowhead. He thinks the Chiefs’ fairy tale is over. Giants by 7.

Lloyd Greenfield: He believes that to be educated is the key to a successful life. And that we must not let education get lost in all the violence discussions. Like with the Chiefs, we must “keep on keeping on”. Chiefs by 3.

Jim “Granddad” Nunnelly: He is still reeling over the central city representation at the recent Mental Health speak-out. One-third of the participants were African American and they came through like champs, offering their insightful opinions freely. Congratulations to each of you that attended. Now, let’s not allow the Chiefs to get depressed again. Jamaal Charles and the defense will stop that from happening. Chiefs by 7

Ed West: He still harping on the police control thing. Plainly, the control of the police department should be left alone. Period. And look to the Chiefs defense (Poe, Hali and Houston) to shut Eli down. Chiefs by 10.

MONDAY NITERS

Beyonce, Giselle, Community

Issues And The Chiefs

Beyonce is known world-wide for her unforgettable songs. In fact, she has sold over 118 million copies, over a 15 year period. And she just turned 32 on Wednesday. Fit, pretty, and business-savvy, Beyonce has whaled out singles like “Irreplaceable” and “Baby Boy”.

But before we talk about how one of her songs relate to the Chiefs, let’s look in on the Monday Niters and see what they think about the issues in our community.

Dr. Charles Allen thinks that Alzheimer’s disease is so powerfully important in our community. Paul Booker, Ed West, and Carl Clark all are interested in police and safety activities. Ike Gardner adds unemployment to the list while Jim Nunnelly feels that mental health delivery system needs vast improvements.

But while we will talk more about these issues as the season progresses, let’s now turn our attention to the biggest conversation in town. Will the Chiefs win more than two games, this year? Well it all starts Sunday, against the Jacksonville Jaguars. Armed with a new quarterback and a rebuilt offensive line, the Chiefs will probably win, down in Florida. Dr. Allen, Paul Booker, Ike Gardner, Ollie Hubbard, Jim Nunnelly and Ed West think that the Chiefs will get off to a good start. But, Carl Clark says that the Chiefs will never be on top, again. Thus, losing against the Jaguars.

Speaking of being “on top”, Beyonce’s number one song was “You Put My Love On Top”. Now that is the question that we are all asking, after the dismal showing by the Chiefs, over the years.

Will the Chiefs put Kansas City on top? Most of us are as skeptical about the Chiefs, as we are about the issues facing our community.

So, let’s take advice from our ancestors...Let’s just take one game at a time. And wait to see if the Chiefs have been listening to Beyonce Giselle Knowles.

Spt Monday Niters

Peanute Brittle, Melody Lanes And The Chiefs

We, Monday Niters, have given up on the Chiefs.

They have been beaten badly by the doormats of the NFL, i.e. Buffalo and Detroit.

What’s more they have lost their only offensive hope through injury. . .Jamal Charles. All they have left now is Peanut Brittle Matt Cassel.

Yes, that’s an appropriate name for Cassel because he is worth only “peanuts” and he is always injured, therefore, “brittle”. So, he is now a bona fide member of a long line of “broken” Chiefs’ quarterbacks.

So, we collectively think the Chiefs will get shocked again Sunday by the San Diego Chargers. The Chargers will strike the Chiefs for with six or seven touchdowns.

Speaking of strikes, we talked a lot about old bowling alleys and good bowling athletes. There have been several bowling establishments frequented by African Americans in the ‘60s -- Monarch Lanes, Melody Lanes, King Louie East and even K.C. Bowl in Kansas. And of late, there is a major bowling contingency that now goes to Premier out in Raytown. Just go out there on a Tuesday afternoon and see scores of seniors rolling the ball. And on Fridays and Thursdays, you can find some of the best bowling around. So, in this column, we prefer to talk about bowlers of yesteryears, rather than talk about Peanut Brittle And The Candy Stripers.

So now for a walk down Memory Lanes.

Dr. Charles Allen: Way back in 1952, Doc was a pin-setter at Sakin’s bowling alley, near Linwood and Troost. In the old days, bowling pins were set manually, and usually by African American youth at white establishments. Though he did not bowl much, he felt unsafe in that job. But not unsafe about the locals. Chiefs by 3

Paul “6902” Booker: Booker believes the there is no way that the Chiefs can beat San Diego. And he has felt that way since he came here in 1966. He does remember Fred Broski’s bowling for dollars. Money or not. San Diego by 40

Carl “Classy” Clark: Believes the Chiefs are just pitiful. But does remember being a league bowler at Melody Lanes in 1965, on a team sponsored by Northwestern Mutual Insurance Company. Not enough insurance for the Chiefs to win, though. San Diego by 14

Congressman “Bubba” Cleaver: Believes the Chiefs are “out of and out for the season. Wisely, Cleaver has appointed a group to research and honor the black bowlers of the past. This group includes Jauqua Preston, Joe Newsome, Nero Hartridge, Ron Winfrey, Maxine Walker, LaDonna Hendrix, Sam Coleman, Jiles Dunn, Deborah Cheadle Thomas, Joycelyn Burgo, Alex Harris, Willie Henderson Jim Nunnelly and Bill Johnson. An announcement is expected soon on the outcomes. Outcome bad for Chiefs. San Diego by 10

Ike “Spoiled by Niecie’s” Gardner: Ike was pin-setter at a white bowling alley in the ‘50s. As a worker, he had to use the back door to enter. Now, that was a long time ago. He hated segregation as much as he hates the Chiefs. San Diego by 35

Lloyd “Loyal Up To A Point” Greenfield: Lloyd is one of the seniors who bowl at Premier on Tuesdays. Bowling a cool 152 average, he is surrounded by seniors who can really bowl and who know a lot about Kansas City’s history. But, this time, Lloyd switches over from the Chiefs. San Diego by 7

Ollie “Breaking New ” Hubbard: Ollie remembers going to parties at the Rhythm Lanes on Troost, way back in the sixties. The teenagers went there for music and refreshments. But, the Chiefs are not refreshing at all and he expects a bolt of lightning to hit the Chiefs. San Diego by 35

Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly: started bowling at King Louie East, when Ron Winfrey became the first black to manage a bowling establishment. Hopes to record some of this rich bowling history. But history will be made Sunday when the Charges run up the largest score in NFL history on the Chiefs. San Diego by 50

Ed “Still Mentoring” West: Being brought up in Osawatamie, Kansas, Ed was only permitted to bowl one night a week at the white bowling alley. However, later he joined the St. James bowling league in 1980. Never a true bowler, he feels the Chiefs have no coach, no offense, no defense thus no win. San Diego by 38

Chiefs: On The Way Back Or Sinking Fast?

The wound and the gift are the same. That simply means that what has hurt you may well be the thing that saves or advances you forward. Is that true with the Chiefs’ upswing? Will injuries continue to plague the Chiefs health? First, let’s look in on our discussion topic, before we talk about the rebounding Chiefs.

Actually, we Monday Niters, hosted by Lloyd Greenfield, and his smart 7-year-old granddaughter. She is bright, personably and healthy in every way. Speaking of health care, the Monday Niters are going to be present at a “Community Conversation” about health care. It’s gonna be October 11 and it is sponsored by the Health Care Foundation of Greater Kansas City. The foundation seriously wants to know what everyday citizens think and want to do about their health care. In fact, every Monday Niter will be prepared to express their views, three weeks from this Saturday.

Now, that announcement swiftly turned the discussion into a history lesson.About the health care providers of the past and today. Right away, the guys started shouting out dentists, physicians and nurses, from the past. Dr. Carl Peterson (physician); Dr. T.T. Lowry (dentist); and Dr. George Taft (physician) were mentioned early. Then quickly, the attention turned to hospitals, prominent to our community back in the fifties and sixties. There was Wheatley hospital and of course, there were The Queen of the World and General Hospital Number Two. And then very capable and pace-setting nurses entered the conversation. Honorably mentioned was Clara Shields Maddox, innovative public health nurse, at the then- Wayne Miner Health Center.

And Ike brought up the name of a current physician, Dr. Joahn Betts-Hall. And Dr. Allen brought up the well-known dentist, Dr. Frank Haugh. And most notable among female health administrators was Dorothy Roberts, co-developer of the Dental Department down at the now-Rodgers Health center. Even Ivy Ganaway was mentioned as having a formidable pre-natal program at Swope, sponsored by the Jackson county government. Everyone mentioned were truly game-changers and contributors. Wonder what they would say at the “community conversation” the second weekend in October? Has the health of our people improved or stayed the same?

The same question should be asked about our new life Chiefs. This team has improved y’all, in just one week. The tv commentators were shouting out names, most of whom we did not know and had never heard of. But they performed well, winning the stats game, against the highly-favored Denver Broncos. Now, those same no-namers will have to show the same stuff, against Miami (who beat New England two weeks ago). These no-namers were superlative in their replacement roles e.g. Knile Davis ran well replacing the injured Jamaal Charles. And Vickerson, former Bronco, performed well consistently. Even brittle Alex Smith passed with “bullet-like” precision. But they lost and few will remember the scoreless “10 minutes” in the third quarter, when the Chiefs just showed out.

Consequently, it was as if the Chiefs were inspired . . . because of the key-player injuries. Thus, it could be said that the poor showing against Tennessee actually worked to the Chiefs’ advantage. Truly, they are a better team, overall as a result. Maybe out of all that crap, came something good, some noticeable improvement, some brighter days ahead. Maybe there is something to the saying that “the wound and the gift are the same”. Do the Monday Niters believe this “wound and gift” thing will continue forward to Sunday’s game against the Dolphins in Miami? Here’s what the guys think . . .

Dr. Charles Allen: Because of the last game, Chiefs are playing well . . . Chiefs by 7.

Paul Booker: All the Chiefs are in the MASH unit (injured) . . . Miami by 10.

Carl Clark: Chiefs must play a complete game to win . . . Miami by 7.

Bubba Cleaver: Chiefs will pick on somebody their size. . . Chiefs by 10.

Ike Gardner: Don’t be taken in by KC’s performance last week . . . Miami by 10.

Lloyd Greenfield: Compliments to the Chiefs’ passing game . . . Chiefs by 7.

Ollie Hubbard: Chiefs are gearing up . . . Chiefs by 3.

Jim Nunnelly: A setback is designed to propel a comeback . . . Chiefs by 10.

Ed West: Reserves are filling in with confidence . . . Chiefs by 7.

Ed “DOZING” West, Technology, And The Chiefs

Ed West is by far the most knowledgeable and most practical Monday Niter. Between naps, he always lends facts to our highly opinionated discussions. All he does is pull out his Iphone and recites the facts.

And that technological move silences the voices of those who are just talking to hear themselves talk. Ed learned about computers at an early age. He was a manager at the old Western Electric Plant, out in Lee’s Summit.

Further, Ed was once the executive in charge of Citizens’ Complaints of the Police department. His knowledge about technology, coupled with his commitment to listening to citizens is a major reason for his widespread, community support. He is respected, admired and often called on to address personal computer issues among his friends.

In addition, his Monday Niter friends agree with him when he says the Chiefs are just as good as Denver. In fact, he pulled out his computer and concluded that except for Baltimore, the Chiefs and Denver have played the same teams. However, he does concede that Denver has beaten their opponents by wider margins. Bottom line.....six victories is six victories...no matter who the team is. But let’s look in on the proceedings from a great Monday night, with the Monday Niters. Here’s some highlights of the long discusions:

Dr. Allen is still persistent about Alzheimer’s and caregivers. In fact, he thinks this research tax must include something about care-givers. More on this next week. . . Congressman Cleaver calls Booker from Washington, just to check on him....Jim Randall, Booker’s neighbor, drops in. . . Ike, Dr. Allen and Booker talk about their days at Niles Home. . . Ed West nods. . .Why aren’t the Chiefs throwing to Dwayne Bowe?. . . Chiefs cannot beat Denver, period. . . Josh Freeman of Grandview will eventually become the starting quarterback for Minnesota. . . Ed is snoring loudly now. . . The Chiefs are running Jamaal Charles to death. . . San Diego always folds down the line. . . remember it was the Baltimore Ravens who beat Denver last year and won the Super Bowl. . . the way the Republicans are acting is paving the way for Hillary Clinton to be President..Carl and Lloyd talk about how many of their classmates have died. . . Cleaver says that “it is crazy up here in Washington”. . .but he’s hanging in there. . .Dontari Poe is doing his thing as the Chiefs’ nose tackle. . . Chiefs lead the league in defense. . . so what will the Chiefs do against the Houston Texans, out at Arrowhead on Sunday?

Dr. Charles Allen: KC is in the easy part of their schedule. Chiefs by 14.

Paul Booker: KC has a better defense. Chiefs by 7.

Carl Clark: KC is weak on offense. Houston by 3.

Ike Gardner: Same plague that struck the Giants struck Houson. Chiefs by 7.

Lloyd Greenfield: KC’s offense will step up Sunday. Chiefs by 3.

Ollie Hubbard: Lincoln University could beat these Texas cowboys. Chiefs by 7.

Jim Nunnelly: KC has All-Pro Defense. No losses until Dennver.Chiefs by 17.

Ed “Awake Now” West: KC has superior defense and a great crowd.Chiefs by 10.

Walt Youngblood: It’ll be a defensive struggle Chiefs by 3.Jim Randall: Homefield advantage Chiefs by 14

Clancy Parker: .Following Doc Allen. Chiefs by 7.

Well, you know we have some Monday Niters who got some bad news over the holidays. It wasn’t about the Chiefs and their playoff hopes. It wasn’t about how Denver squeaked by the Bengals in a thriller. It was about their favorite hang-out, the infamous “6902”. A shooting, resulting in a murder, was recorded. Then, right after that, the place burned up. What a week for the well-attended night club. Booker cried. Carl is just not himself, at all. And Charles is clearly at a loss for words. Several neighbors have commented that the club is usually trouble-free and attracted both young and old. And it is significant because what happened there was more newsworthy than what is happening with our last-chance Chiefs.

Here’s the scene, if the Chiefs are to win a spot in the playoffs. First, they must beat Jacksonville on Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Some think they will because usually (I say usually) the Chiefs win at home. But, in addition to a must-win for the Chiefs’, both San Francisco, Tennessee and Pittsburgh must win. Then, the Chiefs would backdoor into the last playoff spot. But, I have a question for you.

Why is it we always have to struggle our way into the playoffs, especially here in December? Why couldn’t we just have beaten weak Cleveland and crippled Miami? Then, we would be just like the Jets…already in. But, that Herman Edwards and that no-named offensive coordinator just blew it all…with their senseless, play-ground play-calling. Bottom line, prepare yourself for a Chiefs’ loss Sunday or for Denver and Cincinnati to win. Any one of those scenarios will spell the end of the season for Kansas City. And just think, Herm has already started talking about 2007 and how he is going to stick with Tricky Trent Green. And just like always, the Chiefs will soon be talking smack and lament that they were two or three plays away from post-season competition. Crap.

Anybody know when the “6902” is gonna re-open?

Mary Lynn Clark said it best. This has been a relatively quiet Super Bowl. Not much said about it all. Is it because these two black coaches have broken through the “glass ceiling”?

Or is it because there just isn’t much to talk about? Well, let’s see if we can raise the “talk” bar. As you have noted, we predicted the “coaches” this week rather than the teams. That's because this is moment in history where everyone wins. Either way, a black coach wins Super Bowl XLI. Ok? And America wins because it can proudly say that a black coach has won and coached in a Super Bowl. And, last, Caucasian announcers will find it a bit awkward for either over-emphasizing or under-stating it. It will be interesting to see how they handle it.

But, back to Mary Lynn’s comment about the “quietness”. Now, from where I sit, every African American is talking about it, proudly. But the television sponsors are not. They ought to be talking about this like they talked about Elway, finally making it to his first Super Bowl. The way it appears is that Manning will replace Big Bad John as the “hype centerpiece”. And that is an affront to all of us black people. This two-team black coaching thing is right up there with Jackie Robinson, breaking into baseball way back in the ‘40s. And it should be on every media outlet in the country. Foreign countries ought to know that America has made good on some of its promises (not all of them, just some).

But, reality tells me that the giant media outlets will treat this rather “low-key”. Thus, I predict that very little will be said about all of this on Sunday and the days leading up to it. And quite frankly, I mad about it. Whatever you do, don’t say that it doesn’t matter. Sunday may be the last time, for a long time, when we all can sing “We Shall Overcome” with meaning and purpose. After all, to me and to you, Super Bowl Sunday will be a sacred part of Black History Month (lest we forget).

Let’s all rejoice and be glad in it. Thanks, Mary Lynn for nudging us to speak up.

Monday Niters To Be Featured Before Chiefs’ Game with pic

By The Sports Guy

Some of the members of the infamous Monday Niters, who have been watching Monday Night football together for 34- years, will be featured on the Tailgate show Monday night on Channel 9 before the Chiefs play or get beat by the Tennessee Titans.

The group has a column that has been featured in THE CALL with their football predictions of Chiefs’ games since 1980 but began with their New Year’s column in 1970.

They rotate around to each members home for dinner on Monday nights. The Sports Guy has had the opportunity to attend a couple of Monday night games at the home of Jim Nunnelly and recently Ed West. They are all really a great group of guys.

There is a great deal of wisdom and history in the room and they will even be helping The Sports Guy pass out Christmas baskets to needy families next week, but have to tell you. If you really want to watch a football game and actually hear what is said, don’t go near them on Monday night, you won’t hear a thing.

Anything that you can think of is a topic of discussion.

Ike Gardner boasts that he has won the most games overall in the group’s history of course he is immediately challenged by Lloyd Greenfield, Carl Clark and Paul Booker even wakes up to challenge Gardner’s claim that he has won the most games.

“I want to tell you all something. I am not opposed to the Chiefs as much as some of you all are. You paint the picture that I am always against the Chiefs. I am only against them when they are playing a team that they cannot beat. I have never said that they Chiefs are going to lose all of the time,” said Gardner.

“See that ole’ fat mouth Nunnelly started that,” Gardner yelled.

Of course Nunnelly denies doing any such thing.

Ed West shares the secret of the groups long term success.

“Our wives deserve most of the credit. They cook for all of us and make their homes presentable for guests. They are very gracious hosts for a bunch of ragga’ muffins,” said West.

“It is a lot cheaper now to host a game than it was when I first joined the group 25-years ago. Nobody drinks anymore so we don’t have the cost of buying alcohol,” West said.

There are countless photos of the group through the years and the events that they have attended both in Kansas City and St. Louis when there was a group of Monday Niters there. And there were too many New year’s parties to count.

There is even a Governor’s Cup trophy that the group competed against St. Louis with.

Chatter, Conversation And Commentary

There are two important similarities to the two teams in the 2003 Super Bowl. First, they both proudly wave Pirates’ flags, one calling themselves the Buccaneers and the other known respectfully as the Raiders. Then, too, each share the influence of a common head coach, Jon Gruden. There, the similarities cease. On the other hand, the Raiders sport the NFL’s Most Valuable Player in quarterback, Rich Gannon. On the other hand, the Bucs have Warren Sapp, arguably the best defensive lineman to ever play the game in recent times. The Raiders will place their receiving hopes in their Medicare acquisition, Jerry Rice. And the Bucs will put their trust in Deion-like, Keyshawn Johnson. All of this makes for a competitive and entertaining Super Bowl, assuring that each team has a reasonable chance to win. Of course, most Monday Niters believe in Oakland. That’s because they are Gannon worshippers, never letting go of the fact that the Chiefs let this rugged individualist star get away. Plus, they feel some faulty pride in that the AFC West produced the most competitive division in football. Nevertheless, the Bucs have a coaching genius in Jon Gruden. And it is my belief that this “coaching factor” is a real key. While some may rightfully argue that the coach will not wear a uniform, leaving player performance as the most important outcome factor on Sunday. But, any respectable football fan, knows that in close games, the coaching effect reigns supreme. Things like clock management, blitz-timing, and playcalling are all decisions directly influenced by the head coaches. So, given that this will be one of the closest Super Bowl games, Jon Gruden becomes the “difference maker” out in San Diego, giving the Bucs the vital edge and their first championship ever.

Monday Niters

Now, let’s get real. The Chiefs are wishing that Sunday’s game was still pre-season. That way, they could evaluate the new players, rest the old ones at the end of the first quarter, and then dodge the brutal, candid questions, asked by blood-sucking reporters after the game.

But, it is “for real” now. Sunday will open the biggest wound possible in Herm Edwards’ armor. It will show that the Chiefs are destined for another dismal season.

In addition, Sunday will show that the draft, trades, and the legacy were all worthless and unfulfilling.

The Chiefs have too many unknown and untried players, to even think about beating the Patriots. In fact, we should fast forward this game and go directly to the next two games..against Oakland at home and Atlanta away. The Chiefs just might beat Oakland, depending on how well Russell, their strong-armed quarterback, plays. And maybe just maybe we might beat the Vick-less Falcons. If my math is right, three weeks from now we will be 2-1. But, come Sunday, they will lose so badly that we will think there is another Boston Massacre. Even those little men on the New England helmets will dance in both end zones. Tom Brady will pick our hand-made secondary apart. And except for a couple of long runs, the Chiefs will appear like they are still in pre-season mode. Once again, Herm will pick up where he left off, last season. With an inadequately profound statement that says and does nothing. And the reporters will laughingly write the same stories that they wrote all of last year, about how unlikely the Chiefs are to win at all. And the fans will and should direct our attention to Oakland and Atlanta, the Chiefs’ next two opponents. And Herm will go to work on his next appearance before the reporters, hopefully more realistic. For now, though. . . look for the Patriots to beat the Chiefs by at least 30 points.

Enough said.

Monday Niters

By Jim “Granddad” Nunnelly

Well, we all know that the Chiefs are still losing. But, so is Oakland, the Chiefs’ next Arrowhead opponent.

And, we all know about the 40 year rivalry between the two teams. And we also know that, at one time, these AFC stalwarts were competitive and even Super Bowl entrants.

But, like a lot of things, each of these two historical giants have fallen on “hard times”. Neither is expected to do much at all, in 2008. Like last year and the year before that. In fact, these conference siblings are in a shaky state of re-building i.e. they will lose every game they play.

Thus, the game out at Arrowhead will be “a battle of the behinders”. To mark such a sad occasion, I have changed the names of the teams, to reflect how even they are. . . in their likelihood to lose. From now on, these two teams are called the Kansas City Raiders and the Oakland Chiefs. That’s because they are both losers and they will continue doing “hard times of 2008”.

Enough said. Allow me tell you a little about each one of the Monday Nite Footballers Group. . . one by one. . . with their predictions for Sunday.

DR. CHARLES ALLEN (Kansas City) -- A dentist by profession. . . known for his painless procedures and his professional office staff. . a Kappa by fraternity.... an extraordinary grandfather. . . believes Kansas City will win by touchdown because the Chiefs will play better at home.

PAUL BOOKER..(Oakland). . . retired educator...was a specialist in assisting needy students...hails from the Bootheel. . . watches ESPN everyday...good with figures...self-appointed liquor inspector...fantastic grandfather. . . believes Oakland will win by 10 because of the pitiful Chiefs’ pitiful offensive line.

CARL CLARK (Kansas City). . . retired journeyman at Colgate...car enthusiast. world traveler...socially aware and respected. . . effective grandfather....daily visitor to 69th and Prospect. . . believes Kansas City will win by a touchdown, even though neither team is worthy of winning.

BUBBA CLEAVER.(Kansas City). . . nationally-acclaimed elected official and minister. . . former Mayor and now United States Congressman. . .studious and thoughtful to a fault.. . . can remember thousands of people and their exact conversations. . . a proud grandfather. . . believes Kansas City will win by 10 because the Oakland Raiders don’t “raid” any more.

IKE GARDNER..(Oakland). . . fearless pragmatist. . . attends St. James and sits near the back on the aisle. . .a steadfast grandfather. . . cantankerous as hell. . . has seats on the 50 yard line at Arrowhead. . . was a key player in the early days of desegregation of schools. . .believes Kansas City by 3 because there is no chance for Oakland to win.

LLOYD GREENFIELD (Kansas City). . . a retired firefighter turned real estate enthusiast. . . owns hundreds of model cars...has a motorcycle. . . has starred in many theatrical events. . . clear-headed and respected. . . stays in contact with his old high school class..believes the Chiefs will win by 3 but it will be on a “wing and a prayer”.

OLLIE HUBBARD (Oakland). . . Loves and is married to Lincoln university. . . been known to lie about everything. . . knows everybody in Kansas City...formerly a vice principal, now retired....loves to prepare food for his friends so that he can eat the most.....a remarkable grandfather....thinks the Kansas City-Oakland game will be a toss-up but Chiefs could win by 14.

JIM NUNNELLY..(Kansas City). . .has consulted with over 200 organizations nationwide....instrumental in building anddeveloping the Rodgers Health Center. . . .a relentless advocate for teenagers. . . a complete grandfather.......works at Jackson County. . . believes the Chiefs and the Raiders are the two worst teams in the NFL but come Sunday, the Chiefs will deploy a “Midwest Coast Defense” and win by 3.

ED WEST....(Kansas City). . . a gadgetician. . . can construct or repair any computer...has a huge big screen tv. . .a substitute teacher in Raytown....was All-America at Pittsburg State. . .devout member of St. James. . . an encouraging grandfather...believes Kansas City will win by a touchdown because he “just a Raider-hater”

MONDAY NITERS VISIT WITH FORMER CHIEFS GREAT. . . Otis Taylor, former Kansas City Chiefs wide receiver, was the guest of honor recently at the Monday Nite Footballers meeting, at Rev. Emanuel “Bubba” Cleaver’s house. He shared excerpts from his autobiography, “Otis Taylor” by Otis Taylor. The book recounts Otis’ days here, in the 60s and 70s. Len Dawson, Hall of Fame quarterback, wrote the foreword and called Otis “a big play receiver”. More important, Otis listened to Monday Niters as they remembered his spectacular one-hand catches and subsequent long runs. Carl Clark was able to see a picture of his most memorable Chiefs’ plays, when Taylor carried Pat Fischer across the goal line on his back. Many, including Ollie Gates and Rev. Cleaver, have tried to get Otis inducted into the NFL Hall of Fame, but to no avail. Otis played college football at Prairie View A&M during the same time Rev. Cleaver was there. Because of an advanced illness, Otis has been walking with the assistance of a cane. He was a frequent customer at Maxine’s, 31st and Benton.

Monday Niters

Johnny Cash, Memorable Ministers And The Chiefs

About 50 years ago, Johnny Cash recorded a song, called, “A Boy Named Sue”.

Well, the Chiefs are going to face a “Man Called Suh”. He was last year’s “Defensive Rookie Of The Year” in the NFL. His complete name is Ndamudong Suh (pronounced N dam u kong). This unusual name is African and it means “house of spears”. He is 6’4” tall and hails from Portland, Oregon. That where his Cameroon-born father and Jamaica-born mother resides.

What does that have to do with the Chiefs? Well, the paper-thin, Chiefs’ offense will have to face Ndamukong Suh and the rest of the Detroit Lions on Sunday. It’s at noon and you can look for this man named “Suh” to be all over and all in the Chiefs’ backfield, just throwing Peanut Brittle Matt Cassel and Once In A While Jamal Charles all over the place.

Not bad for a boy named “Sue”.

Well, we Monday Niters met over at Ike’s house and had some show-stopping cherry cobbler. Though he didn’t prepare the multi-faceted meal, he walked around kingly, like he was part of it. Truth is his wife, their daughter Shelly and her daughter Danielle prepared every dish and yet, Cantankerous Ike tried to take credit for such a culinary display. A minister would have concluded that he is richly blessed. Speaking of ministers, that was the main topic of our conversations. Here goes, along with our predictions.

Dr. Charles Allen: He remembered Rev. John Williams at the St. Stephen Baptist church. But, he also praised Father Benedict Justice, one of the few black Catholic priests in Kansas City in the ‘40s and ‘50s. But reluctantly and against all religions, he believes in the locals. Chiefs by 3

The Apostle Paul Booker: He praised the Reverend Henderson Bell, his home-church pastor. He considers his pastor as the most approachable minister in town. But Paul doesn’t believe in The Chiefs at all. Detroit by 14

Carl “Righteous Bro-ther” Clark: He was married at the Mason Memorial in Kansas 50 two years ago. But, he has always attended St. James, under Rev. Cleaver, the only minister he has ever known. And he doesn’t know the Chiefs anymore, either. Detroit by 14

Ike “One Mean PaPaw” Gardner: Ike is so spoiled by a group of waitresses at a popular restaurant that he sometimes forgets that he is not a priest of man of the cloth. But he does remember being forced to go to church at Ward’s Chapel, years ago. Since then, he has bonded with Rev. Cleaver and now is an unforced member of St. James. Like him or not, he says no. Detroit by 20

Lloyd “Gentleman” Greenfield: Right off, Lloyd remembered Reverend Chapelle of the Second Baptist Church, as a minister who could related to young black males. Awesome man. But still sticking with the locals. Chiefs by 7

Congressman Emanuel “Bubba” Cleaver II: Being a minister himself, Cleaver had several ministers on his praise list. . .Reverends A.L. Johnson, John Preciphs, Emanuel Newton, John Eason. Isaiah Henderson Sr. and many others. But he got carried away and also praised the Chiefs. Chiefs by 8

Jim “GrandDad” Nunnelly: He praised Rev. Earl Abel for his excellence as a pastor and a friend. Also for mentoring and selecting the very capable Reverend Leonard Butler as his successor. A good pick for Kansas City. Chiefs by 7

Monday Niters

Be Careful And Cautious, With Those Chiefs

Wow! The New Orleans Saints are 2-0. So are seven other NFL teams. One of them is the Kansas City Chiefs.

But, the Saints’ 2-0 is different than the Chiefs 2-0.

In our minds, the Saints will go on to capture the playoffs, if not repeat as World Champions. Whereas, our Chiefs. . . well, we ain’t sure just what will happen to them.

For those of us over 50, we have been down this road before. Same stuff, a different year. And the haters, among us, say it is about to end, this weekend at Arrowhead. That’s when the Chiefs will face San Francisco, the same team that the Saints just beat this past Monday night.

Bottom line, most of us are basking in delight because we remember last year, when we were 0-2, at this time.

So, let’s all toast the Chiefs and their defense for making us “cautiously happy” with their play, thus far. But, now let’s turn to the fella’s and see what they think about the Chiefs’ chances against the 49rs.

THE VENERABLE DR. CHARLES ALLEN: Doc is skeptical about the quarterback situation with the Chiefs. He once bought a Cassel t-shirt but left it home and refused to wear it. Look like he was right because the Chiefs could have won, without Cassel.

Doc says it was the defense, led by Derrick Johnson, that was the difference. But. unlike the haters, Doc says the CHIEFS will win Sunday.

THE APOSTLE PA-UL BOOKER: He is the Executive Vice President of the Chiefs Haters. And we don’t know where Paul started to go wrong. Many think he is overly influenced by another hater in the Monday Niters.

But, the saintly Paul says that SAN FRANCISCO will win on Sunday, ending the Chiefs good beginning. He calls it the “Singletary Factor”, citing the 49rs’ head coach as the difference-maker.

THE COOL CARL CLARK: Like Doc, Carl is not impressed with Cassel, at all. However, he recognizes that both teams are better, this year, even though the Chiefs are weakly, overall. Carl, not quite a hater, did select SAN FRANCISCO, to beat the Chiefs. At least by a field goal. Maybe, Carl is being inducted into the Haters Hall Of Fame. ???

CONGRESSMAN EMANUEL CLEAVER: Steady on the campaign trail, it is fun to watch him take a break from Washington stuff.

In fact, he is slated to become Chairman of the Legislative Black Caucus in Washington, assuming he is re-elected. That will give him even more access to the President and White House.

Go, Cleaver, go.

Obviously, he is doing fine representing Kansas City in Washington. But, the Congressman has allowed the haters to get to him. He really believes the Chiefs will be “Gored” to death, citing the slashing running back from the Niners, Frank Gored. Apparently, Ike and that Booker have gotten to Rev. . . he says SAN FRANCISCO BY 10.

FOREVER CANTA-NKEROUS IKE GARDNER: Last week, we observed Ike’s being nice to a certain group of employees at a local restaurant. They secretly know I am right about his “porcupine-like” personality. In fact, he has chosen to sit with only one other person, further distancing himself from his old friends.

To further this distance, Ike says that Haley and Cassel are “severe disabilities” and must be dealt with, some kinda way. That leaves him no hope for the Chiefs this week. . .SAN FRANCISCO BY 13.

THE LOYALIST, LLOYD GREENFIELD: Lloyd tries to see hope in whatever he does. Being a part-time thespian, he can find positive, sometimes amid all hopelessness. And he needs it to for there are “haters” in this group.

However vocal the detractors are, Lloyd continues his support for the Chiefs. Specifically, he finds the running backs, Charles and Jones, as promising. Unlike the haters, Lloyd “gets it”. CHIEFS, all the way.

THE CNN OF KANSAS CITY, OLLIE HUBBARD: Now, Ollie believes that the Chiefs are going “up, up and away”. Though he didn’t say where they were going, we assume he means the Chiefs will continue to win. However, we must remember that Ollie is a former hater and can’t be totally believed, right away.

When asked by how much, he proudly screams, CHIEFS BY 18.

Is he right? Maybe, maybe not.

Now, back to CNN.

JIM “GRAND DAD” NUNNELLY: I have been friends with the Monday Niters for over forty years and this year, we will celebrate 40 years of uninterrupted friendship.

But, along the way, we have developed into a “two-party” system. Some of us believe in the Chiefs, no matter what and there are those who DON’T believe in the Chiefs, no matter what.

As for me, the Chiefs will have to stop San Francisco’s Smith-to-Davis connection. And with Kansas City’s weak secondary, one can see why we loyalists ought to be concerned.

In spite of this weakness, the return game and defense will be enough for the Chiefs to advance to 3-0. . .CHIEFS, ALL THE WAY.

EASY ED WEST: Ed is a computer geek. In fact, he spent all of Monday night, player computer scrabble with his son, Damon (who is in California). But Ed is perceptive, too.

He has seen the Arrowhead crowd make a difference and he is calling on their help to make the Chiefs an early contender.

But for Sunday, Ed has shifted over to the haters, after seeing San Francisco against the Super Bowl Champions Saints. SAN FRANCISCO BY A LOT.

Final Note: Too bad the Chiefs have let us down so many times before. And unfortunately, we think of the Chiefs as losers, rather than victors.

But, whatever we do, we cannot allow these “tea-party like” haters predict and manage our future.

Clearly, the Chiefs are not New Orleans; but they are 2-0. That makes them equal to the Saints and it is our perception of them that will dictate our expectations of them.

Dr. Allen, Lloyd, even Ollie, and I are aiming and expecting high. While that Booker, Carl, Cleaver, Cantankerous Ike, and Ed West are usually right, the haters are not omnipotent and are wrong this time..

Let’s see, let’s see.

Spt - Monday Niters

Ike Gardner, Fearless Candor And The Chiefs

Ike Gardner is definitely one of a kind. We, in the Monday Niters, risk our emotional lives, each Monday night of the football season. That’s because Ike is cantankerous, arrogant, and one-wayward.

Of course, some of this “fearless candor” can be explained because Ike is/was an only child.

Spending most of his 79 years here, Ike is not a stranger at all. Best known as an educator/administrator, Ike was at the forefront of school desegregation issues, back in the early seventies. There, his candid ways saved the School District from courting catastrophe. He was not diplomatic at all...and thus, we still have some local conrol over our educational system.

In spite of this cantankerousness, Ike is well-respected in our football club. Dr. Allen and Ron Walton refer to him as a “friend for life”. Even though his rough-sounding baritone voice can be heard over everyone, he is well worth listening to.

Ike rants and rages his opinion, on anyone who will listen. Just this past Monday, Congressman Cleaver called Ike from Washington, just to check on his behavior. Ike talked Rev.’s head off, going on and on about the Chiefs. Rev actually hung up and Ike didn’t even notice. He just kept on “Ikeing” with fearless candor, leaving the rest of us speechless.

But, we must seriously consider what Ike says about the Chiefs and their Chiefs’ 5-0 start. Considering the weak schedule, Ike candidly warns us that there are still eleven games to play in 2013. And even though he is voting for the Chiefs to win against Oakland, he is nonetheless cautious about the Chiefs’ mediocrity. Oakland’s newly-found quarterback, Terrell Pryor, will test the Chiefs’ highly-rated defense. Listen to Ol’ Ike and his candor.

Enough of Uncle Cantank. . . now to the rest of us and our thoughts on the Chiefs and other things.

Dr. Charles Allen: Doc is still reminding us about Alzheimer’s disease. Particularly, he advises care-givers that there is no cure for this awful, life-changing disease. However, he does see a great prognosis for the Chiefs and their momentum. . .Chiefs by 7

Paul Booker: He’s happy with the Chiefs’ progress and believes the defense will score often against the Raiders. . . Chiefs by 7

Carl Clark: Carl, on the other hand, believes the Chiefs are bound to lose, sometimes. And he thinks it will be Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Raiders by 7

Lloyd Greenfield: Everyone is commenting on Lloyd’s interest in total community education. Involving everyone in a child’s education is his strong view. Also, he is relentless about the Chiefs and the crowd. . . Chiefs by 3

Ollie Hubbard: Every now and then, Ollie gets out and about. And all the guys were elated to interact with him over dinner (that he didn’t cook or supervise). . . Chiefs by 7

Jim “Granddad” Nunnelly: He likes the Chiefs’ mental health. They are not breaking down, like they used to, last year. The Chiefs’ defense is among the leagues best, especially the front line and linebackers. Look for some errors but overall this is all. .. Chiefs by 10

Ed West: Ed once managed the office of Citizens’ Complaints for the Police Department. Thus, he knows all about the need for local control. Leave it like it is, he shouts. Leadership is encouraging, even for the Chiefs. . . winning by 10

SPT- Monday Niters

Rudolph, MVP Choice, And The 11-3 Chiefs

It is Christmas time and we are all singing those long-remembered Christmas carols. One song stands out, as the Chiefs enter the much-coveted playoff/Super Bowl pursuit. Y’all know the story about Rudolph. He had this freaky red nose and the other reindeer “used to laugh and call him names”. They never let Rudolph join in any reindeer games. Okay, you get the point. But, one foggy Christmas eve, ol’ Rudolph became the MVP among reindeers, everywhere.

Speaking of Most Valuable Player, we must begin to talk about “who is the Chiefs’ MVP this year”. Dr. Allen says it will be indisputably Jamaal Charles. Even wishy-washy Paul Booker says Charles is an all-purpose player and deserves to be MVP. Mr. Socialite, Carl Clark, adds that Charles is “the team”. And Mila Banks closes the conversation by saying that Charles is “running well and scoring”.

That sums it all up….Jamaal Charles is the MVP of the 2013 Chiefs. Please note that Ike Gardner was not asked about his opinion on this.

That was on purpose. Because Ike’s approval rating has sunk to an all-time low. Most people at St. James forgive Ike for his cantankerous ways. Sometimes, he is just against everything, You will remember it was Ike who said the Chiefs would only win four games this year. And even in spite of winning eleven games already, he insists on saying the Chiefs will lose this Sunday, out at Arrowhead.

This abject stubbornness caused Mila Banks to lash out at old Ike. She says that Ike needs to wake up and “know that the Kansas City is playing football”. Meaning, Ike you can stop berating the Chiefs because they have proved your tired self wrong.

In fact, many say that the book “A Christmas Carol” was written about Ike Gardner. It has been said that Ebenezer Scrooge and Ike were room-mates, at one time. Unfortunately, Ike still corresponds with Scrooge frequently. They talk about everything, including how Santa still is using reindeer to deliver Christmas packages..

Speaking of reindeer, let’s return to close out the thought about “Rudolph”. He was ostracized just like the Chiefs were last year. People laughed and talked badly about Rudolph. Like the Chiefs, everyone said ol’ Rudolph was a loser and would never amount to nothing. And now, this year, everyone is acting like they never said anything bad about the Chiefs. Those same haters are rushing home from church, talking about how proud they are of their hometown team.

Bottom line, it was Ike who stirred up the other reindeer against Rudolph, in the first place. So the next time you see Ike Gardner, tell him that Rudolph and the Chiefs are now on top. And like Mila Banks said to Ike…”wake up and know” that even Scrooge changed when presented with facts. Not so with that damn Ike. He has fallen asleep again.

Super Bowl 47: Apogee,

Kaepernick, And The Left Out Chiefs

Apogee is a word, used in astronomy to describe the farther point out there in space. It was used in the novel, Fifty Shades Of Grey to describe the best feeling that can be derived from romantic encounter. Well, on Sunday, the apogee of football games will be played in New Orleans. This game will feature Baltimore Ravens and the San Francisco Forty Niners.

San Francisco will probably win, mainly because of its stellar quarterback, Kaepernick. This kid is “on fire” and will silence the tirades and mental health outbreaks of Ray Lewis. Ray can stir up the crowd but this kid can move the ball down the field. In fact, over half of his passes go for twenty yards or more. His actions speak louder than Ray Lewis’ words. He is arguably the best quarterback in the league.

Speaking of quarterbacks, why can’t the Chiefs buy, borrow or beg for a good quarterback? Other teams do this but Kansas City cannot get it done. That’s why the Chiefs are “left out” and left at home. You would think that a team with six Pro Bowlers can at least find one quarterback, among all these youngsters. And they are not promising that the quarterback position is not their highest draft priority. That attitude is just plain stupid and explains why the Chiefs haven’t been in a Superbowl, since 1970.

So while San Francisco and Baltimore will be kicking it in the SuperDome, the Kansas City Chiefs will be looking wise and otherwise and missing the point.

In the words of Herm Edwards, you play to win the game. Something the Chiefs don’t understand. They need some apogee. . . or something.

Here’s what the Monday Niters think:

Dr. Charles Allen: San Francisco by 14...no doubt about it.

Paul Booker: Ravens by 7 Ray Lewis will sack Jim Harbaugh for a second time.

Carl Clark: San Francisco by 14 Kaepernick will outperform Flacco

Ike Gardner: San Francisco by 7 They have an excellent offense and defense.

Lloyd Greenfield: Sam Francisco by 7 Just watch them go.

Ollie Hubbard: San Francisco by 12 Going with the City by the Bay.

Jim Nunnelly: San Francisco by 10 Smith will make Raytown proud.

Ed West: San Francisco by 10 Just wait and see.

Eric Wesson: San Francisco by 14. Just watch those Young Bloods operate.

Tracy Allen (sports editor): San Francisco by 7 While this will be the last hooray for Ray Ray, his offense won’t help him.

Kansas City Chiefs vs. Denver Broncos

7:00 p.m. NFL Network

Chatter, Commentary

and Conversation

What does Jesse Jackson have to do with last Sunday’s Oakland-Kansas City game? I’ll answer that after I assess the Chiefs, now just over the half-way point in the season.

First, the Chiefs survived. Now, let’s face it, y’all. A win is a win, no matter how ugly. And Sunday’s win against Oakland was not pretty, except for the first and last Kansas City drives. The in-between was horrible, even though it was Trent’s first day back.

However, I must say that we could not have done it without Trent. Ed West flat-out confirms that. Further, He feels that Trent’s presence won the game, only because Huard could not have shepherded that last drive.

And we all agree that Sunday was the appropriate time to re-insert Trent, even though Huard had done a respectable job. Now,tomorrow will be Trent’s real test. He and Kennison will have to be “connected at the

hip”, for the Chiefs to have half a chance. If we should happen to beat Denver, that will put us in good shape for a playoff spot. We know that

Denver is vulnerable because San Diego beat Denver and we beat San Diego. In fact, last Sunday, LaDainian ran like crazy. . . just like Jesse did in 1988.

Speaking of Jesse Jackson, he really did have something to do with Sunday’s game. But before I tell you, let me reminisce for a minute. Back in 1988, I received a call at the (Samuel) Rodgers Health Center. It was from Fuzzy Thompson, wanting a place to have a rally that featured Jesse

Jackson and some farmers from Chillicothe. I agreed but Fuzzy was unsure if it would take place. . .so I kept it a secret from our staff. . .mainly because I did not want to face it, if Jesse failed to come.

Well, Jesse did come and so did all those farmers who were about to lose their family farms. Tractors, limousines, and a string of cars paraded right down in the middle of the Wayne Miner projects. And people came from everywhere, to hear and see ol’ Jesse. And when he donned the back door, he immediately started planning who would talk, when, and at what point he would enter. Details. Details. Right there on the spot. I immediately saw how Jesse operated. . .great organizing skills, clear instructions, which included a tribute to Dr. Rodgers and a “thank you” to me for helping out. All within a ten-minute planning session. Jesse stood tall in my books and one could see why he is important to all of us, especially us sports fans.

On the way out, I mentioned to Jesse that there were no black head coaches in the National Football League. Jesse, who had been a classy running back at Illinois, told me that he would work on that. A few years later, Jesse started meeting with the owners in the NFL, asking them to include one black coach in the final interviews, only when head coaching vacancies occurred. The owners agreed and today, some 10 years after, there are seven black coaches, proudly running things at various franchises. As of this date, the illustrious field of head coaches now include Lovie Smith Chicago), Marvin Lewis (Cincinnati), Tony Dungy (Indianapolis); Dennis Green (Arizona); and Romeo Cremmel (Cleveland).

The remaining two were on the field Sunday, out at Arrowhead. . . Herman Edwards (Kansas City) and Art Shell (Oakland). And that is what Jesse had to do with the game, last Sunday, out at Arrowhead. Thanks, Jesse. You always know what to do and say.

Happy Thanksgiving To All Of Our Readers!

Matt Cassel: Subject Of Many Words

Matt Cassel is in trouble. He is injured and he will never know if his likely benching is because of that injury or just inept play. It doesn’t matter because “it is what it is”.

He was well on his way to being benched anyway. And we all know it. And the Monday Niters spent half their meeting session, discussing Matt Cassel. But before we share some crushing comments, let’s look into the non-news-making aspects of this week’s game against the Tampa Bay Buccaneers.

Here are some interesting and provocative facts you should know before watching Sunday’s game in Tampa Bay.

First, Jamaal Charles is first in the NFL in rushing (no surprise). Justin Huston, Chiefs’ defensive standout is tied for third in quarterback sacks. Tampa Bay’s starting quarterback is Josh Freeman, a graduate of Grandview High school.

Tampa Bay beat the Oakland Raiders in the Super Bowl, just after the 2002 regular season. Bo Jackson refused to play for Tampa Bay, even though he was the first round draft choice. Doug Williams, first African American to quarterback a Super Bowl, was also a first-round pick for Tampa Bay.

In 1978, Trent Green, Chiefs quarterback under Dick Vermeil, also suffered a season-ending concussion. And that brings us back to Matt Cassel’s, boo-producing injury, last Sunday against Baltimore.

Here’s what the Monday Niters remarked, along with their predictions for the must-win game against Tampa Bay.

Dr. Charles Allen: Believes that if Eric Winston had been doing his job, Cassel wouldn’t have gotten hurt in the first place. Case of sour grapes. Dr. Allen also foresees a coaching change in the near future. Oh? Chiefs by 7

Paul Booker: believes that Matt Cassel was just looking for a way to sit down and take a 60-million dollar rest break...and thereby avoid a lot of bad press. However, Booker says that the hometown boy, Josh Freeman, has no respect for his hometown team. . .thus. . .Tampa Bay by 7

Carl Clark: is truly sorry that Cassel had to get hurt. So he blames the coaches for not taking Cassel out of the game in the first place. Either way, the Chiefs will give the fans “the same old Mitt, warmed over”. . . Tampa Bay by 10

Congressman Emanuel Cleaver: Is busy campaigning. But he did take time out to meet with a few sports fans last Sunday, after the Baltimore game. He spoke candidly about his new-found status in Washington, after having become the chairman of the Congressional Black Caucus. Being a part of a privileged few, he talks weekly with President Obama and goes all over the country in his behalf. But when it comes to the Chiefs, he is embarrassed by their sub-mediocre play. But still stays faithful. . .Chiefs by 7

Ike Gardner: Is nearly 95 years old and has been a Chiefs’ season ticket holder, longer than Winston has been alive. Saying forcefully, he has never witnessed Kansas City fans booing any injured player.

But toward this Sunday’s game, Ike says the Chiefs have talent but have incompetent coaches. . .thus, Tampa Bay by 12

Lloyd Greenfield: Is mad at Eric Winston, recently acquired offensive lineman. Lloyd says he’s been going out to the games for years and he thinks it is unjust and out of place for a player (Winston) to make such a comment. As for this Sunday, Lloyd is afraid they are killing Jamaal Charles, by running him so often and under such impossible circumstances.....but he still remains hopeful. . .Chiefs by 3

Ollie Hubbard: doesn’t believe that crowd was cheering AGAINST Matt Cassel but they were cheering FOR Brady Quinn. Nonetheless, the fact remains, the Chiefs are lacking in quarterback play. That makes Ollie get down on his knees at Ebenezer and pray. . .Chiefs by 3

Jim Nunnelly: thinks that all the national media coverage is severely overblown and highly sensationalized. Worthless and idle chatter, allowing a newbie like Winston to get some press. The real issue is that Matt Cassel has not played well enough to win. Huston, Johnson, Hali, Charles, Berry again spectacular. . .Chiefs by 6

Ed West: Believes the offense will finally catch up with the defense, especially with a new quarterback. He strongly feels that Cassel was “put” in a bad situation because of horrible coaching (Ed may have something)....nonetheless.....Chiefs by 3

Eric “Amanda, Bri, and Erica’s Dad” Wesson: Asks the question, Does Clark Hunt ever think, in the back of his mind, “Am I’m paying this guy to throw complete passes to the other team. Throw the passes to the guys in the red uniforms you dummy.” .... Chiefs by 3 in overtime

Note: Ed West just might have a 100 percent rating. 100 percent wrong. If the Chiefs win against San Diego, Ed West would have predicted all the games, inaccurately. The Chiefs started off 0-3 and now, have won four in a row. Wow, how unpredictable can a team get?

It reminds me of a story of a young minister, who was scheduled to preach his very first sermon. When it was his turn to preach, he strutted up to the podium, absolutely certain that he could move this church to previously-unreached heights. Arrogantly, he read the scriptures. And he shook his head, as if he knew the entire Bible and its meaning, by heart. And rather than slowly engage the congregation, he chose to talk down to them, as if they were second graders. He went on like this for what seemed like an eternity. However, the more he spoke, the more he realized that he was not getting or going anywhere, with this high-and-mighty approach. And slowly but reluctantly, he became more humble, as he read all the unresponding faces in the congregation. Feeling the rejection, he was almost in tears, as he prematurely began to close his botched sermon. And finally, he sat down, almost in shame. For he knew he had failed, miserably.

Embarrassed beyond belief after the services, he stood near the pulpit, head hung low. Then, an old man walked up to him and said, “Son, if you had got up, like you sat down; then, you could have sat down, like you got up.”

The moral of the story is that the Chiefs did not do that. They started off humbly and grew to become the winner they were supposed to become. Let’s give a big cheer to our Chiefs, for having come through, in the end.

Spt- Monday nite

George Benson, Where Are You?

Final note: George Benson no longer is on top.

Not sure where he is or if he is still alive.

We know he will always have a special place in our hearts because of his musical genius. But unfortunately, George must not have known about the 2010 Chiefs.

Look at that offensive line who has helped Charles and Jones become All-Pro prospects. And how about that “kids r us” secondary who are not afraid to step in front of a receiver and take the ball away. And how about those running backs who are leading the league.

And for once, we have a pass rush that we haven’t had since Buck Buchanan.

And let’s not forget we won the Super Bowl with a brittle quarterback named Len Dawson.

So, stop complaining that Matt Cassel is not living up to our expectations. Let’s just refer to him as “Matt, The Masquerader”. . .

You all will remember George Benson, a jazz singer-guitarist, who crooned his way into our memories with a song called, “Masquerade”. The song was about a couple of lovers, who were “masking” their activities, which led to the question about a “masquerade”.

Well, the Chiefs are 4-2 now and many still think the Chiefs are not who we think they are. And this week will be a real test to see if the Chiefs are really “just masquerading”. Hmmmm!

And like always, we Monday Niters are wondering if the Chiefs are really contenders for the playoffs or are they just “pretenders”.

First, they are us loyalists, who believe in the Chiefs, thick or thin, lose or win. We see hope in the making and like Obama used to say...yes, we can. And then there are the “haters”, who despite some real evidence, still cling to the notion that the Chiefs will fade soon. But, one “big hater” changed his vote this week. So, let’s inquire with each Monday Niter and query if the Chiefs will be playing beyond January 2.

Dr. Charles “The Chef” Allen: Doc quit a good job at Bendix to pursue his life-long dream of becoming a dentist. That he did and for thirty two years now, he has delivered “pain free” dentistry to this community.

So as the “main loyalist”, Doc sees the Chiefs undoubtedly making the playoffs. He finds great hope in the “B Boys”. He believes that Bowe and Berry are exactly what the “doc” ordered, on offense and defense respectively, No mask here. Chiefs by 10

Paul “Hater No. 1” Booker: When Paul was an educator and a home-school coordinator for the District, he was kind, effective and knowledgeable. Being from the Bootheel, he appeared that he was happy, go-lucky. But, Paul was forced to pick cotton down in Haiti, Mo. And he carries that “hate” with him, right onto things like the Chiefs. No matter what the Chiefs do, Paul just says “no” to the Chiefs. He says the same about their playoff chances. And about the Chiefs against winless Buffalo. A real “hater”. Take off your mask, Booker...it is a brand new day, Buffalo by 7.

Carl “Car Crazy” Clark: You want to see Carl light up? Just ask him about his beloved, 1987 Corvette. He longs for that speed, that body, and that low center of gravity, But when asked about the Chiefs’ playoff chances, he is not as forward as Doc, nor as difficult as “that Booker”. He recognizes some improvement but knows the Chiefs have not really been tested and have lost to the good teams, like Indianapolis and Houston. So we might have to restore his Corvette to get him to say “yes” but for now, he is moving in the right direction. Chiefs by 3.

Congressman “Still Got It” Cleaver: His wife, Dianne, took him to dinner, last Tuesday. It was his birthday and despite a tedious campaign for re-election, he went and had fun. But the real fun will come on October 30 when the whole community is invited to help him celebrate his birthday, at Ararat Temple.

Evelyn Champagne King and the Bloodstones will be there to sing “happy birthday”, even though he is still wearing a mask about his real age. But, on Sunday, he thinks the Chiefs will beat Buffalo and further enhance their chances of playing beyond the regular season.

Happy Birthday Rev and everyone will be there, with voter registration cards and five dollars. By the way, Ararat Temple is just north of the Peachtree Buffet. It all starts at 8. Chiefs by 10.

Ike “The Big Hater” Gardner: Ike was once the chairman of the Desegregation Task Force, some 40 or so years ago. And as an educator and a parent, he knew a lot about the “ins and outs” of the School District. And when he is talking about that part of his life, he is kind, considerate and engaging. However, he has grown to become a “hater of everything”.

Speak of the Chiefs and he will immediately inform you about the coaching “mental midgets”, referring to Haley and Weis. And this week, he has returned to his old, School District self. He actually is predicting that the Chiefs, though still spotty, will beat Buffalo. POW! Did you get that, George Benson? Chiefs by 3

Lloyd “The Biker” Greenfield: Lloyd owns a motorcycle. Not just any big bike. It’s a Harley Davidson Soft Tail Heritage Motorcycle. Now, that’s class. He once rode all the way to Houston, Texas on his toy, He still can be seen around town with his fellow Buffalo Soldiers, Peter Yelorda and Marques Harris. While just cruising, Lloyd has often reflected on the Chiefs and how they have adopted some winning ways, slowly jelling into a playoff team. There ain’t do doubt that the Chiefs will be going to the playoffs, starting Sunday out at Arrowhead. Biking but betting on the locals. Chiefs by 6.

Ollie “Meet The Press” Hubbard: Whenever you talk to Ollie, you feel like you are talking to the press. You have to be real careful what you tell him because he is GONNA report it. But, all of us can attest to his helpful hospitality. He will help you out, no matter what. There ain’t no wearing a mask with Ollie.

He is who he is and we all accept and admire him for that. In fact, Ollie has been the same, ever since he was a little boy, running in and out of his father’s barber shop, down on 18th Street. That’s probably where his “inquiring mind” began.

But, Ollie knows that the Chiefs are back, just like he is with his ability to get around. No doubt about it, Chiefs are contenders and he is going to tell everybody. What’s new? Chiefs by 10.

Jim “Granddad” Nunnelly: Came to town the same year the Chiefs qualified for the Super Bowl in 1969.

And after retiring from Jackson County, has returned to the health care industry, helping especially with Health Care Reform. But, he remembers the glory days of the Chiefs. Those like Otis Taylor and Buck Buchanan and Emmitt Thomas roamed our streets, patronized our businesses and attended our churches.

He yearns for the return to the good old days when the Rodgers Health center and Swope Health Services were born. There was no masquerading then for everyone had jobs and businesses were booming, up and down Troost and around 31st street. That is when he met and became friends with all these Monday Niters.

He believes that the Chiefs are not “lost in a masquerade” and are rising again and will definitely make the playoffs. Chiefs by 22.

Ed “Mr. I-Phone” West: While the rest of us struggle with modern technology, Ed West welcomes it. If a question about anything comes up, Ed just whispers into his i-phone and reads the answer. Now, he does wish the i-phone had big print because he has to wear them triple-bi-focal glasses. Nonetheless, he is a very valuable resource to our community.

Besides technology, Ed is actively engaged in helping teenagers adjust to the Raytown School District. And while he is by far the most factual Monday Niter, he is by no means swayed by the invading “haters”.

He thinks the Chiefs are growing by “leaps and bounds” and will definitely win the AFC West. And he is not wearing a mask, at all. Chiefs by 20.

Spt- Monday Niters F Words

The “F” Words

Carl Clark hosted the Monday Niters, this past week. And amid the usual curses and smart comments, I asked the fellas to use “f” words, when giving their predictions and comments. It just seemed like something to do. So what follows is a host of “f” words, sprinkled throughout the narrative. See how many you can spot.

First off, the Chiefs are five and three, losing all three games on the road. But another fact is that they have won all three games and home plus one away game at Cleveland. Most commentators, including our own, say the Chiefs are fading. Well, that’s a bunch of flak. Because this is the same team that everyone was raving fire about, just three weeks ago. However discontented you may be, folk still believe the Chiefs are playoff-bound.

Losing to Oakland last week put them only a half game up on the Raiders. And many say that San Diego is just beginning its usual, late-season rise. But, don’t be moved by any of that crap. Chiefs still have fire-power and it will re-emerge against Denver on Sunday. If for no more than to save face. Let’s now see what the guys say about the dreaded Denver game, in Denver.

Dr. Charles “A Real Likeable Guy” Allen: Doc believes that the Chiefs will commit a felony against the Broncos. Quietly but forcefully, he says the Broncos don’t have a team that can match with the Chiefs. Practising pain-free dentistry for thirty two years, Doc says the Chiefs will still win the West. Chiefs by 14

Paul “That Hater” Booker: Most people like Booker. He’s pleasant and he’s a good road buddy. There is no joint in town that Booker has not inspected. But, he’s a Chiefs hater, laughingly saying that the Chiefs fantasy time is over. That Booker just can’t see the light, when it comes to the Chiefs. He predicted rightly last week and that fact gives rise to his fight to end this Chiefs’ mania. He has sealed the Chiefs fate. Denver by 3

Carl “Mr. President” Clark: You know Carl is now President of the Ivanhoe Club. Along with folk like Jon Gray, he is trying to make life better for this century-old club. After thinking it over for four weeks now, he thinks Carl has made a transition to a loyalist and he’s got the fever. Chiefs by 7

Congressman “Faith-filled” Cleaver: As you know, Cleaver just beat out a fakir, in his quest to retain his seat in Congress. He actually sat down and chilled a little with us, this last Monday. He ate his little fish and followed up his comments with a funky remarked, aimed at Coach Haley. Though he won’t forget that Oakland fiasco, he still believes in the Chiefs. Chiefs by 10

Ike “Idi Amin” Gardner: I told yall about Ike’s aunt in Keytesville. She is the only person Ike has ever feared. While intellectually bright, he is obstinate and stubborn. He will go to his final grave, hating the Chiefs. But, he hates everything. He will finagle the finest thing into something bad. So no use in trying to convert him into a loyalist. He doesn’t forget and forever, remembers how many times the Chiefs’ coach has made a fantastic fool of himself. One more reason to vote against the Chiefs. Denver by 7

Lloyd “A Gentlemen’s Gentleman” Greenfield: Lloyd filters out the facts, when thinking about his prediction. Never quickly, he fills his mind with facts and filters out the crap. He knows that 5-3 is respectable for the Chiefs and we should never forget that. He believes the Chiefs are a work in progress and he advises all of us to file that away. He says it will be fun, not fouls on Sunday. Chiefs by 6

Ollie “Elephant Memory” Hubbard: Everybody is amazed how Ollie remembers everything. He focuses in on everything so that he can repeat it later, sometimes at the worst times. He flags his memory with everything for he is an ace reporter. It is fair to say that Ollie is aware of the Denver-Chiefs feud, going back to the good old days. But without Shanahan, the Broncos are in their final stages, at 2-6. That’s fair enough for Ollie. Chiefs by 10

Jim “Grand Dad” Nunnelly: He faces everything squarely and will not forfeit his right to speak. Like Ollie, that Cleaver and Ike accuse him of fonduing the truth. That makes him furious and lose control. One of the most loyal people around, he thinks it is futile to entertain any more flak from haters. Anyway you look at it, the Chiefs are gonna make the playoffs. So, stop the fussing and cussing....a sure thing for Sunday. Chiefs by 10

Ed “Clearheaded And Competent” West: Ed is a little shell-shot. In fact, he fears the lack of fundamentals that pervade the Chiefs, at times. He blames the recent loss to Oakland because McCluster wasn’t there. But fast and furious, Dexter will return and fly up and down the field on Sunday...bring Ed to fulfill his desire to see the Chiefs elevate. Chiefs by 7

Eric “Amanda And Erica’s Dad” Wesson: Frankly, I thought that the officiating in the Raiders win was a little fickled, especially the fumble call that led to the Chiefs’ final score. Bowe finally caught a pass and finished with a touchdown. But, I am glad that the Raiders won as I predicted with a field goal in the final drive and final second of the game in overtime. I think that he has caught at least one pass for a touchdown in the last four games. As far as the Broncos I think the Chiefs will finally get out of their funk and squeak by the Broncos by a final score of 13 to 14. Chiefs by 1.

Final note: The Chiefs are learning to play the game. Winning means taking the bumps and bruises, as well as the roses and cheers. And just think, we wrote this long article and never mentioned the most famous “F” word. No need to use filth. After all, it is all about the Chiefs. And we must stop talking about their funeral. Ain’t gonna happen.
