Monday Niters To Be Featured Before Chiefs’ Game with pic

By The Sports Guy

Some of the members of the infamous Monday Niters, who have been watching Monday Night football together for 34- years, will be featured on the Tailgate show Monday night on Channel 9 before the Chiefs play or get beat by the Tennessee Titans.

The group has a column that has been featured in THE CALL with their football predictions of Chiefs’ games since 1980 but began with their New Year’s column in 1970.

They rotate around to each members home for dinner on Monday nights. The Sports Guy has had the opportunity to attend a couple of Monday night games at the home of Jim Nunnelly and recently Ed West. They are all really a great group of guys.

There is a great deal of wisdom and history in the room and they will even be helping The Sports Guy pass out Christmas baskets to needy families next week, but have to tell you. If you really want to watch a football game and actually hear what is said, don’t go near them on Monday night, you won’t hear a thing.

Anything that you can think of is a topic of discussion.

Ike Gardner boasts that he has won the most games overall in the group’s history of course he is immediately challenged by Lloyd Greenfield, Carl Clark and Paul Booker even wakes up to challenge Gardner’s claim that he has won the most games.

“I want to tell you all something. I am not opposed to the Chiefs as much as some of you all are. You paint the picture that I am always against the Chiefs. I am only against them when they are playing a team that they cannot beat. I have never said that they Chiefs are going to lose all of the time,” said Gardner.

“See that ole’ fat mouth Nunnelly started that,” Gardner yelled.

Of course Nunnelly denies doing any such thing.

Ed West shares the secret of the groups long term success.

“Our wives deserve most of the credit. They cook for all of us and make their homes presentable for guests. They are very gracious hosts for a bunch of ragga’ muffins,” said West.

“It is a lot cheaper now to host a game than it was when I first joined the group 25-years ago. Nobody drinks anymore so we don’t have the cost of buying alcohol,” West said.

There are countless photos of the group through the years and the events that they have attended both in Kansas City and St. Louis when there was a group of Monday Niters there. And there were too many New year’s parties to count.

There is even a Governor’s Cup trophy that the group competed against St. Louis with.

SPT- MONDAY NITERS

Marvin Gaye, Matt Cassel,

And Those Chiefs

Y’all know Marvin Gaye, He was the greatest Motown singer ever. Singing hits like “Heard It Through The Grapevine” and “Let’s Get It On” and “Makes Me Wanna Holler”.

But one of his most unknown songs was entitled, “Ain’t That Peculiar”. It is with this song in mind that we will talk about the Kansas City Chiefs, this week. Many of you know that the Chiefs will be facing the Buffalo Bills, in Buffalo, New York this Sunday. And let’s not forget that Matt Cassel will still be the starting quarterback for the Chiefs. . . again.

This past Monday night, we all met out at the Groove Station, in South Kansas City. It is all part of our “get out and see the City” campaign. We, Monday Niters, have been going to each other’s homes for over forty years and from now on, we will find out what the restaurant cuisine is really like. But, the point is we talked about the Chiefs’ highly-inadequate quarterback, Matt Cassel.

Ain”t it peculiar that Matt Cassel never started a college football game. That’s because he played backup to Matt Leinart and Carson Palmer at USC. Further, ain’t it peculiar that he was not a starter in the NFL, either. He gained fame by backing up New England’s Tom Brady, during an unexpected injury. So it’s begs the question....why in the hell should the Chiefs’ expect any more than a backup game, when Matt Cassel is still starting? Now, that is peculiar. But let’s move on, knowing that the Chiefs will not win unless they replace Matt Cassel. Enough said about that.

Back to the Groove Station venue. Ollie hosted us out there because he lives only a few blocks away. That’s Ollie....location, location. And the cuisine was some delicious chicken wings, backed up with a nice sports salad. And the owner shared her story with all the guys. But, we were only interested in talking about the Chiefs’ chances against lowly Buffalo. Except for a couple of us die-hards, everyone picked Buffalo over the Chiefs, especially after that high-school showing against Tony Gonzalez and the Atlanta Falcons. All the guys think that the Chiefs still have a long way to go, before winning. Thus, it is not “peculiar” that they would pick Buffalo over the “Casseled-out” Chiefs. They think that Buffalo’s defense, led by $100 million dollar man, Mario Williams, will maul the Chiefs. Even though Buffalo’s quarterback, Ryan Fitzpatrick, ain’t no better than Cassel. Now, ain’t that peculiar?

Spt - Monday Nite Football

Monday Nite Football

Chiefs And Christmas

Ring-a-ling, ring-a-ling. . .it’s football time again in the city.

And our Kansas City Chiefs, full of vicissitudes, are back; and this time, it is crystal clear that the locals will still be struggling, come Christmas time. In fact, some of us believe the Chiefs will be completely eliminated around Thanksgiving. Let’s look a little closer.

First, our Chiefs will have to play both Super Bowl teams. . . Chicago and Indianapolis. That’s two down.

Then, we have to play San Diego twice. Remember, this team had a 14-2 record, the best in the NFL. That’s two more losses.

And we usually split with topsy-turvy Denver. That makes

five losses, right off the bat.

Not to speak about Cincinnati,

Tennessee, and Minnesota. Don’t forget about Oakland, who will be tougher just because they finally got a quarterback. Essentially, the Chiefs can do no better than 8-8, this year.

Do I sound like Ike, Booker, Carl and Ollie?

No, these are just the funky facts. The Chiefs are truly in trouble, face it. Now, this is not to say the Chiefs are entirely worthless. For they have acquired some key gifts. . . Edwards and Bowe. And there are some “lights” still glowing from last year. There is the perennial, Eddie Kenniston and league’s best running back, Larry Johnson.

On defense, there’s Tamba Hali and Derrick Johnson and even Jared Allen is all “lit up”. While none of these will set atop the Christmas tree, they were nonetheless promising, last year. Unfortunately, these are all ornaments; but there is no tree to put them on. So what am I saying?

Santa or somebody needs to send us a quarterback and real quick. Herm is truly up-a-creek with both Brodie and Huard. Neither is of NFL quality and cannot help us win another playoff spot. No way, these guys are going to make it, especially with the schedule I just mentioned.

Now, Dr. Allen, Lloyd, and Cleaver are pretty loyal Chiefs fans. And certainly they are to be congratulated for “hanging in there”. . . both in good times and bad. But, for me, I’m headed straight to the Salvation Army and start the bell-ringing, next week. And I am encouraging each of you to attend church and begin your prayers early.

For it sure looks like a “lonesome Christmas”, coming up for the Chiefs.

In a bad way, the Chiefs will be home for Christmas and in January, too. Ring-a-ling. Ring-a-ling.

The “Apostle” Paul Booker is the most liked guy among the Monday

Niters. He is low-key, dedicated and always avoids useless

confrontations. Quietly going about his life. In fact, after leaving

the Air Force, many years ago, he quietly enrolled at Lincoln

University. Not having any financial help, Booker had to clean up the

state office buildings, at night, just to be able to go to school in the

day. Nobody knew his financial status. As he began to make friends,

there at Lincoln, he never imagined that those friends would become

lifelong…..like Stewart Watson , Ollie Hubbard, Walt Livingston, and

Charles Allen. And, quietly, he carried those friendships forward, as

he became an educator in the Kansas City School District and as an

evening staff worker at the Niles Home for Children. If Booker has an

outspoken interest, it would be the plight of troubled youth. As

home-school coordinator under Ike Gardner, it was not uncommon for him

to tirelessly search for services for the young people, assigned to him.

In fact, a young man approached Booker one night, at a social gathering

and thanked Booker for keeping him out of prison. Now, that is

Booker…at his best, helping youngsters who could go either way.

Moreover, Booker’s interest in football springs from his untiring

interest in troubled youth. He knows that many of the Chiefs have come

from broken homes and with someone helping them, they have achieved in

athletics. Booker is always quietly, trying to help some youngster

avoid being in trouble. Even though Booker is quiet, he is well-known

around town. In fact, a recent Mayor appointed Booker and another

Monday Niter, to inspect metropolitan area night clubs, on Friday

evenings. On a serious note, Booker is a well-liked, respected, quiet

type of guy, everywhere he goes.

The Denver Broncos’ 24-20 upset victory over the Kansas City Chiefs this past Sunday took its toll on the Monday Niters with nine out of ten expecting the local guys to win. Only Ike Gardner was out on a limb with the right pick.

Earlier in the season, Lloyd Greenfield did not submit predictions a couple of times, but he's still in first place with a 5-2 mark. Monday Nite Footballers Commentary: NAIA: Will The Real Johnny Miller Speak Up?

By Jim Nunnelly

That year makes Johnny Miller speak up. And, when he talks about that year, he smiles. He gives you a I-will-correct-you look, if he thinks you do not have the facts about 1972. Usually, Johnny Miller is a quiet, unassuming man. But, when it's time, he will speak up. Before we explain about 1972, let's look in on the "usual" Johnny Miller. His retirement. His religious and helpful ways. And his memories about past NAIA basketball tournaments. You see, Johnny and his wife, Ovara, have witnessed twenty five consecutive NAIA tournaments. They were there Monday night for the finals in their usual front-row seats at Kemper. Quiet, faithful, loyal.

Retired Last May That's Johnny Miller for you. Quiet and steady. Why, just last May he retired from the General Motors Assembly Plant at Leeds. There he drove a tractor supplying the assembly workers, to keep the line moving. They say he hardly

ATTEND NAIA TOURNAMENT FOR 25 YEARS . . . Pee Wee Summers, Big Eight Official, and Krea State Farm Agent, stops by to chat with Johnny and Ovara Miller. The Millers have been attending the NAIA tournament for 25 years and their memories are featured in this week's Monday Night Footballer's column.

missed a day in his 28 years there.

Raised in East Texas And, as his smile widens a little, he talks a little about his garden. Mustard and turnip greens are his favorite. All that stems from his upbringing back in Henderson, Texas. A

pretty good athlete himself, Johnny played first base in baseball and center in basketball. His quiet-like manner and his six-foot one frame helped him along. But, life back there was different from the one here in Kansas. But, Johnny still has a deep, religious desire to help people just like back in East Texas. And he keeps that belief alive, helping the elderly by painting and repairing

items. And, he and his wife have been loyal members of the West Paseo Christian and Swope Parkway United Christian church for 32 years quietly, faithfully, always there.

Saw A Lot of Tournament Changes And, just like the rest of his life, Johnny Miller mostly listens. But, as he talked about the many

Continued on page 22

Monday Night Footballers

Continued from page 13

changes over the 52 years of the tournament, he fo- cuses. His eyes tell you he knows. And he know he knows, too.

He knows he liked ol’ Municipal Auditorium bet- ter than Kemper. It was a better and closer atmo- sphere for basketball. And, he speaks more confident- ly, as he remembers when the teams were either all black or all white. Either way, for 25 years, Johnny has come to these all-day and all-night games—with the same quiet way.

But, Monday night, he’s up for talking. He quickens the pace when he talks about the NAIA alumni who have gone on to the National Basketball Association. Wil- lis Reed, Earl "The Pearl" Monroe. Yes, he knows these were the best players up till 1972.

An Armchair Historian

And, now Johnny is talk- ing more confidently than ever, as he tosses that quiet- like manner aside. Now, he’s talking about Travis Grant of Kentucky State. That night, in 1972, against Minot State, Grant scored a record high sixty points, hitting twenty seven field goals.

And Johnny Miller was there. He tells you that, with an I’m- for-sure smile. He knows that Grants’ per- formance that night could well have been the most outstanding individual per- formance ever in the his- tory of the March tour- nament. And, it all happened down at the ol’ Municipal auditorium. And without a three-point line.

Finally, Spoke Up

So, Johnny Miller talked up Monday night. Except this time, someone listened to him and asked him what happened. He was there. He saw. He remembered.

And tonight he spoke up. Like so many other older black folks, Johnny is an armchair sports historian, who quietly goes about his way. Never being asked. Never being heard.

But, Monday night at the 1989 championship game, the real Johnny Miller spoke up.

Ike . . . He's Back!

The Denver Broncos’ 24-20 upset victory over the Kansas City Chiefs this past Sunday took its toll on the Monday Niters with nine out of ten expecting the local guys to win. Only Ike Gardner was out on a limb with the right pick.

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary By Jim Nunnelly

39th and Agnes: 'The Clyde Kemp Trail'

"Do not follow where the path may lead. Go instead where there is no path and leave a trail."

That quote seems to characterize Clyde Kemp. What's so special about Clyde Kemp? Is it that he worked nights at the Post Office? No. Or is it he's a regional loan specialist for HUD? No. Or is it that he's part of a family reunion that gives a scholarship to a fellow family member? No. Well, what's the big deal about this trail he leaves?

Simple.

Clyde just happens to be the winningest coach ever, in junior high Catholic League circles. And, though not recognized for his endless efforts, he's done this for twenty years—with little fanfare, just an uncontrollable obsession to serve. And he loves this coaching position and he shows you how much by the many stories he tells; like the one about an eighth grader who wanted to get married. Or about the fact that he's lost less than 50 games in his whole career. Or about the fact that he coached one "ghetto Catholic" now playing baseball with the Montreal Expos. On and on, like a proud history teacher who's just been honored. And teacher he is, let's look in.

For Clyde, entering the basketball coaching field was a complete accident. Not knowing a thing about basketball, he consented to sit with his son's team. This was way back in 1967, at little Blessed Sacrament School (now St. Martins) at 39th and Agnes. He was just to sit on the bench, not coach. Just act like a chaperone for a bunch of Catholic school kids who needed an adult to play. He started coaching that night and the rest is Clyde's history.

Over four hundred wins later, Clyde has convinced his whole family to get involved—his vivacious wife, Frankie, and his two sons. They all help and they're involved in the whole black Catholic movement, reinvesting themselves year after year.

But, Clyde's a Baptist convert. You'd never know it because his respect in the Catholic community is so profound and far reach-

Continued on page 21

Footballers Continued from page 14 ing.

His confident influence ranges from spearheading the rules of the Catholic Sports Program to guiding the merger of inner city Catholic schools. He’s a rock and everyone around him knows it and they all expect him to be “solid and sound” in his judgments.

Especially, his players and the other 34 teams in the eighth grade Catholic League admire his coaching prowess. He just keeps winning championships year after year. Of course, he only tells you it’s the “team” concept or it’s the “fast break” that makes these junior high kids excel to impossible heights. But it’s really Clyde’s “dedication and drive” to make every young man a good student first and an athlete second. That’s it, that’s it, Clyde’s key to his success. He pushes the developing youngster “to the max” and therein lies the “secret map” of the trail.

And he gets those results, too. Ask George Word. Or Cebron Robinson. Or Ricardo Broils. Or Anthony Augmon. Or Al Newman of the Montreal Expos. They all studied the basketball teachings of Clyde the Great. And from there went on to play college ball.

Speaking of study? Where did Clyde learn about basketball? As an intrusion into his privacy, he wasn’t a basketball player in high school. And his speculative football playing days at Sumner in Kansas City, Kas., were cut short when his coach found his mother’s signature forged on a consent form. Further when reflecting back to his childhood days in Mississippi, he really dreamed of being a baseball player.

So, how did it start? He learned from training films bought at Seven-Up sponsored clinics. Yes, that was years and many lessons ago. Today, he still coaches the same eighth grade. And his Vince Lombardi-like style makes him trusted, feared and respected.

Things have changed a little today, since way back in the 60’s. Now his teams sport classy red and white uniforms. And he’s been appointed to the Diocesan School Board. His wife, Frankie, is the cheerleader coach. And his two sons, even the one responsible for him coaching the 5th grade, share his coaching duties at the St. Martin de Porres School (formerly Blessed Sacrament.)

As if divinely guided, he smiles and says about this total involvement in this volunteer sports program, “we (his family) just feel like we need to be there”. And kids, parents, school teachers, nuns, priests, bishops, coaches, all can find him every evening in the fall and winter, down at the Clyde Kemp trail.

Monday Nite Footballers Commentary

The Texas 'No Pass-No Play' Law

By Jim Nunnelly

The state of Texas has a law concerning where athletics stand in the high school. The law is called the "no pass, no play" law. Simply stated, if a student wants to participate in extracurricular activities, like football, basketball or band, he or she must receive a passing grade in every course taken.

Monday Night Footballers debated the question about the fairness of such a law. Right now, the law is awaiting Supreme Court review. Is it right to deny a child participation in sports because he or she does not pass a course? Passed in January of this year, this law has become the most controversial piece of legislation ever passed in regards to educational/athletic circles.

What are the issues?

First of all, there are those who say that the first objective of school should be learning. Those who like the law openly assert that a child should know more than the rules of an athletic contest. He or she ought to know math, English, and the sciences. They see a college star signing a million dollar contract and, at the same time, murder the English language.

Moreover, the proponents of this law agree that there is a 3,000 to one chance that a high school athlete will play professional sports. There is "life after football". We all know what happens to all those high school stars who fade into nowhere because they could not read or write or complete a job application. One Monday Niter, Isaac Gardner holds steadfast to this position.

Now, the other side does not differ that much. But, they do believe that it is unfair to stop an athlete or a gymnast from participating because that student fails one course. They believe that it is ridiculous to think everyone will do well in every subject.

Further, they say that the law discriminates against blacks and minorities. Such a rule could threaten the stability of an inner city school league, anywhere in the United States. Maybe, even here in Kansas City. For we all know that many high school athletes are "marginal, at best" when it comes to the classroom.

In short, the opponents shoot for a tougher requirement on the total grade point average. That approach would allow a student to fail in one class, but would require him or her to do well in the remaining classes.

Which is right, no pass, no play or a tougher grade point requirement? Seven Monday Niters favor the tougher grade point. Though, no such rule exists now, we do believe the time has come when the student athlete will be required to perform much better in the classroom before every being allowed to participate in athletics.

Continued on page 16

Monday Nite Football

Continued from page 14

The day has come and gone when the athlete can "get by" on his or her athletic prowess alone. We firmly believe that educators, families, the community and the student athlete, all will be better off when we require the student to perform better in the classroom. Though it is punitive in some ways, it does remind us that there are people who are not willing to see society as a whole crumble because our athletes cannot compete in the open job market.

Monday Niters, welcome the refreshing trend, doing well on the field and in the classroom.

It is something to think about!

Monday Nite Footballers

Commentary

By Jim Nunnelly

Jerry Price: Propelled by Personal Memory Library

Editor's Note: The Mon- day Niters support wholly. the decision to name Jerry Price as Interscholastic League Football Coach of the Year. Price has been a guiding force to the de- velopment of Monday Nite football and has always added the "expert opinion" when needed. We salute you, JP. May this be only one of many more memo- ries.

Someone once said, "ev- ery man's memory is his private literature." And the recently selected In- terscholastic League Foot- ball Coach-of-the-Year Jer- ry Price has a bank of solid memories — enough to fill a sports library.

First, let's look at Price's pre-Kansas City days. Af- terwards, we'll look at his seventeen years as a high school football coach. And last, we'll look at his pro- peller-like memory. Now, let's go back.

Price's first memories are from Denton, Texas. A hometown, high school all state quarterback, Price wrote in his library when

he went to Langston uni- versity. There, besides be- ing an all-conference tight end and quarterback, Price recorded a hole-in-one from 370 yards away. A pro-like golfer and a credible short stop. Price was a three sport letterman at the all- black Oklahoma univer- sity.

Not bad for a memorable, 165-pound rifleman from Texas. Maybe, that's where the nickname "killer" emerged.

Now Price is no stranger to Kansas Citians. Anyone familiar with high school football knows him. Price has a winning football re- cord of 73-48-3 and made

Continued on page 21

Monday Nite Continued from page 12

the state football playoffs three of the last four years. More memories. Last year, he was coach of the State Champion Girls Track Team from Lincoln Acade- my. And he is expected to take it all again in 1987. In short, Jerry Price loves his kids. Enough, enough.

There has to be more. Yes, there is more. And, though we Monday Niters salute Jerry’s recent achievement, we are mind- ful that JP has a personal library of memories. It’s not those towering trophies hanging in the halls of Lin- coln Prep. Nor is it the fact he’s the only Monday Niter actively engaged in sports. And, it sure isn’t that now his weight is al- most twice what it was at Langston.

No, it’s none of that. It’s Darnita Page Price and the kids. You see, that’s where Jerry gets that pro- peller-like energy to push his “kids” to their max- imum limits. That’s where he finds peace and under- standing after a fourteen to fifteen hour day. And, that’s where he gets the confidence to come back after being mentally de- feated. Yes, that’s the part of Jerry that we don’t know about.

But, it’s part of the whole scene. It’s in his personal library of memories. And, he doesn’t leave home with- out them.
